channel.title: Zeteo
channel.description: Mehdi Hasan's Zeteo — First Draft and news analysis

item.title: New U.S. Iran Strikes Show Trump Has Started a ‘Forever War,’ Exasperated Official Says
item.description: Donald Trump ordered the U.S. military to strike drone, missile, and radar sites in Iran on Friday, in response to an Iranian drone attack on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz the day before. The strikes came amid a supposed 60-day ceasefire to allow for talks aimed at ending the war. One…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Donald Trump ordered the U.S. military to strike drone, missile, and radar sites in Iran on Friday, in response to an Iranian drone attack on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz the day before. The strikes came amid a supposed 60-day ceasefire to allow for talks aimed at ending the war.

One exasperated senior U.S. official told Zeteo the U.S. strikes showed Trump had “started a forever war.”

Earlier on Friday, Trump told reporters at the White House they would “find out soon” if he was going to order new strikes, in response to the Iranian attack on a Singapore-flagged ship that Tehran said was using an unapproved route through the Strait.

“I don’t like the fact that they took a shot yesterday, actually four of them,” Trump said. Asked why he would order a strike when he had said ceasefire talks had been going well, Trump said of Iran: “They’re a little bit different.”

Ebraham Azizi, a senior Iranian national security official, responded to Trump’s comments by saying the U.S. should “respect the rules” of the Strait of Hormuz, over which Iran claims control, and “not mistake control for escalation.”

U.S. Central Command then said it conducted “a powerful response” to the Iranian strike on the ship that was “exiting the Strait of Hormuz along the Omani coast at the time of Iran’s attack.”

The statement continued: “The unwarranted aggression against commercial shipping by Iranian forces clearly violated the ceasefire. Furthermore, Iran’s dangerous behavior undermined freedom of navigation as commerce increasingly flows through the vital international trade corridor.”

The New York Times quoted a U.S. official as saying the strikes hit targets along the Strait and on Queshm Island.

After the U.S. strikes, Azizi said: “The U.S. attacked Iran in the middle of negotiations once again. The failed U.S. president has shown he has no commitment to the principles of negotiation or a ceasefire. This reckless violation of the ceasefire will, as always, lead to retreat and regret on their part. The blame game does not work anymore.”

In a statement carried by Iranian state media, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said the U.S. had violated the ceasefire and any further strikes would be met with a “more extensive” response.

Vice President JD Vance, who has been leading U.S. negotiators in talks held in Switzerland, said: “Iran signed a ceasefire agreement. We have honored it. If they have disagreements about how the MOU is being applied, they can pick up the phone. But violence will be met with violence.”

As the markets closed for the weekend – the time when Trump has privately told U.S. officials he prefers bombing Iran – several administration officials and others close to the Trump White House told Zeteo that this was the scenario many predicted, or feared.

Trump, they said, had started not just another disaster in the Middle East. He had quite possibly started the exact kind of conflict that he at least said he would keep the U.S. out of: a never-ending war that stretches far past the American government’s “mission accomplished” moment, that commits U.S. forces to combat well past the patience of voters and the elite, and that has reverberating unintended consequences for years if not decades to come.

Friday’s events were further evidence that’s precisely the road Trump put the U.S. on, where any idea of a meaningful exit strategy is proven immediately laughable.

A Trump administration official referred to the latest events as another instance of “peacetime bombings,” an intentionally ironic term that further underscores how Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu’s attacks on Iran have produced perhaps the most Orwellian war of our lifetimes.

For any issues with your subscription, please email us at info@zeteonews.com.

item.title: U.S. Is ‘Asleep at the Wheel’ Against Terror Threats, Former DHS Chief of Staff Says
item.description: How secure is a superpower when it makes more enemies than allies? How are threats to the United States being dealt with when they often come from the White House itself? And how much further can Trump’s corruption jeopardize the safety of the people he claims to serve? Who better to answer these…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
How secure is a superpower when it makes more enemies than allies? How are threats to the United States being dealt with when they often come from the White House itself? And how much further can Trump’s corruption jeopardize the safety of the people he claims to serve? Who better to answer these questions than former Department of Homeland Security chief of staff turned whistleblower, Miles Taylor.

“People who want to kill Americans, we are less prepared to stop them than at any point since 9-11,” says Taylor, as he answers questions from John Harwood and Zeteo’s subscribers during this latest Substack live.

Watch the full video above to hear how Trump could weaponize the U.S. Postal Service to jeopardize elections, the security threats political appointees like Kash Patel pose, and a special announcement from Taylor towards the end of the discussion.

Free subscribers can watch a 4-minute preview. Consider upgrading to a paid subscription and never hit a paywall again!

This was a live conversation on Zeteo.com and the Substack app, where subscribers could ask questions. Be sure to subscribe to catch the next Substack Live.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: 🤢 Trump the Bathroom Slob?
item.description: On this day in 2015, by a 5-4 ruling in Obergefell v. Hodges, the Supreme Court made same-sex marriage the law of the land. On the current court, reactionary Justices Clarence Thomas and Samuel Alito have said the ruling should be reconsidered. Happy Friday… finally! It’s Swin again. Since April,…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 2015, by a 5-4 ruling in Obergefell v. Hodges, the Supreme Court made same-sex marriage the law of the land. On the current court, reactionary Justices Clarence Thomas and Samuel Alito have said the ruling should be reconsidered.

Happy Friday… finally! It’s Swin again. Since April, I’ve been covering Trumpworld blowback to

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we’ll get into the strange, strange detail in Haberman and Swan’s just-published book that has the nominal leader of the free world squealing about his privacy. Plus, Trump’s pet Supreme Court justices delivered his nasty, racist regime a big win on immigration that will place thousands of Haitian and Syrian immigrants in danger of their lives.

There’s plenty about the book Regime Change that’s enraging the president of the United States. Donald Trump has been frustrated that the authors’ premier anonymous sources haven’t been punished. He’s still furious after all these months about the Iran war and White House Situation Room reporting. He got incandescently mad about the Jeffrey Epstein stuff.

Sometimes, it is hard to keep track of all the things that are slowly driving this world-historically petty president into a rage-filled psychotic abyss. In speaking to people very close to President Trump, it’s been difficult to overstate how much this book has genuinely triggered him. But there’s something in the book that hasn’t gotten nearly the level of attention of the aforementioned scoops… and yet it’s something that, according to sources familiar with the matter, has privately and absolutely infuriated the president and helped contribute to his decision to impose an intra-administration blackout on publicly talking about the book.

“It makes him look so fucking gross,” one senior Trump appointee tells me. “The president sees everything, and he knows about the trash and bathroom sections, and thinks it’s complete bullshit that it got published.”

The perennially image-conscious and self-obsessed president was complaining to aides and other longtime advisers about the book’s descriptions of his bathroom and bedroom habits.

“The portion nearest the shower would often be soaked through; the staff was never quite sure why, but they worried about mold growing underneath,” Haberman and Swan reported, referring to Trump’s installation of carpeting in his private White House bathroom. “The solution was to lay a small piece of the same carpet – never an actual bath mat – over the larger one.”

If you need to be told why a carpeted bathroom is disgusting, you are likely either a child or the most powerful person alive today.

The book also notes how, as “a nighttime snacker, the President would frequently leave an array of empty potato chip bags, Starbucks wrappers, and ice cream cartons in the trash, or on the floor,” and that

If you think this all sounds weird as hell or disgusting, you’d be right.

That’s why the 80-year-old president is so sensitive about it, petty and small as that sounds.

In recent private conversations, Trump has trashed these revelations as “libel” (they aren’t), and denounced the leaks and accounts as an invasion of his privacy, the sources say. At other points, he’s vented to some confidants that “I don’t do that!” – to which some of them have sat there and thought to themselves: Uh… yeah, you do.

President Trump is supposed to be working on ending a war. The fact that he’s obsessing over stuff like this is both objectively horrifying and also completely in-character. His anger at this and other details is why he and his White House staff ordered a blackout on the book, meaning most senior officials are barred from publicly acknowledging or even tweeting about its existence, two people with knowledge of the situation tell me.

The general silence from the Trump administration on this is, indeed, deafening. For instance, when we’ve tried to get White House spokespeople to comment on the book or our related reporting, the most we’ve gotten out of them is a pivot to just insulting Zeteo. We tried for comment again on Thursday night. Answer came there none.

Still, it’s likely only a matter of time – given how much we know about his self-control – before Trump can’t take it anymore and violates his own blackout decree by openly trashing Haberman, Swan, and their book.

Prove me wrong, Mr. president. Prove me wrong.

  • Strait strike:A UN operation to move ships through the Strait of Hormuz and evacuate 11,000 stranded crew members waspausedafter Iran struck a container ship off Oman. The ship had not coordinated its passage with Iran and was using a route Iran’s Revolutionary Guard said was “unacceptable.”
  • Marco solo:U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubiosaidthe route close to Oman was vital, adding, “If that stops, then we’re going to have a problem.” He spoke in Bahrain, seeking to reassure Arab allies nervous over negotiations between Washington and Tehran to end the four-month war.
  • Price jump:After news of the ship strike,oilprices rose. Analysts said the strike showed the urgent need for a clear U.S.-Iran agreement on the Strait.
  • Lebanon latest:Israeli attackscontinuedin Lebanon despite a supposed ceasefire and with Iran demanding an end to violence as a condition for ending the wider war. Three people were killed in a strike on a car in southern Lebanon, while Israel said it also killed six alleged members of Hezbollah in its so-called “security zone.” Lebanon’s Health Ministry said the death toll from Israeli attacks stood at 4,230.
  • Netanyahu vow:Speaking to Israeli military cadets, Benjamin NetanyahusaidIsrael would “not withdraw from the security zone in southern Lebanon… as long as necessary to ensure Israel’s security.”

After Mullin v. Doe, the ruling by which the Supreme Court’s far-right majority ended Temporary Protected Status for refugees from Haiti and Syria, the stark horror of what comes next was put simply by the New York Times:

“The court’s6-3 decision… clears a path for the potential deportation of 350,000 Haitians and 6,100 Syrians, and it is likely to have implications for TPS holders from about a dozen other countries.”

TPS was passed by Congress in 1990 to allow vetted immigrants from eligible countries to work in the U.S., if deportation would put them in danger. Naturally, Donald Trump, Stephen Miller, and the rest of their putrid crew (hypocrite descendants of immigrants and refugees, the lot) loathe TPS, given that most recipients have dark skin.

In Mullin, noxious Justice Samuel Alito loftily dismissed the idea that Trump might want to punish Syrians, Haitians, and any other nationality targeted for TPS removal because of racism. In a blistering dissent, Justice Elena Kagan listed “statements by the President so repellent and racially inflected that the majority declines to put them in print.” Among them:

“Haitians are ‘eating the dogs . . . . They’re eating the cats. They’re eating… the pets of the people that live [in Springfield, Ohio].’… And: Haitians in the United States ‘probably have AIDS.’ And: Haiti is a ‘shithole country,’ which is ‘filthy, dirty, [and] disgusting.’ And: Haitian immigration is ‘like a death wish for our country.’ And: Haitians, along with some others, are ‘poisoning the blood’ of our country.”

On Thursday, the Republican governor of Ohio, Mike DeWine, spelled out the unfolding horror in his state:

“As a result of today’s ruling, the over 10,000 Haitians who have been living in Ohio… legally through TPS will now be here illegally and will be subject to immediate deportation. This also means that while these Haitians were working and contributing to our community and economy yesterday, today it is now illegal to employ them… changing the immigration status of these individuals is not in the best interest of the United States nor Ohio.”

Nor is Ohio any kind of alone. Earlier this year, nursing home operators pointed out how much their industry relies on immigrants willing to take low-paid, often traumatic jobs. More than 100,000 Haitians are employed in the healthcare sector.

There are other TPS countries, nationals of which can expect to be targeted too: El Salvador, Ethiopia, Lebanon, Myanmar, Somalia, South Sudan, Sudan, Ukraine, Venezuela, and Yemen.

Ukrainians are white. So are the Afrikaans “refugees” the administration lets in on trumped-up claims of “racist” persecution. The rest? They’re not so lucky. Even for a conservative Court majority that knows no shame in how low it will go for its master, this was an historically despicable and ridiculous ruling.

As for the fundamentally and undeniably racist angle to all of this, let former Fox host turned podcaster Megyn Kelly make my point for me:


Truly sick stuff.

  • Ruling class:Supreme Court right-wingers said in a6-3decision that the Trump administration could revive a policy turning back asylum seekers at the southern border. They also struck down a Hawaii gun control law,6-3, and ruled7-2in favor of the makers of Roundup weedkiller in limiting cancer-related lawsuits.
  • Epstein win:In a suit brought by journalist Katie Phang, a DC judgeorderedthe DOJ to unredact Epstein files, including “at least eight email exchanges with [Jeffrey] Epstein regarding a ‘torture video’ and sexual activity with young women, including minors,” and interviews with a woman who claimed to have been abused as a minor by Donald Trump.
  • Michigan moves:Maryland’s Chris Van Hollenbecamethe first senator since Bernie Sanders to endorse Abdul El-Sayed, the progressive candidate for U.S. Senate in Michigan. Pro-Israel group AIPAC was shown to havespentanother $2.4 million supporting rival candidate Haley Stevens, for $7.3 million overall.
  • ICE chills:Two agents tracked down a New York state poll worker and demanded she take down a post naming Jonathan Ross, the agent who killed Renee Good in Minneapolis in January. “For ICE to come to me over a social media post just feels very1984to me,” Paigelynn Gonyeasaid, refusing to comply.
  • Not OK, RFK:At thebehestof the White House, Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. pressed Iowa Libertarians to drop out of U.S House races, theWashington Postreported.TheGuardiansaidnew evidence linked a Kennedy trip to Samoa in 2019 to anti-vaccine advocacy, shortly before a deadly measles outbreak. In Senate testimony, Kennedy has denied any such purpose.
  • Mamdani delivers:A New York City panelvotedto freeze rents for nearly 1 million rent-stabilized apartments, fulfilling a key promise by Mayor Zohran Mamdani. In a statement, Mamdani saluted “a historic victory for New York City tenants” and said he would “continue working to deliver a more affordable city.”
  • More money, one problem:A billionaire wealth taxwill appearon ballots in California, despite opposition from Democratic Governor Gavin Newsom and billionaires. The one-time, 5% tax on California billionaires would fund healthcare, following cuts by Trump.

How many people died in the 2019-20 Samoa measles outbreak, shortly after the island nation was visited by noted anti-vaccine campaigner Robert F. Kennedy, Jr.?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:One Palestinian waskilledin northern Gaza when Israeli troops opened fire yesterday, and two people were wounded by a drone strike in Gaza City, officials said. Two others werekilledtoday. Israel has killed at least 1,031 people in Gaza since its supposed ceasefire last October.
  • 🇻🇪 Earthquake aftermath:Venezuelanssearchedfor survivors after two strong quakes killed at least 230 people and trapped hundreds of others. The coastal region of La Guaira suffered some of the worst damage, with the country’s main airport forced to close, complicating aid efforts.
  • 🇰🇪 Protest arrests:Police fired tear gas anddetainedhundreds amid protests marking two years since at least 60 people were killed outside Kenya’s parliament in Nairobi, in demonstrations triggered by tax increases.

  • 🇨🇳🇹🇼

    Patrol alert:Chinadefendedcoast guard activity east of Taiwan, after Britain, France, and Germany expressed concern. The waters are a key shipping lane for oil, gas, and goods being transported to Europe. China is thought likely to attack Taiwan soon.


JD Vance went to Yorba Linda, California, to flog his new book at the Richard Nixon Presidential Library. Claiming the “deep state” was responsible for the 37th president’s downfall, not Tricky Dick himself, Vance also managed to say, in effect, ‘You think Nixon was corrupt? He’s got nothing on me and Donny T.’ Nor did Nixon have a Supreme Court willing to place a president above the law. And so, in sum… ugh.

Support Builds on the Right for Prosecuting Women Who Get Abortions

For the New York Times, in a piece published on the fourth anniversary of the fall of Roe v. Wade, Caroline Kitchener reports from Austin, Texas, on a disturbing trend among anti-abortion activists. Frustrated by rising abortion rates thanks to telehealth and the availability of abortion pills, “a growing number of conservative leaders are starting to argue that the only way to stop women from ending their pregnancies could be to arrest them.”

The new power broker: How Zohran Mamdani muscled NYC’s Democratic establishment

The headline of Edward-Isaac Dovere’s piece on this week’s New York Democratic primaries is, or at least appears to be, more positive about Mayor Zohran Mamdani than the text underneath. Within that, there is much bleating about the mayor and his three endorsees set for U.S. House seats. “According to one [city council] member who spoke to CNN on the condition of anonymity,” Dovere writes, “Mamdani is only interested in allyship on his terms.” Which would be the terms of… a winner?

You may have noticed there’s a World Cup going on. Such is soccer’s reach, even the London Review of Books is in on the act. Fortunately, historian Simon Skinner’s review of The Power and the Glory: A New History of the World Cup by Jonathan Wilson is a terrific read on the culture, heritage, and various political meanings of the tournament, very much warts and all. The headline quotes Donald Trump on this year’s fan-fleecing ticket prices, and for once, you have to say he might’ve been right.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

He’s got nothing on me and Donny T.’ Nor did Nixon have a Supreme Court willing to place a president above the law. And so, in sum… ugh.

Support Builds on the Right for Prosecuting Women Who Get Abortions

For the New York Times, in a piece published on the fourth anniversary of the fall of Roe v. Wade, Caroline Kitchener reports from Austin, Texas, on a disturbing trend among anti-abortion activists. Frustrated by rising abortion rates thanks to telehealth and the availability of abortion pills, “a growing number of conservative leaders are starting to argue that the only way to stop women from ending their pregnancies could be to arrest them.”

The new power broker: How Zohran Mamdani muscled NYC’s Democratic establishment

The headline of Edward-Isaac Dovere’s piece on this week’s New York Democratic primaries is, or at least appears to be, more positive about Mayor Zohran Mamdani than the text underneath. Within that, there is much bleating about the mayor and his three endorsees set for U.S. House seats. “According to one [city council] member who spoke to CNN on the condition of anonymity,” Dovere writes, “Mamdani is only interested in allyship on his terms.” Which would be the terms of… a winner?

You may have noticed there’s a World Cup going on. Such is soccer’s reach, even the London Review of Books is in on the act. Fortunately, historian Simon Skinner’s review of The Power and the Glory: A New History of the World Cup by Jonathan Wilson is a terrific read on the culture, heritage, and various political meanings of the tournament, very much warts and all. The headline quotes Donald Trump on this year’s fan-fleecing ticket prices, and for once, you have to say he might’ve been right.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: The Extreme Sentences Handed to Anti-ICE Protesters Should Terrify Us All
item.description: “This is mass punishment. Collective punishment. This is guilt by association. This is injustice.” So remarked former U.S. Marine reservist Benjamin Song after being handed down a 100-year sentence on Tuesday in connection with criminal convictions for a demonstration outside the Prairieland ICE…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
“This is mass punishment. Collective punishment. This is guilt by association. This is injustice.”

So remarked former U.S. Marine reservist Benjamin Song after being handed down a 100-year sentence on Tuesday in connection with criminal convictions for a demonstration outside the Prairieland ICE prison in Alvarado, Texas, on July 4 of last year.

Dallas artist Daniel Sanchez Estrada wasn’t even at the protest, but he got 30 years after being caught on camera carrying a box of so-called “Antifa materials” (i.e., pamphlets) to a car, allegedly to protect his wife, Marciela Rueda, who attended (Estrada’s lawyer said he was moving boxes of his own personal material). Rueda was sentenced to 70 years behind bars.

Each of the other defendants got 50 years. They were convicted on a number of charges, including providing material support to terrorists.

This is part of an increasingly disturbing pattern of the Trump administration criminalizing speech and assembly in the United States. And it should alarm us all.

Last week, the Justice Department indicted 15 Minneapolis protesters for things like blocking ICE vehicles and following officers around as they violently abducted people from the streets, fatally shooting two innocent U.S. citizens. The Trump administration has done nothing to hold the killers accountable for those deaths.

DOJ claims the indicted individuals were part of “Direct Access Minnesota” or “DAMN,” with “Antifa ties.” Much like the illusory “Antifa” itself, which, according to former FBI Director Christopher Wray, is “not a group or organization” but instead “a movement or an ideology” (e.g., “I am anti-fascist”), there is virtually no public information on DAMN other than what the DOJ has spouted. Aside from a sparsely populated Instagram page that appears to belong to DAMN MN, there are no live or archived DAMN organizational pages. No Minnesota nonprofit filings under that name. No public mission statement attributable directly to DAMN. No public officers, board members, or founders. No prominent coverage of DAMN as an actual organization – other than the indictment.

And late last month, a federal jury in Washington state convicted an Army veteran and two other Spokane protesters of a federal conspiracy for sitting with linked arms around a bus that was transferring two immigrants to a Takoma detention center. They face up to six years in prison. Acting U.S. Attorney Richard Barker resigned over the charges, stating: “This was the first conspiracy prosecution in Eastern Washington history under 18 U.S.C. Section 372 – a Civil War-era law dusted off to punish members of the Spokane community who stood up for two young men who were unlawfully detained by ICE.”

Section 372, which criminalizes conspiracies to impede federal officers, was also used to charge Jan. 6 insurrectionists for conspiring to prevent members of Congress from completing the electoral vote count establishing President Joe Biden’s win. But the average sentence for the over 1,500 individuals charged was only two years. Over 600 of them were charged with assaulting, resisting, or impeding law enforcement, including through the use of deadly or dangerous weapons such as firearms, chemical sprays, tasers, knives, furniture, and flagpoles. The longest sentence was for Enrique Tarrio, who received 22 years for seditious conspiracy. The sentencing judge remarked that as the head of the Proud Boys, Tarrio “was the ultimate leader, the ultimate person who organized” events of the day. Donald Trump granted clemency to them all just hours after retaking office, with most, including Tarrio, receiving full pardons.

This DOJ’s vicious use of criminal laws to go after protesters has deeply troubling First Amendment implications. A person or group’s political ideology, to use Wray’s phrasing, is core protected speech. Acknowledging the extreme length of the Prairieland sentences, U.S. District Judge Reed O’Connor (one of two judges who imposed them) nonetheless emphasized “the need to deter this type of conduct” and described the July 4 protest as “an assault on democracy.” (O’Connor was appointed by George W. Bush, while the other judge, Mark T. Pittman, was appointed by Trump.)

Although the nation has a sordid history of prosecutions for disfavored speech and assembly, which includes the criminalization of unauthorized meetings of formerly enslaved and free Black people, “[w]hat we generally don’t see is judges talking about targeting a particular political persuasion in order to drive home that deterrence message,” said Texas A&M professor Jenny Carroll.

Two things make these cases substantively different.

First, they trace back to Trump’s barbaric NSPM-7, or National Security Presidential Memorandum-7, in which he explicitly prioritized the investigation and prosecution of individuals and organizations that his administration defines as domestic terrorists. The September 2025 memorandum particularly targets groups it labels “anti-fascist,” but also references “anti-Christianity” and “hostility toward those who hold traditional American views on family, religion, and morality” as concerns in the context of violent extremist activity.

The second is what drove the massive Prairieland sentencing numbers: the federal sentencing guidelines’ terrorism enhancement, which skyrockets the sentencing range for any felony that “involved, or was intended to promote, a federal crime of terrorism.” The terrorism enhancement automatically increases the offense level by 12 levels and sets the floor at 32. It also puts a defendant’s criminal history category to VI – the highest possible range – regardless of their actual prior criminal record. That combination is how Song’s sentence reached 100 years. A level-32 sentence with no priors normally yields 121-151 months, but with the enhancement, it jumps to a 360-months-to-life range – an increase of 20+ years on the low end alone. To that, Judge O’Connor added even more time on consecutive counts.

The terrorism enhancements were created in the early 1990s to cover international terrorism, but were expanded to include domestic terrorism in 1996 after the Oklahoma City bombing. Congress has never created a standalone “domestic terrorism” crime, however, so the enhancement attaches to ordinary statutes (arson, conspiracy, material support, destruction of property) once a judge finds terroristic intent by a preponderance of the evidence – a far lower threshold than beyond a reasonable doubt, which is required for a jury to convict on the underlying crimes. From 2014 to 2019, DOJ invoked the sentencing enhancement in only 15.4% of domestic terrorism cases, reserving it for defendants with ties to groups like ISIS or al-Qaeda. Terrorism enhancements have also historically required centralized vetting through the DOJ’s National Security Division, a process that “constrained the use of a powerful statute.” It was thus used sparingly under Attorney General Merrick Garland in Jan. 6 cases. Oath Keepers founder Stewart Rhodes was an exception, but he was still only serving 18 years before Trump commuted his sentence.

Critics of the Prairieland incident will point to the evidence that Song discharged a firearm, injuring a law enforcement official who was treated the same day for his injuries. His attorney argued unsuccessfully that the shots were “suppressive fire” aimed at the ground near the officer rather than a deliberate attempt to kill him.

The obvious answer to all of this is for Congress to enact a domestic terrorism statute that complies with the First Amendment. It absolutely won’t do this, of course, at least so long as it’s controlled by Republicans while there’s an anti-constitutionalist in the White House. In the meantime, these cases send a chilling message – that the freedom to speak and assemble against this government, one that presides over Prairieland’s “filthy” and “inhumane” conditions that have reportedly left innocent people feeling “like an animal,” comes at serious risk. The risk to life and liberty itself.

Kimberly Wehle, Zeteo’s legal contributor and author of ‘ Constitution in Crisis,’ is a constitutional law professor at the University of Baltimore School of Law.

item.title: In 2026 Midterms, It's MAGA Against the Rest of America
item.description: Fox News recently published stunning poll results for the Senate race in solidly-Republican Ohio: Democrat Sherrod Brown led GOP incumbent Jon Husted, 53% to 45%. In an era of metronomic partisan polarization, that eight-point Democratic margin in Ohio almost defies belief. But an intra-Republican…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Fox News recently published stunning poll results for the Senate race in solidly-Republican Ohio: Democrat Sherrod Brown led GOP incumbent Jon Husted, 53% to 45%.

In an era of metronomic partisan polarization, that eight-point Democratic margin in Ohio almost defies belief. But an intra-Republican fracture explains it: Only 4% of those aligned with Donald Trump’s MAGA movement backed Brown, but 31% of “non-MAGA” Republicans crossed party lines to support the Democratic candidate.

That snapshot reflects an emerging 2026 picture that does not resemble a familiar face-off between evenly-matched Republican and Democratic two parties. Instead, the midterm elections increasingly pit the MAGA minority against the American majority of everyone else.

“This phenomenon is part of what underlies the Democrats’ overwhelming success in 30-plus special elections” during Trump 2.0, says Geoff Garin, a top Democratic pollster. And it fuels the party’s growing optimism about capturing not just the House but also the Senate by winning states like Ohio that present-day Democrats routinely lose.

Trump’s MAGA base has always been sizable and solid; four months after his 2024 re-election, NBC News polling measured it as 36% of all voters. But MAGA has never been enough to win elections on its own.

In 2016, disaffection with Democratic nominee Hillary Clinton added just enough votes to put Trump over the top. In 2024, unhappiness over Joe Biden’s record on inflation and immigration, and concern over his age built a Trump majority that Kamala Harris’s late-starting campaign couldn’t overcome.

Two years later, that majority has disappeared. Though November ballots won’t bear his name, presidential popularity represents the strongest determinant of midterm outcomes. And Trump’s second-term failures have badly eroded his standing among voters Republicans need to win.

He has dissipated his striking 2024 gains among Black and Hispanic voters. His fall among the squishier members of the Republican family may prove more consequential.

Among MAGA loyalists, 94% in YouGov polling this week say they approve of Trump’s second-term job performance; 70% declare “strong” approval. Among non-MAGA Republicans, 55% approve and just 16% approve strongly; 41% disapprove.

Those more-equivocal Republicans disappointed Democrats in 2024. The non-MAGA segment – currently about 40% of the GOP – had misgivings about Trump after his first term and its violent conclusion. But in the end, three of four backed him, finding Harris too far left and Trump more likely to help on inflation and immigration.

“The difference in this cycle,” Garin observed, “is their illusions have been shattered.”

Instead of taming inflation – still the top concern of American voters – Trump’s tariffs and failed war against Iran have worsened it. Meanwhile, the president fattens his bank account, feeds his ego with vanity projects, fabricates prosecutions against enemies, and excuses or obscures the crimes of friends, including the late pedophile Jeffrey Epstein.

Non-MAGA Republicans rated Trump positively on handling inflation by an overwhelming margin at the outset of his second term, YouGov analyst David Montgomery told me. His 66-point net positive rating then has turned net negative by 10 points – a net 76-point shift in the wrong direction.

They’ve turned skeptical on other issues, too. By two-to-one, non-MAGA Republicans in May told YouGov the economy was getting worse rather than better. A Politico poll in January found that 29% of non-MAGA Republicans considered Trump’s deportation policies “too aggressive,” twice the share of MAGA Republicans.

Ominously for the GOP, attitudes among non-MAGA Republicans keep edging closer to those of self-described independents – who have abandoned Trump in droves. On issues large and small, that produces lopsidedly negative national assessments of Trump’s priorities.

In a new Strength in Numbers/Verasight poll this week, just 18% of Americans said Trump’s Iran war had achieved its objectives. By 63% to 8%, respondents said his economic policies have benefited the wealthy and large corporations more than average Americans. A Reuters/Ipsos poll found that only 16% considered it appropriate to hold his UFC fight on White House grounds.

In other words, most of America rejects what has come from the movement that likes to call itself the “real America.”

Compared to their MAGA counterparts, says YouGov’s Montgomery, non-MAGA Republicans skew slightly younger, more male, and more non-white. Many more of them graduated from college.

Disaffection with a failed presidency doesn’t necessarily mean non-MAGA Republicans will back Democratic candidates this fall. In 2024, the overwhelming majority who didn’t support Trump chose not to vote at all.

But some will, as Brown’s Ohio lead over Husted shows. Similarly, Democratic Senate nominees are running much stronger than usual in Iowa, Texas, and North Carolina.

In Maine, scandals involving Democrat Graham Platner haven’t erased his lead over incumbent Republican Susan Collins. Democratic incumbent Jon Ossoff has seized the high ground in Georgia, while Republican incumbent Dan Sullivan struggles in Alaska. A national Fox poll in April found Democrats leading 52% to 47% for the House – partly because of support from 17% of non-MAGA Republicans.

MAGA appears loyal enough to keep Trump’s national job approval from dropping much below its current 36-39% range in the leading polling averages. Given the alienation of other Republicans, Democrats may not need it to.

John Harwood is the former chief Washington correspondent for CNBC and White House correspondent for CNN. He has interviewed every president from George H.W. Bush to Joe Biden. Sign up for ‘The Stakes with John Harwood’ to get all of his columns in your inbox.

item.title: 🌿 🦠 ‘Kill All the F*cking Algae’: Trump’s Failed DC Pyongyang-ification Is Driving Him Insane
item.description: On this day in 1876, Native Americans led by Crazy Horse and Sitting Bull wiped out U.S. forces under Lt. Col. George Armstrong Custer at the Battle of the Little Bighorn in southern Montana, the greatest Native American victory over the U.S. Army. Happy Thursday, everybody. It’s Swin again. As…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1876, Native Americans led by Crazy Horse and Sitting Bull wiped out U.S. forces under Lt. Col. George Armstrong Custer at the Battle of the Little Bighorn in southern Montana, the greatest Native American victory over the U.S. Army.

Happy Thursday, everybody. It’s Swin again. As we’ve been reporting for months at Zeteo, Donald Trump and his Republican Party have been marshaling a gargantuan sum of YOUR money – in the billions! – for initiatives designed to feed his vanity and perpetuate his cult of personality. It’s one of the more disturbing aspects of an infernally authoritarian time, the kind of thing we lied about to ourselves for decades, insisting it can’t happen here. And yet the president and his gang of thieves and reprobates are so clumsy that it’s hard not to laugh at them, even as they try to arrest you

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we dive back into Trump and the GOP’s bungled Pyongyang-ification of our capital, and how it underscores the limits of his reign of terror. Plus, Trump stokes more Capitol Hill chaos, and he stokes a shouting match with a GOP senator, while shivving his own party over a bipartisan housing bill.

On the night of June 17, I made a joke on Bluesky that Donald Trump “needs this war to end” so he “can stop worrying about war on Iran and get back to his main priority of war on Reflecting Pool.” Because the second Trump era defies all satire, an hour later, the Interior Department began comparing efforts to combat “the dead algae” plaguing the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool to Trump’s war on Iran.

The Reflecting Pool saga, now escalated to the White House imposing a mini-police-state around the pool while accused of killing ducks, is nothing if not emblematic of the Maddest-Emperor phase of Trump’s Mad Emperor presidency. He and several departments are siphoning a ton of public money, time, and resources into a vanity project. Now the project is over-budget and devolving into a mortifying self-inflicted spectacle, the president is having armed agents – and literal U.S. troops – patrol the area. Trump is bragging about how many people have been arrested or cited for disrespecting his pool, and much of the U.S. government is spreading Trump’s lie that “vandals” caused the damage that his own “renovation” has in fact wrought. The president is also threatening to sue media outlets that piss him off about the pool. And in keeping with the broader traditions of the Trump-Vance administration, they can’t even manage to pull off this small disaster without seeming suspiciously corrupt.

The Trump presidency is full of perfect metaphors for the depravity and self-defeating failures of the ruling party’s policies. For instance, Trump’s military birthday parade in DC last year was a visual representation of the blundering limitations of his fascist personality cult, and another sign that his administration can’t do anything big or small without doing it “ illegally.” Now Trump’s hyper-fixation on the reflecting pool – including his expensive, boneheaded inability to fix it and his demands for a cover-up of yet another humiliation – stands tall among painfully on-the-nose metaphors for the horror he and his party are causing and the wars they’re losing.

It has everything: flop-sweat incompetence, state-sponsored militarized crackdown, sinister but eminently laughable cultishness, deaths, and North-Korean-regime-style deception and propaganda. (That is perhaps unfair to the North Koreans, who often exercise a far greater level of message discipline than the current U.S. government.)

And it’s not just the reflecting pool! As Prem reported for Zeteo in March, the president is attempting to turn the very anti-Trump capital into his own bumbling imitation of Pyongyang, where his face, name, and MAGA hero-worship loom large, whether residents like it or not. From the Lincoln Memorial to the Kennedy Center, Trump’s efforts to rebrand the city in his image aren’t just stalling. They’re showing the limits of his authoritarian power grabs.


Earlier this month, the Kennedy Center board (which Trump stacked with loyalists) was forced to comply with a federal judge’s order to remove Trump’s name from the facade and from distributed materials. Sources who’ve spoken to Trump say he insists his name “will be back” on the building, perhaps with material harder for workers to pry off. He’s wondered aloud to confidants why the board hasn’t stacked the Kennedy Center’s general counsel’s office, which complied with the judge’s order, with “better lawyers.”

Elsewhere in DC, Trump and his allies appear incapable of doing something as basic as organizing a music festival without the whole thing falling apart.

The failures of these efforts seem to be driving Trump slowly (more) insane. Sources close to the president note an unsettling – if quintessentially Trumpian – degree of obsession and rage over the fiascos plaguing his time as decorator-in-chief, especially when it comes to his precious pool.

In the past week, the Interior Department and other offices have devoted much of their social media output to screeching at the media for reporting on the debacle, or to warning so-called “criminals” that they’ll be punished if caught tormenting the pool. (Never mind that Trump himself had SUVs driven over the pool bottom in May, after it had been repainted, leaving tire tracks in one patch that was still wet, and lingering questions about the cause of the “350-ft. gash” Trump has ranted about.)

This communications strategy comes from a top-down approach. People with knowledge of the matter, in and close to the federal government, tell me Trump has demanded, and is receiving, up-to-the-minute updates on the reflecting pool. He’s given spoken and in some cases written directives, including a very succinct: “Kill all the fucking algae.” He’s fumed at the media coverage, and in days past, he’s given conflicting, rapidly changing orders of what bald-faced lies to parade. The sources tell me Trump is personally directing this crisis-comms dumpster fire, and sending some of his most senior officials out to debase themselves by protecting the honor of his paint-peeling, algae-infested, hydrogen peroxide-filled, allegedly duck-murdering pool. (Multiple officials told me that I can’t prove Trump killed those ducks. “They could have died from anything,” one Trump appointee tells me. I’m as upset as you are that none of that could get put on the record.)

Earlier this month, the White House commanded the federal apparatus to issue statements chiding the media for “fake” news, insisting the reflecting pool was beautiful and Trump could do no wrong – only to have the president flip the script and finally acknowledge that, yes, the pool looks like shit, but it’s all the fault of those “vandals.” The vandals appear to be completely imaginary, but that’s not stopping the administration and its allies from squawking about it, all to shield Trump’s ego.

The president is doing all of this at a time when he’s supposed to be ending a war that he started and then swiftly lost in the Middle East.

“He just wants to get back to the pool and America 250, and making America great again,” one White House official says.

So, what say you, America? Feeling great yet?

  • Senate fallout:GOP tensions over Tuesday’s Senate war powers resolution burst into view at lunch with the president on Capitol Hill. Louisiana Senator Bill Cassidysaidhe presented his concerns about the war. Donald Trump attacked him and also Senator Dave McCormick for missing the key vote. The Pennsylvanian had been at an event in his state – with Trump.
  • Big talk:Regarding talks to end the war, Trumptoldreporters the U.S. was “winning by a lot” with Iran “making very big concessions.” In Kuwait, Secretary of State Marco Rubio sought to reassure Arab nations,saying: “We’re not going ⁠to do anything that undermines the security of… longstanding allies in the region.”
  • NATO meeting:Sitting with Secretary General Mark Rutte at the White House, Trumpcomplainedabout member nations not helping with his war. True to form, Rutte buttered Trump up, as “the leader of the free world,” who he said was doing “important” things in regard to Iran’s nuclear ambitions.
  • Strait talk:With the Strait of Hormuz open, oil pricesfellto their lowest levels since the start of the war. Rubio said the U.S. opposed Iran’s plan to charge tolls.
  • ‘Permission to operate’:A senior Israeli officialsaidthe Trump administration was placing “restrictions” on Israeli operations in Lebanon. Israel, however,saidit would not withdraw from areas where fighting continued despite a notional ceasefire. Iranian Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf said, “An end to the war in Lebanon is as important as an end to the war in Iran.”

Donald Trump went to Capitol Hill on Wednesday for lunch with Republican senators. His White House shit show came with him. The result was a legislative win marred by a shouting match over his illegal Iran war, and a chat to reporters so strained it seemed Senate leader John Thune was about to burst out of his collar, so clenched were his teeth and his jaw.

“We like our leader,” Trump said. “We like everybody really in the room – I don’t like a few people, but that’s OK, I think you know who they are.”

Bill Cassidy, whose Louisiana seat Trump took away, described his clash with the president:

‘You have not told the American people what’s going on. It was supposed to last four weeks. It’s lasted four months. Original objectives have not been achieved, and I want to know what’s going on.’ He did not particularly care for my comments. He raised his voice. I lost my temper. That’s not appropriate. It’s the Irish in me. But I again matched his tone and his volume and it went back and forth.

Trump was mad – “as a murder hornet,” said Senator John Kennedy – about the war powers resolution the Senate passed Tuesday, meaning both chambers have told the president to end his war or seek authorization. What happens next is untested, but the Senate delivered a huge rebuke, 50-48. Two Republicans didn’t vote. Mitch McConnell is sick. Dave McCormick was in his home state, Pennsylvania. He was with Trump for a swing-seat event, but that didn’t stop Trump from attacking him.

Incredibly, that wasn’t the most ludicrous bit of Trump’s day. Before lunch, House Speaker Mike Johnson and other top GOP figures spoke in celebration of a housing bill, a rare bipartisan measure that would keep Wall Street from buying up single-family homes – supposedly a Trump priority. But Trump suddenly said he would not sign the bill as planned. His reason: Senate Republicans won’t tear up the rules to pass the SAVE Act, a crock of nonsense about so-called voter fraud Trump thinks will save the GOP in November.

A rare boost for ordinary Americans, plunged into farce. We are ruled by a mad moron king.

  • National mauled:Bannersappeared on the Interior Department, showing George Washington as “America’s First” and Donald Trump under his slogan, “America First.” Washington looked pained. On the National Mall, Trump gave a rally-style speech on the opening night of his “Freedom 250” run of events. Guess what: herantedabout the supposed vandalism of his disastrous reflecting pool renovation.
  • NY state of mind:House Democratic leader Hakeem Jeffries, a New Yorker, andother senior Demsgave a cool reception to primary wins in the city for three left-wingers endorsed by Zohran Mamdani. Jeffriessaid, with some misplaced condescension, the mayor had “work to do in terms of the conversations that he’s going to have with members of Congress moving forward.”
  • Racists gonna racist:Trump aide Stephen Miller and wife Katie Miller blamed immigration for the New York results. “Change the voters, change the country,”saidStephen. “New York will now be run by foreign communists,”burbledKatie.
  • Too late:Retiring Republican Senator Thom Tillissaidhe regretted his decisive vote to confirm Pete Hegseth as secretary of defense, citing concerns over the former Fox host’s alleged drinking and lack of experience.
  • Post alert:Postmaster General David Steinertoldsenators that under a proposed Trump administration rule, mail-in ballots won’t be delivered if states don’t give voter info to the federal government.
  • Court wins:An appeals courtrejectedthe Trump administration’s attempt to get voter information from Michigan. A judgeblockeda Trump order requiring citizenship proof to register to vote and requiring mail ballots be received by election day in 13 blue states.

From which country did Sam Glosser, Stephen Miller’s great-grandfather, arrive in the U.S. as a penniless immigrant in 1903?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:A day after a UNreportsaid Israel “deliberately targeted Palestinian children resulting in genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes,” a 12-year-old boy waskilledby a strike that also wounded several. More than 1,000 people have been killed by Israel since the “ceasefire” last October.

  • 🇻🇪

    Major quakes:Magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 earthquakeshitVenezuela yesterday, killing more than 160 people after collapsing buildings in the capital Caracas. The death toll is expected to rise.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    Major strike:After Ukraine hit a Russian natural gas plant, President Volodymyr Zelenskysaid, “It is important that as many Russians as possible come to understand that it is the Russian leadership’s rejection of diplomacy that is prolonging the war.”

  • 🇬🇧🇫🇷

    Hot stuff:With much of western Europesufferingunder a “heat dome,” France recorded its hottest ever day, with the national thermal indicator, which averages day and night temperatures at 30 weather stations, hitting 85.6 degrees Fahrenheit (29.8 degrees Celsius). The UK also saw its hottest-ever day in June, with Gosport, Hampshire, recording a temperature of 97 degrees Fahrenheit (36.1 degrees Celsius).

  • 🇨🇴

    Colombia concession:Progressive Iván Cepedaadmitteddefeat by Abelardo de la Espriella, the far-right presidential candidate backed by Donald Trump.

  • 🇿🇼

    Power grab:Zimbabwe’s Senateapproveda bill to keep 83-year-old President Emmerson Mnangagwa, aka “The Crocodile,” in office until 2030. The measure also provides for the president to be elected by parliament.


Oratory on Capitol Hill has… changed. Here, GOP Senator Thom Tillis tells reporters what he thinks shameless MAGA warrior Bill Pulte is about to produce as acting director of national intelligence. It’s almost as if Trumpism has coarsened our politics.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

George Armstrong Custer at the Battle of the Little Bighorn in southern Montana, the greatest Native American victory over the U.S. Army. Happy Thursday, everybody. It’s Swin again. As…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1876, Native Americans led by Crazy Horse and Sitting Bull wiped out U.S. forces under Lt. Col. George Armstrong Custer at the Battle of the Little Bighorn in southern Montana, the greatest Native American victory over the U.S. Army.

Happy Thursday, everybody. It’s Swin again. As we’ve been reporting for months at Zeteo, Donald Trump and his Republican Party have been marshaling a gargantuan sum of YOUR money – in the billions! – for initiatives designed to feed his vanity and perpetuate his cult of personality. The Reflecting Pool saga, now escalated to the White House imposing a mini-police-state around the pool while accused of killing ducks, is nothing if not emblematic of the Maddest-Emperor phase of Trump’s Mad Emperor presidency. He and several departments are siphoning a ton of public money, time, and resources into a vanity project. Now the project is over-budget and devolving into a mortifying self-inflicted spectacle, the president is having armed agents – and literal U.S. troops – patrol the area. Trump is bragging about how many people have been arrested or cited for disrespecting his pool, and much of the U.S. government is spreading Trump’s lie that “vandals” caused the damage that his own “renovation” has in fact wrought. The president is also threatening to sue media outlets that piss him off about the pool. And in keeping with the broader traditions of the Trump-Vance administration, they can’t even manage to pull off this small disaster without seeming suspiciously corrupt.

The Trump presidency is full of perfect metaphors for the depravity and self-defeating failures of the ruling party’s policies. For instance, Trump’s military birthday parade in DC last year was a visual representation of the blundering limitations of his fascist personality cult, and another sign that his administration can’t do anything big or small without doing it “ illegally.” Now Trump’s hyper-fixation on the reflecting pool – including his expensive, boneheaded inability to fix it and his demands for a cover-up of yet another humiliation – stands tall among painfully on-the-nose metaphors for the horror he and his party are causing and the wars they’re losing.

It has everything: flop-sweat incompetence, state-sponsored militarized crackdown, sinister but eminently laughable cultishness, deaths, and North-Korean-regime-style deception and propaganda. (That is perhaps unfair to the North Koreans, who often exercise a far greater level of message discipline than the current U.S. government.)

And it’s not just the reflecting pool! As Prem reported for Zeteo in March, the president is attempting to turn the very anti-Trump capital into his own bumbling imitation of Pyongyang, where his face, name, and MAGA hero-worship loom large, whether residents like it or not. From the Lincoln Memorial to the Kennedy Center, Trump’s efforts to rebrand the city in his image aren’t just stalling. They’re showing the limits of his authoritarian power grabs.


Earlier this month, the Kennedy Center board (which Trump stacked with loyalists) was forced to comply with a federal judge’s order to remove Trump’s name from the facade and from distributed materials. Sources who’ve spoken to Trump say he insists his name “will be back” on the building, perhaps with material harder for workers to pry off. He’s wondered aloud to confidants why the board hasn’t stacked the Kennedy Center’s general counsel’s office, which complied with the judge’s order, with “better lawyers.”

Elsewhere in DC, Trump and his allies appear incapable of doing something as basic as organizing a music festival without the whole thing falling apart.

The failures of these efforts seem to be driving Trump slowly (more) insane. Sources close to the president note an unsettling – if quintessentially Trumpian – degree of obsession and rage over the fiascos plaguing his time as decorator-in-chief, especially when it comes to his precious pool.

In the past week, the Interior Department and other offices have devoted much of their social media output to screeching at the media for reporting on the debacle, or to warning so-called “criminals” that they’ll be punished if caught tormenting the pool. (Never mind that Trump himself had SUVs driven over the pool bottom in May, after it had been repainted, leaving tire tracks in one patch that was still wet, and lingering questions about the cause of the “350-ft. gash” Trump has ranted about.)

This communications strategy comes from a top-down approach. People with knowledge of the matter, in and close to the federal government, tell me Trump has demanded, and is receiving, up-to-the-minute updates on the reflecting pool. He’s given spoken and in some cases written directives, including a very succinct: “Kill all the fucking algae.” He’s fumed at the media coverage, and in days past, he’s given conflicting, rapidly changing orders of what bald-faced lies to parade. The sources tell me Trump is personally directing this crisis-comms dumpster fire, and sending some of his most senior officials out to debase themselves by protecting the honor of his paint-peeling, algae-infested, hydrogen peroxide-filled, allegedly duck-murdering pool. (Multiple officials told me that I can’t prove Trump killed those ducks. “They could have died from anything,” one Trump appointee tells me. I’m as upset as you are that none of that could get put on the record.)

Earlier this month, the White House commanded the federal apparatus to issue statements chiding the media for “fake” news, insisting the reflecting pool was beautiful and Trump could do no wrong – only to have the president flip the script and finally acknowledge that, yes, the pool looks like shit, but it’s all the fault of those “vandals.” The vandals appear to be completely imaginary, but that’s not stopping the administration and its allies from squawking about it, all to shield Trump’s ego.

The president is doing all of this at a time when he’s supposed to be ending a war that he started and then swiftly lost in the Middle East.

“He just wants to get back to the pool and America 250, and making America great again,” one White House official says.

So, what say you, America? Feeling great yet?

  • Senate fallout:GOP tensions over Tuesday’s Senate war powers resolution burst into view at lunch with the president on Capitol Hill. Louisiana Senator Bill Cassidysaidhe presented his concerns about the war. Donald Trump attacked him and also Senator Dave McCormick for missing the key vote. The Pennsylvanian had been at an event in his state – with Trump.
  • Big talk:Regarding talks to end the war, Trumptoldreporters the U.S. was “winning by a lot” with Iran “making very big concessions.” In Kuwait, Secretary of State Marco Rubio sought to reassure Arab nations,saying: “We’re not going ⁠to do anything that undermines the security of… longstanding allies in the region.”
  • NATO meeting:Sitting with Secretary General Mark Rutte at the White House, Trumpcomplainedabout member nations not helping with his war. True to form, Rutte buttered Trump up, as “the leader of the free world,” who he said was doing “important” things in regard to Iran’s nuclear ambitions.
  • Strait talk:With the Strait of Hormuz open, oil pricesfellto their lowest levels since the start of the war. Rubio said the U.S. opposed Iran’s plan to charge tolls.
  • ‘Permission to operate’:A senior Israeli officialsaidthe Trump administration was placing “restrictions” on Israeli operations in Lebanon. Israel, however,saidit would not withdraw from areas where fighting continued despite a notional ceasefire. Iranian Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf said, “An end to the war in Lebanon is as important as an end to the war in Iran.”

Donald Trump went to Capitol Hill on Wednesday for lunch with Republican senators. His White House shit show came with him. The result was a legislative win marred by a shouting match over his illegal Iran war, and a chat to reporters so strained it seemed Senate leader John Thune was about to burst out of his collar, so clenched were his teeth and his jaw.

“We like our leader,” Trump said. “We like everybody really in the room – I don’t like a few people, but that’s OK, I think you know who they are.”

Bill Cassidy, whose Louisiana seat Trump took away, described his clash with the president:

‘You have not told the American people what’s going on. It was supposed to last four weeks. It’s lasted four months. Original objectives have not been achieved, and I want to know what’s going on.’ He did not particularly care for my comments. He raised his voice. I lost my temper. That’s not appropriate. It’s the Irish in me. But I again matched his tone and his volume and it went back and forth.

Trump was mad – “as a murder hornet,” said Senator John Kennedy – about the war powers resolution the Senate passed Tuesday, meaning both chambers have told the president to end his war or seek authorization. What happens next is untested, but the Senate delivered a huge rebuke, 50-48. Two Republicans didn’t vote. Mitch McConnell is sick. Dave McCormick was in his home state, Pennsylvania. He was with Trump for a swing-seat event, but that didn’t stop Trump from attacking him.

Incredibly, that wasn’t the most ludicrous bit of Trump’s day. Before lunch, House Speaker Mike Johnson and other top GOP figures spoke in celebration of a housing bill, a rare bipartisan measure that would keep Wall Street from buying up single-family homes – supposedly a Trump priority. But Trump suddenly said he would not sign the bill as planned. His reason: Senate Republicans won’t tear up the rules to pass the SAVE Act, a crock of nonsense about so-called voter fraud Trump thinks will save the GOP in November.

A rare boost for ordinary Americans, plunged into farce. We are ruled by a mad moron king.

  • National mauled:Bannersappeared on the Interior Department, showing George Washington as “America’s First” and Donald Trump under his slogan, “America First.” Washington looked pained. On the National Mall, Trump gave a rally-style speech on the opening night of his “Freedom 250” run of events. Guess what: herantedabout the supposed vandalism of his disastrous reflecting pool renovation.
  • NY state of mind:House Democratic leader Hakeem Jeffries, a New Yorker, andother senior Demsgave a cool reception to primary wins in the city for three left-wingers endorsed by Zohran Mamdani. Jeffriessaid, with some misplaced condescension, the mayor had “work to do in terms of the conversations that he’s going to have with members of Congress moving forward.”
  • Racists gonna racist:Trump aide Stephen Miller and wife Katie Miller blamed immigration for the New York results. “Change the voters, change the country,”saidStephen. “New York will now be run by foreign communists,”burbledKatie.
  • Too late:Retiring Republican Senator Thom Tillissaidhe regretted his decisive vote to confirm Pete Hegseth as secretary of defense, citing concerns over the former Fox host’s alleged drinking and lack of experience.
  • Post alert:Postmaster General David Steinertoldsenators that under a proposed Trump administration rule, mail-in ballots won’t be delivered if states don’t give voter info to the federal government.
  • Court wins:An appeals courtrejectedthe Trump administration’s attempt to get voter information from Michigan. A judgeblockeda Trump order requiring citizenship proof to register to vote and requiring mail ballots be received by election day in 13 blue states.

From which country did Sam Glosser, Stephen Miller’s great-grandfather, arrive in the U.S. as a penniless immigrant in 1903?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:A day after a UNreportsaid Israel “deliberately targeted Palestinian children resulting in genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes,” a 12-year-old boy waskilledby a strike that also wounded several. More than 1,000 people have been killed by Israel since the “ceasefire” last October.

  • 🇻🇪

    Major quakes:Magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 earthquakeshitVenezuela yesterday, killing more than 160 people after collapsing buildings in the capital Caracas. The death toll is expected to rise.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    Major strike:After Ukraine hit a Russian natural gas plant, President Volodymyr Zelenskysaid, “It is important that as many Russians as possible come to understand that it is the Russian leadership’s rejection of diplomacy that is prolonging the war.”

  • 🇬🇧🇫🇷

    Hot stuff:With much of western Europesufferingunder a “heat dome,” France recorded its hottest ever day, with the national thermal indicator, which averages day and night temperatures at 30 weather stations, hitting 85.6 degrees Fahrenheit (29.8 degrees Celsius). The UK also saw its hottest-ever day in June, with Gosport, Hampshire, recording a temperature of 97 degrees Fahrenheit (36.1 degrees Celsius).

  • 🇨🇴

    Colombia concession:Progressive Iván Cepedaadmitteddefeat by Abelardo de la Espriella, the far-right presidential candidate backed by Donald Trump.

  • 🇿🇼

    Power grab:Zimbabwe’s Senateapproveda bill to keep 83-year-old President Emmerson Mnangagwa, aka “The Crocodile,” in office until 2030. The measure also provides for the president to be elected by parliament.


Oratory on Capitol Hill has… changed. Here, GOP Senator Thom Tillis tells reporters what he thinks shameless MAGA warrior Bill Pulte is about to produce as acting director of national intelligence. It’s almost as if Trumpism has coarsened our politics.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.
