item.title: Is Israel Dividing Fox with Trump?
item.description: Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we look at how Trump’s ‘Fox News Cabinet’ is bent out of shape over the president “capitulating”…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we look at how Trump’s ‘Fox News Cabinet’ is bent out of shape over the president “capitulating” to Iran and turning its back on Israel. Plus, is Tony Dokoupil trying to rehab his image by distancing himself from Bari Weiss? Also, what does the CBS News ombudsman actually DO at the network?

Donald Trump’s ‘Fox News Cabinet,’ which gleefully pushed the administration to join Israel in launching its doomed and illegal war against Iran, is now beside itself that the president is desperately backing out of the fight by signing a “weak” peace agreement that reveals without a shred of doubt that the United States lost.

Mark Levin, perhaps the most influential of the pro-Israel Fox MAGA hawks in convincing Trump that it was necessary to jump into the Middle East bloodbath, has made it clear over the last few days that he feels that the administration – and specifically Vice President JD Vance – has turned its back on Israel.

“I want to say to people in and out of the administration, stop trashing, smearing, bullying the little state of Israel,” Levin, who also serves on the president’s Homeland Security Advisory Council, raged during this past weekend’s broadcast of his Fox show. “Stop cozying up to and telling us that the enemy regime in Iran is now more rational, more moderate, and a regime that we can deal with.”


Levin, as you may remember, has not only been a loyal MAGA sycophant throughout Trump’s two terms in office, but also was one of the key media figures who pressed the president into joining Israel’s first brief conflict with Iran last year. It was also Levin’s hawkish stance that helped spark the “MAGA civil war” between the Tucker Carlson isolationist wing of the conservative movement and the establishment neocons of Fox.

Prior to going scorched earth on Fox’s airwaves, Levin had singled out Vance – who has been seen as the architect of the memorandum of understanding with Iran – for warning Israel’s cabinet ministers against criticizing the United States over the ceasefire agreement.

“Donald J. Trump is the only head of state in the entire world who is sympathetic to the nation of Israel at this moment in time… If I was in the cabinet of the Israeli government, I might not be attacking the only powerful ally that I have anywhere left in the entire world,” Vance said during a White House press conference last week.

“I think it’s terrific that people who will likely seek the GOP nomination for president are revealing themselves,” Levin raged on social media. “Amazing how Iran is being talked about as increasingly reasonable and moderate as Israel is being smeared left and Woke Right.”

Levin, who had been begging Trump to “finish off” Iran days before the MOU was signed, has continuously trashed the deal as an “egregious capitulation” to Hezbollah due to the Israel-Lebanon ceasefire while torching the proposed $300 billion “slush fund” to Iran. “Not a word about the regime paying reparations to our country, Israel, or the Arab countries for the massive damage caused by their ballistic missiles and other missiles,” he groused.

Notably, throughout all of this caterwauling over the MOU and how Iran is able to “thumb their nose at us” over the deal, Levin has largely kept the president’s name out of his mouth – instead, spreading the blame to Vance and the rest of the administration. “Why can’t you say Trump’s name when you criticize him? Are you scared Mark?” Mehdi pointedly asked the conservative pundit last week.

Meanwhile, Levin – who has been extremely outspoken against MAGA anti-war critics such as Megyn Kelly and Carlson – isn’t the only Fox warmonger who has been bent out of shape over the MOU and Vance’s broadsides against Israel.

Brian Kilmeade, co-host of Trump’s favorite morning show ‘Fox & Friends,’ has also urged the vice president to “rethink his attack on Israel” before calling for Trump – who has privately raged that Israel is trying to drag him back into the war – to reassert his cozy relationship with the Israeli government.

“The president is the best friend Israel has ever had — that is 100% true. But Israel will survive,” Kilmeade declared on Friday’s broadcast of ‘The Five.’ “They are not gonna die from a nuclear weapon. They’ll knock it outta the sky, do a preemptive attack.”

The hawkish Fox host also said that the MOU is on Vance, not Trump. “But this is his deal. It’s not the president’s deal,” he declared last week. And during Monday’s broadcast, amid scathing conservative criticism of the agreement, Kilmeade went so far as to suggest the vice president should be sidelined from peace negotiations.

“How much better would you feel if Marco Rubio was in that room as opposed to or with the Vice President of the United States?” Kilmeade wondered during an interview with GOP Senator Tim Sheehy.

And then there’s Marc Thiessen, the neoconservative ex-George W. Bush staffer who has become one of the president’s most-trusted Fox advisers amid the war. He’s written that the MOU “risks squandering” the president’s “historic victory in Iran” while griping about the “major cash infusion” the deal gives Iran.

Vance, for his part, has taken his own shots at these pundits amid their complaints. During an appearance on Kelly’s show last week, he was asked about Levin and Thiessen criticizing the MOU as a “Vance deal,” prompting him to accuse the pair of parroting Iranian media.

“So first of all, why do they believe Iranian propaganda only about one thing, the peace deal? They don’t seem to believe Iranian propaganda – rightfully so – about anything else,” the vice president reacted.

Still, while Levin, Kilmeade, and Thiessen have been outspoken in their disdain for the Iran deal and what it means for Israel, other Fox stars have dutifully backed Vance and the administration – and at least one made some eye-popping comments.

“Vance sent a warning to the Jews,” Jesse Watters noted on his Thursday night broadcast, prompting Mehdi to wonder where the Anti-Defamation League and its CEO, Jonathan Greenblatt, were in admonishing those remarks. “All the people who normally obsess over antisemitic placards from pro-Palestine college students? Is this not MUCH worse??” Mehdi added.

Amid the fallout from the “shitshow” that Bari Weiss set off inside CBS News with the “Black Thursday” firings at ‘60 Minutes, which is the pinnacle of the extensive damage she’s created to the network’s brand (and that of its parent company), the face of her CBS reinvention is looking to rehabilitate his own image – by distancing himself from the anti-woke editor-in-chief.

In recent weeks, Tony Dokoupil – who was handpicked by Weiss in December to anchor ‘CBS Evening News,’ the news broadcast made famous by Walter Cronkite – has attempted to reintroduce himself to the public in a seeming effort to be portrayed in a new light, especially in an interview with the Wall Street Journal this week.

“When it comes to Bari Weiss, she’s the editor in chief, she runs a 9 a.m. meeting and has lots of ideas,” he told the Journal this week, suggesting that he and the show aren’t micromanaged by Weiss. “When we like the idea, we use it. If we don’t and if it doesn’t work for our show, we don’t.”

At the same time, Dokoupil also asserted that he’s “never met David Ellison,” the owner of Paramount, who has been accused of pushing CBS in a MAGA-friendly direction in an apparent attempt to curry favor with the Trump administration amid the upcoming Warner Bros. merger.

“He’s never had a comment about my show. He’s never called me to complain about coverage. If he tried to, it wouldn’t have an impact,” Dokoupil insisted.

“This is the [CBS] PR team,” a CBS News insider told me. “They’re just trying to soften the image of Tony and trying to wash away everything that happened before while trying to present the show as a cohesive unit,” the insider added, saying it was notable that Dokoupil made the comments to the Journal.

Elsewhere in the WSJ profile, Dokoupil also critically addressed the visa snafu that led to his broadcasting from Taiwan during Trump’s U.S.-China summit and prompted a series of embarrassing headlines. “It was a mistake,” he said.

He further nodded to his rough first few months in the anchor chair, which began with a cringeworthy boast that he’d be more trustworthy than Cronkite and was beset by bottom-tier ratings, technical gaffes, friendly interviews with Trump administration officials, an ICE ride-along with Kristi Noem, and a widely mocked on-air salute to “ultimate Florida man” Marco Rubio.

“Dokoupil told The Journal he was making a joke, not trying to be cute about a Trump administration official, but that he wouldn’t do it again,” the Journal noted.

Still, Dokoupil appeared to thread the needle when it came to having empathy for his fired ‘60 Minutes’ colleagues and endorsing their claims that Weiss had attempted to inject political bias into their stories and exhibited editorial interference.

“When they make comments like that, I pay attention. But I can only speak for what’s happening on this side of the street, and it’s not the experience that I’ve had,” he said, while in a comment to the Journal, Weiss added: “What’s being called ‘editorial interference’ is in reality the job description of an editor in chief.”

Weeks before the WSJ interview, Dokoupil appeared to make his first step towards creating space between his show and Weiss. Following the ‘60 Minutes’ overhaul, which saw Weiss fire correspondents Sharyn Alfonsi and Cecilia Vega and push out the show’s senior leadership. ‘60 Minutes’ stalwart Scott Pelley was unceremoniously ousted after a heated clash with new executive producer Nick Bilton, a tech journalist with no broadcast news experience.

The ‘Evening News’ ran a detailed, fact-based report about Pelley’s firing before Dokoupil gave his own tribute to the longtime CBS News journalist and his career. According to some staffers and network insiders, the package on Pelley was seen as a way to help draw some distance between Weiss and Dokoupil amid the five-alarm controversy.

With the never-ending series of negative headlines and media coverage obviously taking a toll, as one reporter noted that Dokpupil “seems forlorn” and has lost “energy in his delivery,” it was almost certainly some welcome relief to be the focal point of a holiday puff-piece this past weekend.

In a Father’s Day feature in Us Weekly, Dokoupil joined other TV news broadcasters in writing letters to their children to mark the occasion. “When you’re in the house, it’s like a warm fire. When we’re apart I miss you. Because I work in news, and I have for all your lives, I hope you already know that we’ve been lucky, and that this time is precious,” he wrote.

“Between this and the [Us Weekly] Father’s Day spread, they’re clearly trying to rehabilitate Tony’s reputation,” one CBS News senior staffer told me, before adding: “I think the damage is done. More transparent than Walter Cronkite? Get lost…”

Whether this image rehab works for Dokoupil or not, one thing is abundantly clear – the Paramount machine knows that Weiss is a publicly toxic commodity right now. Even if Ellison remains committed to her as the long-term editorial steward of a combined CNN-CBS News, which is very much to be seen, the brass would prefer that she stay in the background for the time being.

Shortly before he installed Weiss as CBS News chief, Ellison appointed former Trump appointee and right-wing think tank leader Kenneth Weinstein to be the network’s ombudsman – which was part of an agreement he made with FCC chairman (and Trump media attack dog) Brendan Carr to push the Paramount merger through.

Yet, with the news network mired in unending scandal and internal turmoil amid Weiss’s tempestuous reign, the New York Times noted that media experts have torched Paramount for having “essentially hired a watchdog who doesn’t bark.”

In the nine months since he was hired, Weinstein hasn’t issued any public statements about the network’s news coverage or controversies – such as Weiss being accused by ‘60 Minutes’ reporters of politically meddling with their stories. CBS staffers have also said he hasn’t issued any internal guidance or feedback.

Defending Weinstein, who told CBS employees he’s only scheduled to work one day a month, Paramount said in a statement that he’s doing his job. “Since September, he’s independently assessed the issues brought to him and, when appropriate, discussed them with CBS News and Paramount Skydance leadership,” the company said.

Meanwhile, one person who doesn’t think Weinstein needs to do anything is Carr – who recently said he didn’t think the ombudsman should look into the ‘60 Minutes’ correspondents’ claims of bias.

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘ Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Zohran Mamdani Is the Democrats’ New Kingmaker
item.description: Several months ago, with primary elections coming up, New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani and the city chapter of the Democratic Socialists of America decided to make them a test of strength. Mamdani endorsed three candidates for Congress, and both he and NYC-DSA worked overtime campaigning and…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Several months ago, with primary elections coming up, New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani and the city chapter of the Democratic Socialists of America decided to make them a test of strength. Mamdani endorsed three candidates for Congress, and both he and NYC-DSA worked overtime campaigning and fundraising for them.

The election was Tuesday, and the result was a clean sweep for Mamdani’s candidates. His friend Brad Lander easily defeated incumbent Rep. Dan Goldman in the 10th district, while Claire Valdez defeated Brooklyn borough president Antonio Reynoso for an open seat in the 7th district. In a shocking upset, former Mamdani campaign volunteer Darializa Avila Chevalier defeated Rep. Adriano Espaillat, a five-term incumbent and the chair of the Congressional Hispanic Caucus, in the 13th district.

Mamdani and his DSA allies are proving to be something of local kingmakers – and fairly cutthroat ones too. Espaillat endorsed Mamdani for the general election in the mayor’s race, while Nydia Velázquez – the 7th district’s retiring incumbent, who supported Reynoso – endorsed Mamdani for the primary.

For Mamdani, playing nice with establishment Democrats is evidently not as important as building up his political power base and putting allies into key positions.

Mamdani’s evident knack for power politics may end up being nationally important as Democratic Party voters ponder whether or not to run Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez for president against whomever the GOP nominates. (My money is on either Donald Trump or Ratko Mladić.)

The 13th district victory was particularly welcome because, while Chevalier did have a long history of posting inflammatory things online (all too common these days, alas), Espaillat’s allies descended to Elon Musk-esque gutter racism trying to win, with a former senior adviser accusing Chevalier of somehow conspiring to replace local Dominicans with Haitians and Muslims. (Espaillat rejected the comments.)

Eight out of 10 DSA candidates also won at the state level, with another too close to call at the time of writing. Amusingly, Mamdani did not endorse any state candidates, reportedly to maintain good relations with Assembly Speaker Carl Heastie; meanwhile AOC endorsed at the state level but not the national one, to maintain good relations with the House Democratic leadership, and then in the end almost the whole slate won anyway.

Progressive candidates with Brooklyn Can’t Wait – which is not a NYC-DSA project but is politically adjacent – have also taken an apparent majority on the obscure but highly influential Brooklyn Democratic Committee. It might be the death blow to the infamous Brooklyn Democratic machine, which is about as corrupt as Tammany Hall of old, except the public gets almost none of the patronage spoils. The fact that former Mayor Eric Adams – a former aide of his was indicted for corruption as I was writing this article – got his start there gives you a sense of it.

One of the most annoying metaphors in common political parlance is that of “political capital,” which, as typically used, implies that a politician has a certain stock of power that is used up whenever they do anything. Construct a new bus lane, and it dwindles a little bit; set up a big healthcare program, it dwindles a lot.

It grates because it’s the exact opposite of how capital is supposed to work in the real world. The entire point, as everyone from orthodox Marxists to Wall Street investors will tell you, is to put your capital into something that will deliver a return. “Spending” your capital is supposed to give you more capital.

Now, there arguably is something to the idea that politicians have a relatively short window of opportunity when they are first elected to get big things done, and that voters tend to dislike big changes at first no matter what they are, but those would better be called political momentum and status quo bias respectively.

“Investing political capital” ought to be reserved for actions that bolster a politician or organization’s power – and Mamdani and NYC-DSA have done just that. Winning primaries in which the entire party establishment and even most of the local labor movement were on the other side will strike fear into the hearts of other incumbents in New York. Half of power, after all, is being perceived to have power. New York members of Congress, city council members, and other officials will be looking to get on Mamdani’s good side. People who were planning on opposing him will think twice.

As I have previously discussed, Mamdani is proving to be quite an adept leader. By selecting some tough but winnable races, he took a calculated gamble – as opposed to endorsing someone in every state primary, which would have made him look hapless and weak. The moderate Rep. Tom Suozzi won easily out in Long Island, but that is well outside of Mamdani’s zone of influence – for now. As noted above, Mamdani also cannily swapped endorsement duties with AOC.

And this victory, in turn, builds on more than a decade of grinding organizing work. NYC-DSA has been laying the groundwork for all out-battles for national seats since the mid-2010s. In 2018, after years of preparation, they defeated the Independent Democratic Caucus, a faction of turncoat state senators then-Governor Andrew Cuomo had set up to hand control of the state Senate to the GOP. That year was also when AOC turfed out Rep. Joe Crowley, to be fair, but that was more a story of the incumbent being caught napping.

Eight years later, NYC-DSA has built a highly effective turnout operation. It was the most important group helping Mamdani defeat Cuomo for the mayoralty, and now together they went punch for punch with a powerful incumbent member of Congress who fought dirty, and still beat him.

Obviously New York City is an unusual place, and one should not extrapolate Mamdani’s wins too far. But there is clearly something happening all around the country. In recent primary elections in Washington, DC, the DC-DSA slate won every race it contested, including the mayor’s. In Los Angeles, LA-DSA member Nithya Raman is facing down incumbent Mayor Karen Bass in the general election this year.

Unlike in Trump’s first term, rank-and-file Democrats are dissatisfied with their party leadership. They are itching for bareknuckle political brawlers who will take the fight directly to Trump, and they also want leaders to reflect the new overwhelmingly anti-Israel, anti-ICE party consensus.

The reason Espaillat drew his primary challenger was reportedly that he was very reluctant to help or meet with Mahmoud Khalil, the Palestinian academic who has been viciously persecuted by the Trump regime. It seems that sort of behavior is where Zohran Mamdani is drawing the line, and if polling is any judge, Democratic voters are behind him.

Ryan Cooper is a senior editor at the American Prospect, and the author of ‘How Are You Going to Pay for That?: Smart Answers to the Dumbest Question in Politics.’

item.title: Trump Told Netanyahu Everybody, Including ‘All the Jews,’ Is Sick of Him
item.description: There are shocking headlines to be drawn from Regime Change , the new blockbuster by Early on, Trump is shown meeting in the Oval Office with Tucker Carlson and Elon Musk. Musk is distracted, the then-billionaire man-child “transfixed” by a golden pager given by Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
There are shocking headlines to be drawn from Regime Change, the new blockbuster by

Early on, Trump is shown meeting in the Oval Office with Tucker Carlson and Elon Musk. Musk is distracted, the then-billionaire man-child “transfixed” by a golden pager given by Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, “staring at it intently, silently working it between his fingers like a Rubik’s Cube.”

“’Don’t push the button,’ Trump joked.”

It was February 2025. The president’s “joke” emerged from his fascination with Israel’s attack on Hezbollah leaders in Lebanon the previous September, using exploding pagers: an operation that killed at least 32, maimed more than 3,200, and was widely condemned as a war crime. Never mind pesky things like morality. As described by Haberman and Swan:

Trump then ‘regaled’ his guests ‘with horror stories of the destruction that the explosions had wrought. He had seen pictures, he said. Mutilated genitals and missing hands. He was horrified by the injuries, but fascinated as well, lingering on the scenes and the details. One survivor, he said, ‘looked like a great white came and just took a chunk out of him. It was like a shark bite. It was horrible.’ He grew voluble, repeating, ‘It’s horrible, horrible!’

If you were paying attention in season one, you may recall Trump’s view of sharks according to Stormy Daniels – “He is obsessed with sharks… Terrified of sharks… ‘I hope all the sharks die’” – and you may laugh. But this is season two, altogether darker. The scene continues:

And there was something else that captivated him. Many of the devices had detonated in public, and it was hard to know who was holding a pager when it exploded. The indiscriminate nature of the killing and maiming had shocked Trump, and while he was taken by the ingenuity, he showed a measure of disbelief at its recklessness. He seemed at once enthralled and horrified.

This man is president. And yet the book has many more worrying places to go.

Carlson’s Oval Office visit also produces an exchange in which he tells Trump, Israel wants him “to go to war with Iran.” Trump says, “We’re not doing that.” Carlson replies: “Good, because the only thing that can blow up your administration is if you get into a war with Iran.”

Prescient words, for a Fox host turned testicle-tanning far-right podcaster who claims to have been attacked in bed by a demon. Granted, words recalled after the event, presenting their speaker in his best (testicle-tanning) light. Still, score one for Tucker.

As Regime Change is published, the Iran war Trump started on Israel’s say-so is lurching to a close. Or not. Lumbered with negotiations, Vice President JD Vance also has Trump’s promise of blame if he fails. With meticulous reporting including from inside the Situation Room, Haberman and Swan show how Netanyahu guided Trump into this hell and will do so again if he chooses, Vance just one more hapless lackey. Whatever the Israeli PM is, he is not an idiot. And he knows one when he sees one.

Reading Regime Change, it’s more than mildly disturbing to find one’s self experiencing the slightest, briefest, creeping, fragile hint of appreciation for the diplomatic efforts of Jared Kushner. Trump’s son-in-law has no official role in government or in attempts for Middle East peace. It’s clear he’s primarily in the game for his own obscene profit. His vision for a “New Gaza” is ridiculous, his version of peace not one that contains a scintilla of justice for the Palestinian people. But at least – at the very least – he’s tried to stop the killing. Compare him to Trump, and the Big Lebowski occurs: “Say what you like about Jared Kushner, at least he has an ethos.”

Then again, maybe not. Kushner, Trump’s real-estate pal turned haplessly overstretched envoy, Steve Witkoff, and their ally Tony Blair, a former prime minister who knows a lot about war, having built a good case to be indicted for war crimes, deserve every humiliation they get. And as Haberman and Swan show, under Trump they sure do get them.

Trump came up with a brilliant idea for how to save Gaza: “What if we own it? That’s a good idea, right? We’ll just own Gaza.” Mike Waltz, then national security adviser, and communications director Steven Cheung are among the profiles in courage shown kowtowing on that one. Unchecked, the mad emperor went out and spewed to the press.

“Legitimately nutso,” one senior aide said later. “But very on brand.”

It’s also very on brand only to voice such an opinion later, anonymously, to a couple of New York Times reporters with a book deal. But that’s the world we live in.

The U.S. does not own Gaza. Talks with Iran drag on. Israel keeps on killing, deaths from its airstrikes, drones, tanks, and guns past 73,000, a dreadful feat in less than three years. Amid such obscenity, the best response to reading Haberman and Swan’s meticulous account of Trump’s Israel “policy” – a term used only as a catch-all, language not having caught up with the horror – is anger. Often as I read Regime Change I recalled my thoughts on reading Truss at 10, Anthony Seldon and Jonathan Meakin’s 2024 account of Britain’s own mad (thankfully former) leader, Liz Truss:

Trump’s power and danger is fueled by his truly incredible ignorance. In scenes from summer 2025, Swan and Haberman show Kushner and Blair “at work on a postwar rebuilding plan for Gaza… jointly present[ing] their ideas to Trump on how to handle Gaza, with its millions of Palestinian refugees, in a meeting at the White House. When Trump looked over the plan, he offered a predictable comment about the prospects for building a Trump-branded hotel in Gaza – a remark an advisor quickly insisted was made in jest.”

No, again, it wasn’t. Nor is it remotely funny that a growing sense of optimism was punctured dramatically, Swan and Haberman write, “about 12 hours later, when Israel fired missiles at a site in Doha, Qatar, targeting a meeting of Hamas negotiators focused on a possible end to the war [in Gaza]… a stunning airborne attack on the capital of a U.S. ally whose leaders could help broker the very deals Trump was hoping for.”

Kushner and Witkoff were “enraged.” They can’t have been surprised.

According to Swan and Haberman, that September, Trump told Netanyahu: “Everybody’s sick of you, Bibi. All the Jews are sick of you. Even the two Jews on this call are sick of you.” The two men have had more clashes since. But Trump is consistent in nothing except his inconsistency. Netanyahu will always be able to bend the president’s ear.

There’s much more in Regime Change, about every aspect of Trump’s chaotic presidency, including a fuller version of an excerpt published by the Times in April, headlined “How Trump Took the U.S. to War With Iran,” which like other episodes features the sort of Situation Room reporting that reportedly has the White House worried about leaks. Such reports, usually in Axios, are of course constructed of anonymous quotes to reporters by aides professing to be concerned about anonymous quotes to reporters. The parlor game of working out who leaked what to Swan and Haberman is fun – hint: In the Situation Room, where recording isn’t allowed, some principals dialed in – but it’s ultimately pointless. As Zeteo has reported, the administration’s internal leak hunt hasn’t gone well, because everyone leaked. In the end, Washington’s gonna Washington, reporters gonna report.

It’s just as well that reporters as prodigiously talented, if now by their own accounts extremely tired, as Haberman and Swan will stay on the beat and the case. It’s also just as well that Regime Change contains clear indications the authors do not see themselves as holier-than-thou Times types, above the fray, claiming “objectivity,” reporting for posterity. Their tone is measured, on the page and in interviews too, but horror at what Trump has wrought runs through their work like a vein.

A sit-down interview finishes the book, Haberman and Swan on the other side of the Resolute Desk from their subject. What transpires is “legitimately nutso,” Trump delightedly comparing himself to Hitler, Mao, Napoleon and other leading maniacs, citing a “historian” who turns out to be Gary Player’s caddy. Swan and Haberman don’t like comparisons to Woodward and Bernstein but they can’t avoid them. Nixon in his final days, drinking and ranting at the tapes in the walls, was just a warm-up for Mad King Donald.

“We gathered our things and walked out,” the authors write, in their final lines, set in mid-March this year. “The rain had eased, though a heavy gray still hung over the capital.”

As Regime Change comes out, it is summer. In Washington, at sunset, the storm clouds are sometimes tinted orange.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: AIPAC Loses, as the Left Wins Big in New York
item.description: On this day in 2022, five conservative justices on the Supreme Court, three installed by Donald Trump in his first term, ended the federal right to abortion by striking down Roe v. Wade. Good morning! Andrew here, to recap another major 2026 midterm primary night. And boy, was it a big night for…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 2022, five conservative justices on the Supreme Court, three installed by Donald Trump in his first term, ended the federal right to abortion by striking down Roe v. Wade.

Good morning! Andrew here, to recap another major 2026 midterm primary night. And boy, was it a big night for the left in New York. I work for Zeteo, not corporate media, so I can say this without having to pack up my desk: We love to see it, folks.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Zohran Mamdani sends three anti-AIPAC Democrats to Congress, John Fetterman cries about it, and a UN panel says Israel “deliberately targeted Palestinian children resulting in genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes.”

The Democrats’ left flank in Congress is about to expand, and move a whole lot further left, thanks to Zohran Mamdani and the voters of New York.

Mamdani, in his first year as mayor, endorsed three candidates for Congress: Brad Lander, Claire Valdez, and Darializa Avila Chevalier. All three campaigned against the pro-Israel lobby, AIPAC. All three won.

Like Mamdani’s victory last fall, Tuesday’s elections represent a political earthquake – in the Democratic Party, in America’s biggest city, and in the hub of global capital.

As Zachary Donnini, head of data science at VoteHub, put it on Twitter, “The Democratic Tea Party is here. Tonight is shaping up to be one of the more consequential nights in recent Democratic politics, with Mamdani- and DSA-endorsed candidates reforming the party.”

Lander, initially a foe in the mayoral race, teamed up with Mamdani to take down Andrew Cuomo before gaining the new mayor’s endorsement in New York’s 10th congressional district. He easily defeated Democratic incumbent Rep. Dan Goldman, long a reliable Israel ally, saying: “I will be one of the Jewish members of Congress most willing to stand up for Palestinian human rights.”

The race wasn’t close.

But the other two elections might say even more about where the Democratic Party is headed.

While Lander is a progressive, Valdez and Avila Chevalier are Democratic socialists, endorsed by New York City Democratic Socialists of America. They are likely to be two of the furthest-left members of Congress. They might even make some current “Squad” members look like moderates!

Valdez defeated Antonio Reynoso, the Brooklyn Borough president handpicked by retiring Rep. Nydia Velázquez to replace her in New York’s 7th congressional district. An artist, union organizer, and New York State Assembly member, Valdez ran hard at Reynoso from the left, including over his refusal to name Israel’s genocide in Gaza. She won big.

Avila Chevalier, an Afro-Dominican and Muslim community organizer, ran against incumbent Rep. Adriano Espaillat, chair of the Congressional Hispanic Caucus, in New York’s 13th congressional district.

Espaillat and his allies significantly outspent her, and she faced heavy criticism for attending a pro-Palestinian rally on Oct. 8, 2023, a day after the Hamas-led attacks in Israel. Avila Chevalier was also panned for her support for prison abolition and defunding the police. To top it all off, she also faced a racist anti-Haitian smear campaign from Espaillat allies. In recent days, videos circulated on Twitter showing people harassing Avila Chevalier and screaming at her, with one man calling her a “Jew hater.”

In the end, the pro-Palestine Avila Chevalier won a close race, scoring a stunning upset. As a former Columbia University student encampment leader put it on Twitter: “Columbia, the school that once arrested Darializa for peacefully protesting, is now likely going to be represented by her in Congress.”

Avila Chevalier and Valdez both overcame a torrent of pro-Israel cash and dark money. And, perhaps as expected, Israel supporters are not handling it well.

Spare a thought for Fox’s favorite Democrat, John Fetterman. The Israel-first lawmaker streamed in to Sean Hannity’s show from his Democratic Senate office to complain Tuesday night.

“If you have contempt for Israel, you are also anti-American and you are anti-western civilization, you’re anti-capitalism and the American way of life,” said Fetterman.

Thankfully, Mamdani and Ms. Rachel pre-taped a message for him.


In other election news:

  • In New York’s 12th district, Micah Lasher defeated Alex Bores in one of the most expensive primary elections ever. The race

    becamea proxy battle over AI regulation; the deregulators won. Other beaten candidates included Jack Schlossberg, who’s JFK’s grandson, and George Conway, a prominent Never Trump Republican lawyer turned Democrat for the race.

  • In Maryland, Adrian Boafo won the primary to succeed longtime Dem House leader and Israel

    allySteny Hoyer. It’s a win for AIPAC, whose super PACspent$5.7 million boosting Boafo, earning him themoniker“AIPAC Adrian.”

  • In Utah, incumbent Dem Rep. Ben McAdams easily defeated challenger Nate Blouin, who was backed by Bernie Sanders.

  • In South Carolina, Trump’s third choice for governor, Alan Wilson, won the Republican nod.

  • Unique rebuke:Four Republicans joined Democrats topassa Senate resolution instructing Trump to end the war or seek congressional authorization. For thefirst timesince the War Powers Resolution passed in 1973, both chambers have formally rejected a president’s war. The Supreme Court hasneverhad to rule on what the move means.Observersagreed it was a major development. Trumpcalledit “poorly timed” and “meaningless.”
  • Nuclear stand-off:Iranian officialsdisputedTrump and JD Vance’s claims that Tehran had agreed to nuclear inspections. Trumpsaidif Iran had not agreed to inspections, he would have called off the negotiations. Talks are expected to continue next week.
  • Cash splash:Iranian Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibafsaidthe U.S. would release $12 billion in frozen assets. Trump said the money would buy U.S. produce. The PentagonaskedCongress for $80 billion to cover war costs.
  • Lebanon latest:New talks between Lebanon and Israelbeganin Washington, DC, yesterday. The UNsaidthat Israel has destroyed more than 11,000 residential buildings in the recent escalation. Thousands of others have been damaged.
  • Gas probe:Trumpsaidhe had ordered the Justice Department to investigate why gas prices aren’t lower. “Gasoline prices better start going down a lot faster than what I’m seeing!” he wrote on Truth Social.

In a report released yesterday, a UN commission of inquiry said Israeli authorities and security forces “have deliberately targeted Palestinian children resulting in genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes in the Gaza Strip and war crimes in the West Bank.”

It followed the commission’s conclusion last year that Israel committed genocide in Gaza. More than 73,000 people have been killed by Israel in Gaza since October 2023. More than 1,000 killings have occurred since the so-called ceasefire of October last year.

The UN Independent International Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory, including East Jerusalem, and Israel, said: “Deliberate targeting of children is one of the key elements establishing genocidal intent of the Israeli authorities and security forces to destroy the Palestinian group, in whole or in part, in Gaza.

“Severe physical and mental injuries, mass trauma, orphanhood, separation, disability, repeated displacements, starvation, and the collapse of education and healthcare have erased childhood and will continue to affect children in Gaza throughout their lives.”

The commission chair, Srinivasan Muralidhar, said: “The evidence shows that Palestinian children have been deliberately targeted and killed by the Israeli security forces. Even after the October 2025 ceasefire, children continue to be killed and seriously injured, with continued disregard by Israel for the ceasefire and for the protection owed to Palestinian children under international law.”

The report said Palestinian children were subject to arrest, torture, and other forms of mistreatment, including sexual violence. It said Israel targeted neonatal and maternity care centers, caused starvation, and destroyed orphanages and educational facilities.

Quoting a Gaza doctor, the report’s title was stark: “The essence of childhood has been destroyed.”

Israel’s Foreign Ministry, you’ll be shocked to hear, “utterly rejected” the report, which it called a “libelous sham” and “a propaganda piece as outrageous as” previous reports.

Muralidhar said, “Even if the bombs and guns fall silent in Gaza and West Bank, Palestinian children will not simply recover overnight. The destruction of their health, education and development is irreversible.”

Today, Israel killed another Palestinian child.

  • Poor reflection:Donald Trumprantedabout his Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool fiasco, heralding six arrests over supposed vandalism. TheNew York Timessaidthe government knew about renovation problems way earlier than it said. Also on the National Mall, FBI Director Kash Patel’s country singer girlfriend, Alexis Wilkins, wasconfirmedas a performer at Wednesday’s “Freedom 250” kick-off.
  • Weaponized justice:Anti-ICE protesters convicted on terrorism charges over a protest in Texasreceivedharsh sentences of at least 50 years in prison.
  • Poll flop:A Reuters/Ipsospollshowed Trump slumped to 34% job approval, the lowest of his second term. Only 24% thought his Iran war was worth the cost.
  • Chewing the fat:The White House wasmoved to denythat a mystery individualgrantedcompassionate access to an experimental obesity drug was Trump.
  • Bad call:In an election year shaped by anger over Israel’s Gaza genocide, GOP Maine Senator Susan Collinssaidher main aim as appropriations chair was to make permanent U.S. defunding of UNRWA, an agency that provides food and aid to Palestinians.
  • Deport in a storm:Two Trump-appointed judges on a DC appeals courtrevivedthe administration’s move to fast-track deportations of undocumented migrants.
  • Who knew:In a 6-3 ruling, Supreme Court conservatives, usually receptive to religious rights cases,saida Louisiana man whose dreadlocks were forcibly shaved by prison guards could not sue for violation of his Rastafarian beliefs.

Don’t tell Trump, but there’s another reflecting pool at the other end of the National Mall from the Lincoln Memorial, at the U.S. Capitol. A statue of which president stands guard there?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Drop Site Newsobtainedinternal documents showing Donald Trump’s “Board of Peace,” formed as part of the supposed Gaza ceasefire last October, is pushing to end any path to Palestinian statehood.

  • 🇮🇱

    Net loss:Former Israeli Prime Minister Naftali Bennettsaidhe initiated smuggling “tens of thousands of Starlink receptors” into Iran, seeking to help protesters, only for “the current incompetent Israeli government” to fumble the scheme.

  • 🇬🇧

    Prime delivery:Prime minister-in-waiting Andy Burnham met Keir Starmer, whoquitas Britain’s leader on Monday. Starmer told ministers he wanted an “orderly transition.” A UK-European Union summit set for July has been postponed.

  • 🇰🇪

    Ebola update:Kenya’s health ministersuspendedconstruction of a quarantine center for Americans exposed to the disease, but said “fear [it] could serve as a vehicle for Ebola importation into surrounding communities is scientifically unfounded.”


It would be hard to beat journalist Aaron Rupar’s comment with this clip of Donald Trump being foul about transgender athletes during his swing-seat speech in Pennsylvania, so we won’t try: “Checking in on the guy with the nuclear codes.”

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Did Elon Musk 'Sentence' 4 Million Kids 'to Death,' and Will He Sue You If You Say 'Yes'?
item.description: On this day in 1992, mafia boss John Gotti was given a life sentence on charges of racketeering and conspiracy to commit murder. Among New Yorkers nonetheless enamored of the “Teflon Don,” property developer Donald J. Trump admired, and later mimicked, Gotti’s pugnacious attitude in court. Good…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1992, mafia boss John Gotti was given a life sentence on charges of racketeering and conspiracy to commit murder. Among New Yorkers nonetheless enamored of the “Teflon Don,” property developer Donald J. Trump admired, and later mimicked, Gotti’s pugnacious attitude in court.

Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Tuesday wherever you are, a Happy Pride, and a Happy World Cup. I love the extended World Cup. I love that the World Cup is playing almost anywhere you go. I LOVE the World Cup. Who are you cheering for?

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ a man who just became a trillionaire is losing his mind on his own social media platform after being called out for possibly helping leave hundreds of thousands of people, many of them helpless children, to die. Plus, Susan Collins has thoughts about her role in removing abortion rights, about Graham Platner, and about U.S. support for Israel – all of them ridiculous. Let’s get to it.

Ten-year-old Peter Donde was suffering from H.I.V., contracted from his mother during childbirth. With the help of a U.S.-led aid program called PEPFAR, he was able to stay healthy.

Last February, the South Sudanese child died.

Elon Musk – who just became the world’s first trillionaire – is insisting he had nothing to do with it.

“There is not even a single dead child!” he says. “If there were, it would be worldwide headline news!”

In fact, several headlines, worldwide, have told us horror stories just like Donde’s. He is one of hundreds of thousands of people, including awful numbers of children, who have died since the Trump-Vance administration callously dismantled the United States Agency for International Development (USAID), the U.S. government’s international humanitarian and development agency, which administered programs like PEPFAR. The brutal cuts were led by Musk and his so-called Department of Government Efficiency, which, while tasked with cutting waste and making government more efficient, did not actually accomplish much of that at all.

On top of DOGE’s apparent failure to achieve its mandate was the active harm it caused, seen in the deaths of so many children like Donde, and with so many more destined to meet a similar fate if the Trump brutality continues. That widely reported reality was one Rep. Ro Khanna pointed out on Saturday, during an interview on the ‘I’ve Had It’ podcast.

“There needs to be accountability for Elon Musk,” Khanna said. “You know, they’re celebrating that he created 4,400 millionaires [with the SpaceX IPO that made him a trillionaire], but they don’t talk about the 4.5 million children around the world who he possibly sentenced to death by dismantling USAID. He needs to answer for that. He needs to be subpoenaed. He needs to face investigation. He needs to answer for what he did with DOGE. It’s not just, ‘Let’s move on.’”

On Sunday, Khanna tweeted out research from UCLA that found cuts to USAID could lead to more than 14 million additional deaths globally by 2030, including more than 4.5 million children under the age of five.

Musk shot back, saying: “Time to sue this liar.”

It’s a serious threat. Just last week, the world’s richest, most thin-skinned man took legal action against a German broadcaster, forcing it to retract its claim that he helped incite anti-migrant violence in Belfast, Northern Ireland. And Musk’s ire was first trained on Khanna this week due to another moment in the interview on ‘I’ve Had It’, when Khanna said that if Musk were taxed once at 5%, the U.S. could have universal childcare.

“He’s such an evil liar,” Musk responded.

Khanna replied: “Elon, how about just paying the 5% instead of hurling random insults?” He cited the UCLA study again, showing Musk the research about the possible deaths of millions of children.

Musk stayed furious, boosting several tweets attacking Khanna and suggesting the California congressman was trying to incite violence against him.

In one tweet, Musk boosted a tweet calling Khanna “the most active insider trader in Congress.” “‘Robber’ Khanna!” he added. In another, Musk said: “Liars and stock insider traders like Ro the Robber should be in prison!!”

In contrast to Khanna citing research to suggest Musk and DOGE may have culpability for the results of cuts to USAID, Musk cited no evidence for his own potentially defamatory claim that the Democratic congressman is an “insider trader” or “robber.”

Such petulance, remember, came from a man who wielded a chainsaw at CPAC and treated his whole ghastly DOGE project as some sort of hilarious caper, laughing and preening while ignoring laws duly passed, congressional spending mandates, and simple human decency, all so he could gigglingly feed thousands of lives into a whirring wood chipper.

Remember? At nearly 2am on Feb. 3, 2025, Musk tweeted: “We spent the weekend feeding USAID into the wood chipper. Could gone to some great parties. Did that instead.”

America’s very own oligarch claims to be a free speech champion, an “absolutist” even, and yet is now threatening to sue a fellow public figure for criticizing him – a contradiction not lost on Khanna, who, after weathering personal attacks and threats, challenged Musk to debate.

“Today, Elon Musk threatened to sue me, and he called on the Justice Department to put me in prison. That’s right: the world’s richest person has spent all day tweeting, going after me,” Khanna said in a video Monday. “Why? Because I cited an academic study that his DOGE cuts may lead to the deaths of millions of children overseas.

“You know, Elon, I thought you were a free speech guy. Why not debate me on these issues, instead of threatening lawfare?”

Khanna expounded in another appearance with Jennifer Welch of ‘I’ve Had It,’ saying: “I actually believe in debate. And I thought that was the whole MAGA ethos: we’re tough, we’re going to debate, we’re not just going to hide behind lawfare.”

Despite his flurry of tweets, Musk did not immediately respond to Khanna’s invitation.

But he has already helped Khanna’s (expected) 2028 presidential campaign with his online meltdown over the past 24 hours and his threat of legal action. After all, few Dems have publicly taken the fight to Musk in this way. Khanna shared with Zeteo a message to his fellow Democrats.

“For all the talk of standing up to the Trump administration, I am the only one who has actually landed a major blow with the Epstein files on Trump and taken on the first trillionaire by calling for a 5% tax and calling out the DOGE cuts,” he said. “Our base wants fighters who will take on extreme wealth and power and stand up for the working class.”

  • Nukes move:In Bürgenstock, Switzerland, Vice President JD VancesaidIran had agreed to let International Atomic Energy Agency inspectors back into the country. The Iranians, however,saidthey had made “no new commitments.”
  • Sanctions up:The Trump administration temporarilyliftedoil sanctions, like nuclear inspections, an element of the 2015 JCPOA nuclear deal, negotiated by Barack Obama, that Trump tore up in 2018 and has angrily repudiated since.
  • Talks trips:The U.S. said Secretary of State Marco Rubio would visit the UAE, Kuwait, and Bahrain this week. Mohammed Bagher Ghalibaf, the Iranian speaker,wentto Oman for talks about how to run the Strait of Hormuz.
  • Oil down:With traffic moving through the Strait, if atuneven rates, U.S. oil pricesdroppedmore than 2%.
  • Lebanon latest:Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchinoted“major progress” in ending fighting between Hezbollah and Israel in Lebanon, a key demand to end the wider war. As of Monday evening, a ceasefire appeared to be holding, though two were killed by Israeli gunfire on Tuesday. Since March 2, Israel has killed more than 4,000 people in Lebanon, mostly in the south.

Facing Fox softballs, Maine Republican Senator Susan Collins still managed to be characteristically cack-handed when defending her vote to confirm Brett Kavanaugh, one of the Supreme Court right-wingers who removed the right to abortion.

“I disagreed with the Supreme Court’s 6-3 decision overturning Roe v. Wade,” Collins said, “but the fact is that whether Justice Kavanaugh were confirmed or not, Roe v. Wade would have been overturned [6-3] and also I supported Elena Kagan, Sonia Sotomayor, and… Justice [Ketanji] Brown Jackson, who voted to sustain Roe v. Wade.”

Small problem: The court struck down Roe 5-4, Chief Justice John Roberts joining the liberals on that question. (Roberts attempted a compromise position, voting to uphold Mississippi’s 15-week abortion ban while keeping Roe.) Kavanaugh’s vote was decisive in overturning Roe, which means Collins’s vote in his knife-edge Senate confirmation was decisive too. And voters like abortion rights.

Fresh from being endorsed by Planned Parenthood, Graham Platner’s campaign posted: “Susan Collins is lying through her teeth.”

“Either Senator Collins is lying, or she doesn’t remember one of the most consequential Supreme Court decisions of modern history,” the candidate said in a video.

Despite scandals, including allegations from a former girlfriend who co-founded Ladies for Kavanaugh, Platner still leads Collins in the polls. Eager to help, therefore, Fox host Martha MacCallum chose not to dwell on the fact that Collins, who claims not to like Platner’s alleged treatment of women or alleged mockery of a fellow veteran, votes 94% of the time with a president who was found liable for sexual abuse and first came to office after taunting John McCain’s imprisonment.

Dodging a vaguely tricky question about her famous promise to serve two terms, a mere three six-year terms ago now, Collins lamented “rising antisemitism in our country that Graham Platner certainly is part of and has been… a consistent theme throughout his entire life.”

As proof, Collins said Platner “accuses Israel of committing genocide.” Which it does. Platner, she said, “praises Hamas for its tactics in killing Israeli soldiers,” a reference to a 2014 Reddit comment about military tactics. Collins also said: “It’s important to recognize that AIPAC is made up of Americans who care deeply about our relationship with Israel. Platner makes it sound like this is some sort of evil foreign influence, and that is absolutely wrong.”

Alas for Collins, she of “I am pro-Israel” infamy, voters increasingly do not agree.

  • Mall balls:Trumpranted onabout his $16.5 million fiasco at the Lincoln Memorial reflecting pool, home to peeling blue paint and rampant green algae, as well as the appearance of an “86 47” anti-Trump meme on the grass of the National Mall. Trump claims vandalism. Five people werereportedlyarrested. Trumpsaidhe was preparing to sue ABC for its reporting on the pool story.
  • Court press:With the Supreme Court due to hand down rulings on Tuesday, expertstoldtheWashington Postthey expected Trump to lose on scrapping birthright citizenship and removing a Federal Reserve governor, but succeed in his bid to be allowed to fire heads of independent government agencies.
  • Fed giant dies:Alan Greenspan, who led the Fed for more than 18 years,diedaged 100. TheNew York Timescalledhim “a polarizing force in shaping market-friendly policies.” Considering his impact in areas such as home ownership among underserved minorities, historian Rick Perlsteinsaid: “May [he] forever rot in the sub-, sub-, subprime depths of hell.”
  • Texas scold ’em:Republican Senator John Cornyn, beaten by Trump-endorsed Ken Paxton,toldSemafor he was “very worried about the midterms,” would not help his conqueror, and was now “free to disagree” with the president.
  • Last Walz:A federal judgeblockeda Trump DOJ attempt to subpoena Minnesota Governor Tim Walz in an immigration investigation, calling it “blatantly unlawful.” Another judgeblockeda Trump attempt to purge non-citizens from voter rolls.
  • Lobby hires:Sinister tech giant Palantir is scooping up Democrats, Sludgereported, employing former Alaska Senator Mark Begich and a former chief of staff to House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries of New York.

In 1990, running for the Texas Supreme Court, John Cornyn falsely represented himself as a graduate of which ancient university?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:An 18-year-old girl on her way to take her high school exams was killed and others were wounded by an Israeli airstrike in the Rimal neighborhood, Palestinian news agency Wafasaid. More than 73,000 people have been killed by Israel in Gaza since October 2023.
  • 🇵🇸 West Bank killings:Israeli troops shot dead two teens near Karmei Tzur, an illegal Israeli settlement, Palestinian officialssaid.

  • 🇬🇧

    Next please:After Prime Minister Keir Starmer resigned andannounceda timetable for his departure, former Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham, the “King of the North,” arrived in London, set to become Britain’s seventh leader since 2016.
  • 🇨🇺🇲🇽 Havana help:Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaumsaidher country would soon restart oil shipments to Cuba, as the island suffers under a U.S. embargo, facing challenges including severe power outages, water shortages, and spoiled food.
  • 🇨🇩 Ebola update:The death toll in DR Congo rose to 254 from 1,003 cases, officials said. At least 365 people were reported to be in hospitals or in isolation.


In the Oval Office, showing respect entirely out of whack with the subject, CBS correspondent Ed O’Keefe patiently asked Donald Trump about his claim that the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool, on which he has spent $16.5 million, is peeling and full of algae because it has been “vandalized” by naughty left-wingers. Well, that and because “Barack Hussein Obama.”

And lo, after five minutes of this insanity, Americans were left to ask themselves once again, “Really? We’re still doing this?”

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Mamdani Allies Face Off Against AIPAC, Dark Money in New York
item.description: Earlier this month, during Game 1 of the NBA Finals between the New York Knicks and the San Antonio Spurs, Mayor Zohran Mamdani turned up in a Finals-themed ad alongside three candidates he’s backing for Congress: Brad Lander, Claire Valdez, and Darializa Avila Chevalier. “This is the team. This is…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Earlier this month, during Game 1 of the NBA Finals between the New York Knicks and the San Antonio Spurs, Mayor Zohran Mamdani turned up in a Finals-themed ad alongside three candidates he’s backing for Congress: Brad Lander, Claire Valdez, and Darializa Avila Chevalier.

“This is the team. This is our year,” Mamdani said in the spot, funded by all three campaigns.

With that ad, Tuesday’s primaries became a test of how much the mayor’s endorsement is worth a year into his first term, and whether he can build a political machine. In recent weeks, pro-Israel interests and dark-money groups have poured cash into the race, to try to stymie Mamdani’s ascent as a kingmaker in New York.

Lander and Avila Chevalier are challenging Democratic incumbents, Reps. Dan Goldman and Adriano Espaillat. Valdez is running for the open seat of retiring Democratic Rep. Nydia Velázquez, against her chosen successor, Antonio Reynoso.

When it comes to the details, Mamdani’s “team” isn’t quite as unified as the ad might suggest. Of the three, only Valdez and Avila Chevalier have endorsed each other. (Like Mamdani, Valdez and Avila Chevalier are Democratic socialists, endorsed by the New York City Democratic Socialists of America.)

Lander – the former New York City Comptroller and a Mamdani ally from the mayoral race last year – hasn’t formally endorsed either candidate with whom he shared a court. He also cut a separate video with Reynoso, a longtime ally from their days on the City Council.

Still, if Mamdani’s candidates win on Tuesday, Mamdani won’t only be seen as a uniquely strong communicator or telegenic presence – but as a powerbroker.

Here’s who’s trying to stop him:

In New York’s 13th congressional district, Mandani’s preferred candidate, Avila Chevalier, has been significantly outspent by incumbent Rep. Espaillat, who chairs the Congressional Hispanic Caucus. Outside spending in the race is a similar story, with pro-Espaillat groups spending $7 million, more than double the $3 million spent by Avila Chevalier’s allies.

According to Drop Site News, Espaillat’s campaign has received a surge of donations from pro-Israel donors. And thanks to new campaign finance disclosures made over the weekend, we know some pro-Espaillat spending has come from AIPAC and other pro-Israel forces.

Espaillat has seen $2.9 million in support from Bold America. That super PAC has received $650,000 from United Democracy Project, an outside group bankrolled by AIPAC. United Democracy Project appears to have been Bold America’s first donor.

United for Progress, a super PAC that’s spent $412,000 backing Espaillat, won’t have to disclose its donors before the election because it only started spending on the race on June 10, less than two weeks before the primary. However, the pro-Israel group DMFI PAC – better known as Democratic Majority for Israel – disclosed over the weekend that it’s donated at least $200,000 to United for Progress.

Espaillat has also benefited from $1.9 million in dark-money spending from Progressive Unity Fund. While the Progressive Unity Fund will eventually have to disclose its donors, it won’t have to do so until after the primary, because it too started spending on the race on June 10.

Avila Chevalier has seen $1.5 million in support from the progressive super PAC Justice Democrats PAC. She’s also benefited from $1.3 million in spending by American Priorities, a lefty super PAC conceived as a counterweight to AIPAC.

The race could be close. Earlier this month, a poll from Justice Democrats found Avila Chevalier with a 39%-35% lead over Espaillat.

In New York’s 7th district, Mamdani is backing Valdez, a union organizer and artist, over Reynoso, the Brooklyn Borough President. The two are competing for the chance to succeed retiring Rep. Velázquez, who has endorsed Reynoso.

This primary race doesn’t fall neatly along ideological lines. The New York City chapter of DSA is backing Valdez, while Reynoso has been endorsed by the progressive New York Working Families Party.

Valdez has a fundraising advantage, having raised $1.3 million to Reynoso’s $883,000. And Valdez has benefited from $1.4 million in outside spending, compared to $1 million for Reynoso.

Over the past 10 days, however, there’s been a surge of outside support for Reynoso, with a group called Real Fight NYC spending $700,000. Real Fight NYC formed late in the race, on June 10, allowing it to avoid disclosing its donors before the election – an increasingly common tactic. The group began spending on Reynoso’s behalf two days later.

The group has reportedly received nearly $200,000 from the American Federation of Teachers, a major teachers’ union, but most of its funding remains dark. According to Jewish Insider, former NYC Comptroller Scott Stringer is helping direct the group’s activities. Last year, Stringer ran against Mamdani in the Democratic primary for mayor and finished in fifth place.

Valdez has seen $456,000 in support from the progressive super PAC American Priorities; $371,000 from Justice Democrats PAC; $357,000 from a pro-Mamdani PAC called New Yorkers for Lower Costs; and $175,000 from Leaders We Deserve, an outside group founded by gun-control activist David Hogg.

In New York’s 10th district, Mamdani is backing Lander, the former New York City comptroller, against Rep. Goldman, an heir to the Levi Strauss fortune who has spent two terms as one of the richest members of Congress. Public polling has shown Lander up by more than 20 points.

Back in 2025, Mamdani and Lander cross-endorsed each other’s mayoral campaigns under New York City’s primary ranked-choice voting system, to stop Andrew Cuomo’s bid for mayor. After Mamdani’s general election victory, Lander was widely expected to join the Mamdani administration – possibly as first deputy mayor – before opting to challenge Goldman.

Goldman refused to endorse Mamdani even in the general election, when Mamdani was the Democratic nominee, saying he had asked Mamdani to condemn anti-Israel violence but “frankly haven’t really seen him do much on that” and that “many in the Jewish community do not feel safe right now.” Goldman’s district, however, ended up breaking heavily for Mamdani, who carried it with roughly 60% of the vote in November.

As in NY-13, pro-Israel money has flowed in behind the incumbent. Goldman has DMFI PAC’s endorsement and has taken $1.7 million from pro-Israel groups and donors over his career. New Yorkers Fighting Back, a super PAC formed by Goldman allies, has spent roughly $316,000; it raised $100,000 from DMFI PAC in May and $150,000 from Goldman’s uncle. Goldman has significantly outspent Lander while pouring at least $1 million of his own wealth into the race.

Lander’s outside support has been far smaller and progressive in origin, with the Working Families Party PAC spending roughly $168,000 on his behalf and smaller sums from progressive groups, Indivisible Action and MoveOn.

Zeteo’s Andrew McDonald contributed to this story.
