item.title: From Exploding Cats to Mega-Prisons: Meet Colombia’s Next President
item.description: On Aug. 7, Abelardo de la Espriella will take office as Colombia’s newest president, in a major rightwards shift from Gustavo Petro’s outgoing leftist administration. As Colombia continues to grapple with paramilitary violence , De la Espriella, who has dubbed himself “The Tiger,” has proposed a…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On Aug. 7, Abelardo de la Espriella will take office as Colombia’s newest president, in a major rightwards shift from Gustavo Petro’s outgoing leftist administration.

As Colombia continues to grapple with paramilitary violence, De la Espriella, who has dubbed himself “The Tiger,” has proposed a hard-line approach backed by the likes of Javier Milei and Donald Trump.

So as another controversial and outspoken right-winger takes power in South America, here are 11 things you need to know about Abelardo de la Espriella.

Before launching his presidential campaign, De la Espriella was a lawyer and businessman. He will be the first modern Colombian president elected with no prior political experience.

Often seen wearing tailored suits, fedoras, and fancy watches, De la Espriella has hinged his public persona on his millionaire status. He self-produced a covers album, including a version of Frank Sinatra’s ‘My Way’ with an accompanying video in which he was shown eating sushi on a private jet. For those interested in living the De la Espriella lifestyle, his website sells everything from his own rum and wine brands to luxury suit jackets.

Yes, you read that right. On a television show, De la Espriella confessed that when young, he tied firecrackers to cats to try to make them fly, but they ended up exploding. He first said he was an “innocent” child at the time, then walked the story back, saying it was a bad joke. Sure.

In the 2000s, De la Espriella created a so-called “peace” foundation which platformed paramilitary leaders at universities across Colombia. In 2011, the country’s Supreme Court said the foundation did not, in fact, promote peace and ordered an investigation following allegations of conspiracy and money laundering. The case was later dropped by the Attorney General’s office, then led by a friend of De la Espriella.

De la Espriella came to prominence defending paramilitaries, high-profile businessmen, and politicians accused of corruption including Alex Saab, who helped launder hundreds millions of dollars for Venezuela’s now-ousted president, Nicolás Maduro. (De la Espriella has since distanced himself from Maduro’s ally and praised Trump administration policies on Venezuela.) De la Espriella also represented former Colombian president Álvaro Uribe, who has been implicated in paramilitary violence.

According to Colombia’s Foundation for Press Freedom, between 2008 and 2019 De la Espriella filed 109 defamation or slander cases against journalists. Many of his critics view this track record as a dire warning for the freedom of the press under his presidency.

On a popular radio show, De la Espriella said women would vote for him because of the size of his genitals, then showed a female reporter a photo of himself in sweatpants, asking her to zoom in on his groin. The reporter said she felt “violated, harassed, and disgusted.” A court ordered him to publicly apologize.

De la Espriella publicly vowed to “disembowel” the political left, arguing it is a “plague” to be “eradicated.” After intense backlash, he walked the statement back, arguing it was a figure of speech and that he would rule according to the law.

Following in the footsteps of El Salvador’s Nayib Bukele, De la Espriella has promised to build 10 mega-prisons and kill criminals like “rats” and “cockroaches.” Such prisons are notorious for human rights violations, from inhumane conditions including extreme overcrowding to torture and collective punishment.

In a stark shift from the Petro government’s peace agreements with guerillas, De la Espriella has promised to unleash violence. He vowed to bomb narco-terrorist camps and has suggested establishing military alliances with the U.S. and Israel to help take such groups down.

De la Espriella has vowed to restore diplomatic ties, breaking from yet another Petro-era policy. Despite Israel’s continuing genocide in Gaza and illegal bombardment of Lebanon, De la Espriella has campaigned on strengthening the relationship, suggesting opening a Colombian embassy in Jerusalem, a step towards legitimizing the city as the capital of Israel.

Zeteo’s Melanie Riehl contributed to this piece.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Has a Far-Right, Islamophobic Cult Leader Been 'Controlling' Former Intel Chief Tulsi Gabbard All These Years?
item.description: 🚨 BREAKING: British Prime Minister Keir Starmer resigned today, saying he would stay till September, as Manchester mayor turned Labour Party MP Andy Burnham seemed set to take over. Donald Trump honored Starmer with a rude post. Good morning, and happy Monday to all. Martin here, having conquered…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
🚨 BREAKING: British Prime Minister Keir Starmer resigned today, saying he would stay till September, as Manchester mayor turned Labour Party MP Andy Burnham seemed set to take over. Donald Trump honored Starmer with a rude post.

Good morning, and happy Monday to all. Martin here, having conquered the urge to go down the Lincoln Memorial reflecting pool and get myself a curl of blue paint, peeled from the bottom thanks to algae or hydrogen peroxide or both, to frame over my desk as a memento of Donald Trump’s Washington. Turns out I might’ve been arrested, held for five hours, and charged, as a former Olympic canoeist was on Friday, for simply touching such paint. Really.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we consider another bizarre tale of DC under The Donald: an extraordinary Washington Post report about how Tulsi Gabbard, director of national intelligence until last week, relied heavily on advice and instruction from a cult leader in her native Hawaii. We also look to Switzerland, where JD Vance was left flailing by yet more Trumpian insanity over war or peace with Iran. Here, as they say, we go.

The Washington Post may be reduced under Jeff Bezos, who we now know trashed his staff while sucking up to Donald Trump, but it can still produce remarkable work. Sunday brought an epic: “Tulsi Gabbard, her guru and the mysterious messages that helped shape her political career.”

Reporter Jon Swaine tells us that in a bizarre career that has swerved from social conservatism in Hawaii to progressivism in Congress, from presidential outsider to alleged “ Russian asset” to MAGA turncoat and

Read that again: the official who, until last week, oversaw all U.S. intelligence work, who inserted herself in efforts to boost fake allegations of election fraud and perhaps rig the midterms, took instruction and direction from a Hindu cult leader.

Now imagine that the cult leader did not lead a “breakaway Hare Krishna group.” Imagine the cult leader was Muslim.

Would the story have stayed untold so long? Would what was known have been brushed aside by Trump’s Republicans when Gabbard was confirmed?

It seems sure it would not.

According to Swaine, a former Gabbard aide contacted him to say she worked for Chris Butler, “the eccentric religious leader Gabbard once described as her guru,” head of Science of Identity Foundation, a group based in Hawaii.

Butler, 78, and Gabbard, 45, have denied a mentor-pupil relationship. But the source, Rebecca Saltzburg, provided hundreds of emails.

“Their content was extraordinary,” Swaine wrote. “Dozens… appeared to document directives and advice for Gabbard from her time in Congress. Some contained instructions on what legislation she should propose, which policies she should embrace and how she should conduct herself on television… A memo about a proposal to partition war-torn Iraq into three states quoted an unnamed person as saying it was ‘time for TG to come up with this idea.’

“Some of the language was harshly critical. One memo… from January 2015, contained a derisive assessment of a statement Gabbard was to give in response to President Barack Obama’s annual address to Congress. ‘In the first place, nobody gives a shit what you think about his State of the Union speech, unless you’re going to say something of interest,’ the memo quoted someone as saying. ‘You’re not even trying. You’ve become really intellectually lazy.’”

“In another, Gabbard was described as ‘chickenshit’ and ‘mealymouthed’ for her comments on a policy proposal.”

Saltzburg said the emails were from Butler. (His deputy claimed authorship, then went quiet when Swaine questioned the claim.) Butler and Gabbard declined to be interviewed. A PR agent for SIF decried “Hinduphobia, anti-Hindu religious bigotry,” claiming: “When a Hindu public figure has a spiritual teacher or shares views with a Hindu religious figure, that alone is somehow evidence of sinister control.” Gabbard’s chief of staff repeated such charges. Swaine’s source was attacked, previous claims against the group cited.

But Swaine found “unmistakable parallels” between the memos and Gabbard’s House record, as well as messages apparently from Gabbard approving of the directions.

Swaine described Butler’s own record: “His teachings belonged to no political tribe. He inveighed against Muslims, homosexuality, gun control and public schools, but also promoted environmentalism and anti-capitalism.” Gabbard’s political debut was as a “forceful” opponent of same-sex marriage. He noted Gabbard’s parents followed Butler. He described pushback when he and a colleague went to Hawaii to report.

Among the emails was “a 173-page dossier from 2014 titled ‘TG Issues.’ It compiled advice for Gabbard on dozens of topics… peppered with imperatives. ‘Start introducing bills,’ it said on one issue. ‘Need to get on it and hit hard. Stop being weak,’ it said on another.”

Memos even directed Gabbard on a signature and controversial issue, her view that the U.S. should not try to oust Bashar al-Assad, then president of war-torn Syria.

“As I examined the document,” Swaine wrote, “eight months after Gabbard’s confirmation as director of national intelligence, I found it striking that such deep suspicion of U.S. intelligence appeared to have been fed to someone who would later coordinate the CIA, the National Security Agency and more than a dozen similar agencies.”

The memos showed Gabbard being told what to say and how to look, including in the 2016 presidential election, in which she backed Bernie Sanders, voted for Hillary Clinton, and then, after Trump won, visited him at Trump Tower.

The Post also reported an effort to inflate support for Gabbard, using fake social media accounts and comments, “overseen by a person… [sometimes referred to] as ‘S.’ According to Saltzburg and four other former members, ‘S’ is a shorthand for Butler, who is known as Srila Prabhupada and Siddhaswarupananda Paramahamsa. (Often, references to Butler also include the title ‘Jagad Guru,’ which means spiritual teacher.)”

Again, ask yourself if Gabbard could have risen so high if such a “guru” had been a Muslim, such terminology redolent of Islam.

Then ask yourself a further question: ultimately, what does Gabbard’s career tell us about how we are governed now? A Fox weekend host with an alleged drinking problem is secretary of defense. An anti-vaccine conspiracy theorist runs health and human services. And a dedicated cult member became director of national intelligence, despite the association being long known.

This is what a kakistocracy looks like.

And Gabbard’s extreme behavior in particular, highlighted by Swaine and the Post, shows us it’s not just a matter of Trump being mad as a hatter. He’s dragged us all through the looking glass now.

  • Slow start:Iranian and U.S. negotiatorsmetin Switzerland on Sunday and Monday. Lead U.S. negotiator JD Vance was undercut by his own boss, as Donald Trump renewed threats to Tehran and Iransuspendedtalks in return. Today, Vancehailed“great progress” in the talks.
  • Crazy talk:Speaking to Fox, Trumpsaidthat if Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz, as it said it would on Saturday, “We’ll take over the rest of the country” and “I’ll blow the shit out of them” because “I can do whatever I want.”
  • Hard talk:Iranian President Masoud PezeshkiansaidTehran would commit to not building a nuclear bomb but would not give up its right to uranium enrichment. The U.S. would be “forced to accept” this, he said.
  • Strait threat:Iran hawk and Trump lickspittle Senator Lindsey Grahamtold CBS: “If this deal fails, President Trump is going to take the Strait of Hormuz over by force. The United States will control the Strait of Hormuz. We’ll charge a fee for all those who go through, to pay for the operation.”
  • Costs rise:Oil prices were upon Sunday. One expertpredicted“a spike like you never saw before” if agreement is not reached, with reserves perilously low.
  • Israel attacks:After a day of carnage in Lebanon on Friday, Israeli airstrikes and droneskilleddozens on Saturday. The cessation of such attacks is a key point in the memorandum of understanding between the U.S. and Iran.
  • Lebanon latest:Trump voiced his frustration with Israel but alsopostedthat if Iran did not stop Hezbollah “causing trouble,” he would “hit Iran very hard again, just like we did last week, only harder.”

At the Bürgenstock resort near Lake Lucerne in Switzerland, Vice President JD Vance appeared to suffer an embarrassing snub at the hands of Iranian officials.

Video from a scheduled press session on Sunday showed Vance waiting with Trump envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner while Iranian officials, including Foreign Minister Abbas Arahgchi, entered the room. After briefly conferring with Pakistani mediators, the Iranians left. Vance was then shown talking to the Pakistanis, Field Marshal Asif Munir and Prime Minister Muhammad Shehbaz Sharif, and apparently being ignored by Qatari Prime Minister Sheikh Mohammed bin Abdulrahman Al Thani, another key figure in talks to end the war. Vance, the Pakistanis, and Al Thani then spoke to reporters.

As the footage spread online, Iranian state-controlled media said the Iranian delegation rejected a proposed handshake and photo op, “underscor[ing] Tehran’s preference for substantive negotiations over symbolic gestures, avoiding imagery that could be used to suggest diplomatic progress regardless of the talks’ actual outcome.”

In response, the Trump-friendly New York Post quoted a U.S. official as saying Iran had agreed to the press availability, brought state media with them, but “left the meeting upon seeing the volume of press in attendance and then distributed an inaccurate account of the situation.”

Either way, it was inarguable that Vance, the U.S. talks lead widely seen to be the recipient of Donald Trump's what’s technically known as a “shit sandwich,” did not look even close to comfortable in such high-stakes surroundings.

  • Pool fool:Trumpsaid“sick, deranged people” vandalized the Lincoln Memorial reflecting pool, on which he has spent$16 millionfor a flaking pond full of algae he said might have to be drained. A man held after touching a bit of peeling blue paintdescribedhis ordeal. A duckling wasreporteddead in the pool.
  • Qatar Force One:Trump unveiled the Gulf nation’s gift, a plane “transformed into a flying White House at a level of luxury that nobody’s ever seen before,” in thewordsof the leader of a nation struggling with a cost-of-living crisis.
  • Pardon probe:Trump appointees quashed a criminal investigation into possible payments surrounding the president’s grant of clemency to a convicted fraudster, theNew York Timesreported.
  • Mystery woman:For Father’s Day, Trump, 80,posteda picture of a “great daughter,” adding: “My honor!!! President DJT.” It wasn’t either of his daughters.
  • Funds fuss:Arizona Senator Ruben Gallego “repeatedly used campaign cash to fund luxury outings with his wife and to care for his children,”Politicosaid. The Democrat, widely seen as a potential presidential contender, denied wrongdoing.
  • Maine man:Democratic U.S. Senate candidate Graham Platner willreceivean endorsement from Planned Parenthood, a week after incumbent Republican Susan Collins said she did not regret her decisive vote to confirm Brett Kavanaugh, one of the justices who took away the right to abortion in 2022.

In 2001, Trump donor turned no-bid reflecting pool “cleaner” John J. Cafaro pleaded guilty to conspiring to bribe which infamous Democrat, the fifth man ever expelled from Congress?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:At least nine people, including two women, a child, and a journalist, werekilledby Israel over the weekend. Reporter Ahmed Wishah, whose brother was recently killed, wasrememberedas “kind and principled” while his employer, Al Jazeera, called his killing “a heinous crime.” Nearly 300 Palestinian journalists have been killed since October 2023.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    Gas crisis:Ukrainian attackspromptedRussian-held Crimea to suspend gasoline sales to civilians. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said: “Russia understands only strength, and our long-range strength is working for peace.”
  • 🇨🇴 Colombia decides:Trump-endorsed far-right candidate Abelardo de la Espriellawon a narrow victoryin the presidential election over leftist Iván Cepeda. During the campaign, Espriella said he would “disembowel” the left. Cepeda did not immediately concede.


“Vance sent a warning to the Jews.” I cannot imagine a CNN or MS Now saying something similar, and if they did, their careers would be over. But antisemitism has been normalized and mainstreamed on the right, which means Fox host Jesse Watters will get away with this vile and sweeping rhetoric

  • Asked on NBC’s ‘Meet the Press’ if he agreed with

    strategist James CarvillethatDonald Trumpdeserved credit for ending his own disastrous war with Iran,Democratic Senator Cory Booker…disagreed: “That’s like… an arsonist starting a fire and getting credit for running out of the burning building. This president has led this nation into a disaster. We have surrendered our power. We have capitulated to the enemy. And they now are mocking us.”
  • UN Ambassador Mike Waltztold CBS’s ‘Face the Nation’thatTrumphad achieved “a step forward no other administration has ever been able to do, and that’s have direct talks” with Iranian leaders. Which begged the question, how does Waltz think aides toBarack Obamahashed out the 2015 nuclear deal Trump gleefully tore up? By semaphore?
  • Senator Mike Leetold Fox thatTrump’sbeloved SAVE Act, which seeks to cripple Democrats at the polls via voter ID reform, can pass the Senate despite being 10 votes short. “There are a couple ways to get there,” Leeinsisted. “One would be nuking the filibuster… that appears not likely to happen. But the other way is to exhaust the other side, to continue to debate the bill until it passes.”
  • Monday, June 22:The Senate returns (the House follows Tuesday) to try to work out what to do about the role of director of national intelligence, Donald Trump having scrapped a hearing for nominee Jay Clayton, leavingMAGA warrior Bill Pultein place as acting DNI, and Democrats refusing to re-authorize a key foreign surveillance authority.
  • Tuesday, June 23:Primaries are held in New York, Maryland, and Utah, with runoffs in South Carolina. In New York, progressives endorsed by Mayor Zohran Mamdani will look to defeat U.S. House incumbents Adriano Espaillat and Dan Goldman, while high-profile fields compete for two open seats.
  • Wednesday, June 24:Trump will open his “Freedom 250” celebration by headlining a rally on the National Mall. It was meant to be a concert, but acts, includingnoted fakersMilli Vanilli, balked at being associated with the president. Also on Wednesday, both pro-choice and anti-abortion rights campaigners will mark the fourth anniversary of the U.S. Supreme Court overturningRoe v. Wade, the 1973 ruling that previously guaranteed abortion rights.
  • Thursday, June 25:Freedom 250’s “Great American State Fair” begins on the Mall. Some states not participating havecited concernsabout the partisan nature of an event that will kick off with a celebration of “The American Canon,”promisingto “enshrin[e] the architecture, infrastructure, and culture that forged our nation into a global force.”

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

  • Crazy talk:Speaking to Fox, Trumpsaidthat if Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz, as it said it would on Saturday, “We’ll take over the rest of the country” and “I’ll blow the shit out of them” because “I can do whatever I want.”
  • Hard talk:Iranian President Masoud PezeshkiansaidTehran would commit to not building a nuclear bomb but would not give up its right to uranium enrichment. The U.S. would be “forced to accept” this, he said.
  • Strait threat:Iran hawk and Trump lickspittle Senator Lindsey Grahamtold CBS: “If this deal fails, President Trump is going to take the Strait of Hormuz over by force. The United States will control the Strait of Hormuz. We’ll charge a fee for all those who go through, to pay for the operation.”
  • Costs rise:Oil prices were upon Sunday. One expertpredicted“a spike like you never saw before” if agreement is not reached, with reserves perilously low.
  • Israel attacks:After a day of carnage in Lebanon on Friday, Israeli airstrikes and droneskilleddozens on Saturday. The cessation of such attacks is a key point in the memorandum of understanding between the U.S. and Iran.
  • Lebanon latest:Trump voiced his frustration with Israel but alsopostedthat if Iran did not stop Hezbollah “causing trouble,” he would “hit Iran very hard again, just like we did last week, only harder.”

At the Bürgenstock resort near Lake Lucerne in Switzerland, Vice President JD Vance appeared to suffer an embarrassing snub at the hands of Iranian officials.

Video from a scheduled press session on Sunday showed Vance waiting with Trump envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner while Iranian officials, including Foreign Minister Abbas Arahgchi, entered the room. After briefly conferring with Pakistani mediators, the Iranians left. Vance was then shown talking to the Pakistanis, Field Marshal Asif Munir and Prime Minister Muhammad Shehbaz Sharif, and apparently being ignored by Qatari Prime Minister Sheikh Mohammed bin Abdulrahman Al Thani, another key figure in talks to end the war. Vance, the Pakistanis, and Al Thani then spoke to reporters.

As the footage spread online, Iranian state-controlled media said the Iranian delegation rejected a proposed handshake and photo op, “underscor[ing] Tehran’s preference for substantive negotiations over symbolic gestures, avoiding imagery that could be used to suggest diplomatic progress regardless of the talks’ actual outcome.”

In response, the Trump-friendly New York Post quoted a U.S. official as saying Iran had agreed to the press availability, brought state media with them, but “left the meeting upon seeing the volume of press in attendance and then distributed an inaccurate account of the situation.”

Either way, it was inarguable that Vance, the U.S. talks lead widely seen to be the recipient of Donald Trump's what’s technically known as a “shit sandwich,” did not look even close to comfortable in such high-stakes surroundings.

  • Pool fool:Trumpsaid“sick, deranged people” vandalized the Lincoln Memorial reflecting pool, on which he has spent$16 millionfor a flaking pond full of algae he said might have to be drained. A man held after touching a bit of peeling blue paintdescribedhis ordeal. A duckling wasreporteddead in the pool.
  • Qatar Force One:Trump unveiled the Gulf nation’s gift, a plane “transformed into a flying White House at a level of luxury that nobody’s ever seen before,” in thewordsof the leader of a nation struggling with a cost-of-living crisis.
  • Pardon probe:Trump appointees quashed a criminal investigation into possible payments surrounding the president’s grant of clemency to a convicted fraudster, theNew York Timesreported.
  • Mystery woman:For Father’s Day, Trump, 80,posteda picture of a “great daughter,” adding: “My honor!!! President DJT.” It wasn’t either of his daughters.
  • Funds fuss:Arizona Senator Ruben Gallego “repeatedly used campaign cash to fund luxury outings with his wife and to care for his children,”Politicosaid. The Democrat, widely seen as a potential presidential contender, denied wrongdoing.
  • Maine man:Democratic U.S. Senate candidate Graham Platner willreceivean endorsement from Planned Parenthood, a week after incumbent Republican Susan Collins said she did not regret her decisive vote to confirm Brett Kavanaugh, one of the justices who took away the right to abortion in 2022.

In 2001, Trump donor turned no-bid reflecting pool “cleaner” John J. Cafaro pleaded guilty to conspiring to bribe which infamous Democrat, the fifth man ever expelled from Congress?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:At least nine people, including two women, a child, and a journalist, werekilledby Israel over the weekend. Reporter Ahmed Wishah, whose brother was recently killed, wasrememberedas “kind and principled” while his employer, Al Jazeera, called his killing “a heinous crime.” Nearly 300 Palestinian journalists have been killed since October 2023.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    Gas crisis:Ukrainian attackspromptedRussian-held Crimea to suspend gasoline sales to civilians. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said: “Russia understands only strength, and our long-range strength is working for peace.”
  • 🇨🇴 Colombia decides:Trump-endorsed far-right candidate Abelardo de la Espriellawon a narrow victoryin the presidential election over leftist Iván Cepeda. During the campaign, Espriella said he would “disembowel” the left. Cepeda did not immediately concede.


“Vance sent a warning to the Jews.” I cannot imagine a CNN or MS Now saying something similar, and if they did, their careers would be over. But antisemitism has been normalized and mainstreamed on the right, which means Fox host Jesse Watters will get away with this vile and sweeping rhetoric

  • Asked on NBC’s ‘Meet the Press’ if he agreed with

    strategist James CarvillethatDonald Trumpdeserved credit for ending his own disastrous war with Iran,Democratic Senator Cory Booker…disagreed: “That’s like… an arsonist starting a fire and getting credit for running out of the burning building. This president has led this nation into a disaster. We have surrendered our power. We have capitulated to the enemy. And they now are mocking us.”
  • UN Ambassador Mike Waltztold CBS’s ‘Face the Nation’thatTrumphad achieved “a step forward no other administration has ever been able to do, and that’s have direct talks” with Iranian leaders. Which begged the question, how does Waltz think aides toBarack Obamahashed out the 2015 nuclear deal Trump gleefully tore up? By semaphore?
  • Senator Mike Leetold Fox thatTrump’sbeloved SAVE Act, which seeks to cripple Democrats at the polls via voter ID reform, can pass the Senate despite being 10 votes short. “There are a couple ways to get there,” Leeinsisted. “One would be nuking the filibuster… that appears not likely to happen. But the other way is to exhaust the other side, to continue to debate the bill until it passes.”
  • Monday, June 22:The Senate returns (the House follows Tuesday) to try to work out what to do about the role of director of national intelligence, Donald Trump having scrapped a hearing for nominee Jay Clayton, leavingMAGA warrior Bill Pultein place as acting DNI, and Democrats refusing to re-authorize a key foreign surveillance authority.
  • Tuesday, June 23:Primaries are held in New York, Maryland, and Utah, with runoffs in South Carolina. In New York, progressives endorsed by Mayor Zohran Mamdani will look to defeat U.S. House incumbents Adriano Espaillat and Dan Goldman, while high-profile fields compete for two open seats.
  • Wednesday, June 24:Trump will open his “Freedom 250” celebration by headlining a rally on the National Mall. It was meant to be a concert, but acts, includingnoted fakersMilli Vanilli, balked at being associated with the president. Also on Wednesday, both pro-choice and anti-abortion rights campaigners will mark the fourth anniversary of the U.S. Supreme Court overturningRoe v. Wade, the 1973 ruling that previously guaranteed abortion rights.
  • Thursday, June 25:Freedom 250’s “Great American State Fair” begins on the Mall. Some states not participating havecited concernsabout the partisan nature of an event that will kick off with a celebration of “The American Canon,”promisingto “enshrin[e] the architecture, infrastructure, and culture that forged our nation into a global force.”

item.title: BREAKING: Keir Starmer Resigns as British Prime Minister
item.description: Keir Starmer has announced that he is stepping down as the UK’s prime minister following weeks of mounting pressure over the Labour Party’s direction and electoral performance. In a statement delivered outside Downing Street, Starmer said: “The question my party is asking now is whether I am best…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Keir Starmer has announced that he is stepping down as the UK’s prime minister following weeks of mounting pressure over the Labour Party’s direction and electoral performance.

In a statement delivered outside Downing Street, Starmer said: “The question my party is asking now is whether I am best placed to lead us into the next general election. I have heard the answer of my parliamentary party to that question and I accept that answer with good grace.”

He added: “Every decision I’ve taken has been about putting the country I love first and that is why I will resign as leader of the Labour Party.”

His departure means the United Kingdom is set to have its seventh prime minister since the Brexit referendum in 2016.

Starmer’s resignation comes after a difficult period for the government, with Labour suffering significant losses in the Scottish, Welsh, and local elections in May, and facing growing concern over the rising popularity of Nigel Farage’s Reform UK and Zack Polanski’s Green Party.

Calls for the prime minister to step aside intensified following the election results, which saw Labour lose control to Plaid Cymru in Wales, and lose support across the rest of the country while Reform UK and the Green Party made substantial gains.

The aftermath prompted a number of ministerial resignations, including that of Health Secretary Wes Streeting. His departure was particularly significant given his status as one of Labour’s most prominent figures and a potential leadership contender, further raising questions about Starmer’s authority within the party.

The politician widely seen as the leading contender, Andy Burnham, had until recently been unable to challenge for the Labour leadership because he was not a member of parliament.

That changed when Josh Simons resigned as MP for Makerfield, triggering a by-election that gave Burnham a route back to Westminster.

The contest quickly became one of the most closely watched by-elections in recent years. Reform UK heavily targeted the seat following its strong local election performance in the area, leading some Labour figures to fear the party could suffer a damaging defeat.

Instead, Burnham secured a comfortable victory, and his team has made it clear that they now intend to challenge for the Labour leadership.

In the days that followed, reports emerged that senior cabinet ministers had privately informed Starmer that he no longer commanded sufficient support within the cabinet or the party to continue as prime minister. More than 100 Labour MPs had publicly called for him to resign before his announcement.

Starmer spent the weekend mulling over the decision at Chequers, the prime minister’s official country retreat, with his wife and family, before making the announcement today.

His resignation marks a dramatic reversal in fortunes for a politician who, less than two years ago, led Labour to one of the largest election victories in modern British political history.

Labour won 411 seats at the 2024 general election, giving Starmer a Commons majority of 174. In the post-war era, only Tony Blair’s landslide victory in 1997 produced a larger majority for a governing party.

But it was a landslide built on relatively weak public support. The Labour Party won fewer votes than it did under Jeremy Corbyn in 2019, while turnout fell to its lowest level at a general election since 2001. Labour’s victory was aided by a sharp collapse in Conservative support and a fragmentation of opposition votes between Reform UK, the Liberal Democrats, and the Greens.

Since entering Downing Street, Starmer’s government has made a series of political errors. His own MPs rebelled over plans to reform disability benefits, and there have been major U-turns on winter fuel payments and the two-child benefit cap.

The appointment of Peter Mandelson as ambassador to the United States put Starmer’s decision-making under significant scrutiny after Mandelson resigned following fresh revelations about his past association with the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

Labour will now begin the process of selecting a new leader. Burnham is expected to be among the leading contenders following his return to Westminster, although it remains unclear whether other figures will enter the contest.

If no rival candidates emerge, Burnham could become Labour leader unopposed. However, any challenge from another candidate would trigger a formal leadership contest.

The timetable for the contest is expected to be announced in the coming days. Until a successor is chosen, Starmer is expected to remain in office.

item.title: Why Trump’s Iran War Was ‘Doomed From the Very Start’
item.description: I have spent the past few days looking over old notes on conversations I’ve had with senior U.S. officials, Donald Trump advisers, and other sources since January, before Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu started their war on Iran. Like many of you, I am still trying to make sense…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
I have spent the past few days looking over old notes on conversations I’ve had with senior U.S. officials, Donald Trump advisers, and other sources since January, before Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu started their war on Iran. Like many of you, I am still trying to make sense of what on earth just happened – how a war so stupid, so self-defeating, so grossly nihilistic unfolded behind the scenes, orchestrated by the most pathetic, fascist dorks a collapsing American empire could ever belch out.

In reviewing my own reporting, there’s one thing I can’t quite get past: President Trump didn’t just lose this war. He lost it within its first 72 hours, long before he made us all endure the last several months of death and destruction. And I don’t say this lightly: Trump’s Iran war is not just the most Orwellian war of our lifetimes – it is also the worst-run war in modern U.S. history, perhaps in all of our nation’s history.

“It was doomed from the very start,” one senior Trump administration official tells me. “We went in with no real mission and we all knew that. Now we have to spend the next five months hoping voters don’t bitch-slap us for it.”

By now, it should be unmistakably clear to everybody, no matter what continent you live on, that Trump lost. This is true no matter how many times the White House commands your lying eyes to avert their gaze. And he didn’t just lose-lose; he lost in ways so humiliating that even some of this president’s toughest critics would not have been able to predict them in February, without getting laughed out of the room for being too charitable toward the Iranian leadership and military. Don’t take my word for it; ask the neocons who embraced Trump and his massive bombing operations, only to have their dreams shattered.

I’ve been reporting on the past decade-plus of Trumpism, so I long ago grew accustomed to covering what MAGA actually means for federal governance and policymaking: It’s just monstrous corruption, neo-Confederate dilettantes in charge, violent rapacity at home and abroad, nonstop dick-stepping and boneheaded own-goaling, and a pathological commitment to the annihilation of objective truth. (Yes, all politicians lie and spin, but Trump and his gang take it to a whole different hemisphere of con artistry.)

The war on Iran is the quintessential distillation of all of that; arguably everything horrific and debauched about the past 10 years of Trump have been building to this very moment. And what we’re left with – as a supposed, chronically in-limbo “peace deal” is apparently signed by Tehran and Washington – is a top-to-bottom disaster of historic proportions. We are not anywhere close to unpacking all the short and long-term damage to the world that President Trump has unleashed with this bloodbath in the Middle East. In all likelihood, we’ll be processing this war and Trump’s defeat for decades, and our children will live with the consequences.

Of course, one could argue that this war was always unwinnable for the Americans, and therefore Trump had lost his war before he even launched it. However, I’ve pinpointed the first three days of the conflict as a fate-sealing 72 hours because that is when I started hearing from an array of government officials and Republicans close to the president just how much Trump was privately freaking out over the U.S. death toll and casualties in the earliest days of the war. Those first 72 hours were also when sources started telling me that the Pentagon’s cover-up of U.S. casualties was already underway.

To those who spoke to and met with Trump in those first three days, it was shocking, if not surprising, the level at which he was spooked by the initial reports of Americans killed, hurt, or missing. This wasn’t, or shouldn’t have been, unexpected. As we reported at Zeteo in February, during the months leading up to the war, the president was repeatedly briefed by senior advisers on how the conflict would definitely get Americans killed; the only question was how many.

I don’t mean to suggest this president was reacting out of empathy or any moral revulsion to his

In the inaugural 72 hours of his war of choice, the president – mentally dessicated and emotionally unwell as he is – keenly knew that.

Trump’s private reactions in those hours were so visceral, so borderline panicky, that several high-ranking government officials told me at the time that they thought the president might try to pull the plug on the whole thing before the first week of the war was over. These sources say that during that brief window, the president kept asking why the Iranian leaders hadn’t been successfully forced to the negotiating table yet.

After all, Trump had bought into Israeli PM Netanyahu’s private presentation to the White House that winning the war would be quick and easy. As Zeteo has reported, Trump was briefed on how, if he launched his war, Iran would likely respond by working to spike oil prices and throttle the global economy; he ignored and downplayed those warnings.

Furthermore, in the early weeks of 2026, President Trump was utterly high on his own supply and thought that because his invasion and Maduro-kidnapping operation in Venezuela was over and done quickly, his blitz on Iran would enjoy a similar fate. In the run-up to the Iran war, people close to President Trump kept telling me he would routinely, excitedly cite his Venezuela attack during his private soliloquies about why he desperately wanted to attack Iran again. Trump repeated, incessantly, that because Venezuela went so “well” and “great” for him, that meant he’d bring the Iranians to their knees in no time.

That did not happen.

Before the first week of his new war had wrapped, the freakout was very real and very noisy.

“I don’t want you to think I’m exaggerating; I really think he might try to end this now,” another senior administration official told me that week. “This is not going well and he is asking how many more days this is going to take. Not months, days. He doesn’t have the patience for what this would take to win.”

Of course, Trump would stay the course into the summer and publicly put on a confident face – and many more Iranians and others would perish and suffer because of it. He pushed the global economy to the brink of spectacular implosion, moved unsettlingly up to the brink of ordering ground invasions or even genocide against the Iranian people, ratcheted up the paranoia and backstabbing in his own administration, and increased the cost of living and his own steep unpopularity at home. All for an illegal war that he’s now attempting to bail on while leaving Iran as the strategic victor.

Trump’s war was so shambolic that it managed to benefit the regime in Tehran in aspects big and small. For instance, two U.S. officials tell me they’ve reviewed intelligence memos that suggest the chaos and emergency of the war has made it easier for Iranian officials to crack down on dissidents in their country, including pro-Western activists.

But Trump’s surge of anxiety at the dawn of the conflict – over Defense Department reports of U.S. casualties that he knew would happen – made clear to several top officials surrounding him that he didn’t have the stomach for a protracted conflict. And these officials knew that a protracted war was the only way the Americans would have any chance at achieving the “unconditional surrender” that Trump initially demanded.

“It made me say: We lost. That’s it. And the war had only just begun,” another U.S. official tells me. “I don’t care if you were for or against this thing, if you’re the commander-in-chief, if you go to war, you can’t start thinking about cutting and running that early.”

But when it comes to waging war, Trump is – as he is with all things – a mercurial moron who swings wildly between extremes. By early March, the American president who had flipped out so feverishly over an early body count was actively contemplating getting a much, much greater number of his soldiers killed or captured. As we reported that month, some of his appointees and close, hawkish allies were advising him that if he really wanted to achieve the total victory he craved, there was no way to do that without invading. And such an invasion – to seize Iran’s highly enriched uranium, to take Kharg Island, or whatever the president latched on to at any given moment – would not guarantee an American victory, though it certainly would raise the odds of a years-long, Iraq-style quagmire.

Still, Trump was mad, and stayed mad for most of the war. He wanted to show Iran who was boss, so he quickly warmed to the idea and ordered the Pete Hegseth-fronted so-called “War Department” to ramp up preparations for different invasion scenarios.

He came dangerously close to pulling the trigger on this – but so far, has not. His laziness ultimately overran his genocidal bloodlust.

Indeed, President Trump’s laziness – and the warmongering sloth of his entire administration – bled into every meaningful facet of the conflict. In those first 72 hours of the Iran war, I kept asking Trump administration officials and other plugged-in sources what the endgame was. What was the mission? What are we doing there?

Their answers were often the same as what they had told me shortly before the war began: No one in the upper echelons of the Trump-Vance administration could seem to agree on what the mission was. As I wrote at Zeteo a week before Trump kicked off the bloodletting:

I am just stunned at how little anyone we’ve spoken to lately – whether they be senior administration officials, Trump advisers, or anyone else close to the decision-making process – can give me a straight answer about what the mission would be here, beyond the Mad King Donald just doing his thing. Sources with knowledge of the situation say that is primarily because Trump himself can’t land on a reason for war and bombing, beyond Iran pissing him off, if they don’t reach some “deal” that satisfies him. One day, Trump will privately say it would be great – and easier, than his critics suggest – for the U.S. if the regime in Tehran were decapitated. Then just days after that, he’ll bring up in a meeting that many people are telling him that a regime-change war could produce a lot of blowback, but that he may have to bomb Iran anyway.

In our reporting, essentially nobody seems capable of giving a straight answer on whether the mission would be simply to attack military and nuclear-related sites, and then try to force the Iranians back to the negotiating table, or if government overthrow and a more drawn-out conflict is more likely. When I asked a Trump adviser recently what the mission would be, they replied: “What mission?” clarifying that it’s not clear what Trump’s endgame would be beyond “projecting American strength.” Multiple administration officials told me that it is their privately held assumption that if the president were to order a larger war against Iran, the unpopularity of such an idea in the available polling suggests that Trump’s war would only harden his already historically low approval ratings. When you talk to the Trumpland elite, it is clear that many of them don’t know why they’re all doing this, except for the fact that their boss told them to do it. And that is equally horrifying and infuriating. This is no way to run a declining empire!

There was no clear mission, and it is impossible to win a war without a mission. Trump, his top officials, and practically the entirety of the Republican Party elite chose to forget that as they proceeded to eagerly set the world on fire.

Throughout the war, the Trump administration embarked on a spree of lying and laughable propaganda that would have made the party apparatchiks in George Orwell’s Nineteen Eighty-Four blush. For one thing, the Trump White House routinely tried to convince the world that war was, in fact, peace. As the supposed ceasefire kept breaking down and the war repeatedly flared up again, Trump (with a straight face) insisted his ceasefire remained intact. As the bloodbath raged, the administration kept sneering at its critics for calling their grand “military operation” a “war”... even though the president and his appointees would regularly slip up and use the term “war.” And as part of the Trump administration’s sustained assault on objective reality, senior officials put an astounding amount of pressure on Department of Defense staff to cook their own Iran war data – which was almost universally showing that the U.S. was losing the fight – just so that the White House could feed the president “good news” when there wasn’t any.

And in their latest Orwellian flourish, the Trump administration is kicking their propaganda machine into hyperdrive in order to define losing as winning.

“The plan is the same as it was months ago when the White House kept leaking that the president was going to ‘declare victory,’” one senior U.S. official says, adding sarcastically: “We won if we just say we did. Just keep saying: ‘Donald Trump won, America won, Donald Trump won.’ If we say it enough times, it’ll come true, won’t it?”

item.title: Could Trump Resign After His Iran Humiliation? Jim Acosta Weighs In
item.description: President Donald Trump hasn’t had a great week. He essentially surrendered to Iran , his name was stripped from the Kennedy Center, his multimillion dollar reflecting pool remodel is already crumbling , plus his health appears to be declining and stars are backing out of his “America 250”…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
President Donald Trump hasn’t had a great week. He essentially surrendered to Iran, his name was stripped from the Kennedy Center, his multimillion dollar reflecting pool remodel is already crumbling, plus his health appears to be declining and stars are backing out of his “America 250” celebration. It raises the question: will he finally quit?

“It is very much the latter days of a dictator who is on his way out,” says CNN host turned independent journalist Jim Acosta to Zeteo’s John Harwood. The two discussed Trump’s recent embarrassing endeavors in a Substack live conversation, in which they answered questions from an audience of subscribers.

So, will Trump call it quits? Will he throw JD Vance under the bus over Iran? How worried should we be about people like Stephen Miller and Peter Thiel? Watch the full video above to hear Acosta’s take on all that and more – and let us know what you think in the comments below.

We’ve made this conversation free for all subscribers, but if you support the work we do, consider upgrading to a paid subscription so you never hit a paywall again!

This was a live conversation on Zeteo.com and the Substack app, where subscribers could ask questions. Be sure to subscribe to catch the next Substack Live.

item.title: This Week in Palestine: Israeli Sniper Shoots Child in the Face, Israeli Settlers Torch Mosques
item.description: The video is hard to watch. Little Zeina pleading over her father’s body, “Father, father, my beloved father, come to me…. Why did you leave me? Why?” Zeina, of course, knows her father didn’t leave her. He and her brother were taken from her by Israel. They were killed while collecting water.…
item.author: This Week in Palestine | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The video is hard to watch. Little Zeina pleading over her father’s body, “Father, father, my beloved father, come to me…. Why did you leave me? Why?”

Zeina, of course, knows her father didn’t leave her. He and her brother were taken from her by Israel. They were killed while collecting water.

Zeina’s father, Mohammad Al-Habeel, and her brother, 5-year-old Mousa, are two of the more than 1,000 Palestinians killed since the so-called “ceasefire” agreement last October. “What ceasefire?” as Zeteo contributor Diana Buttu recently asked.

This week also saw more settler violence, including arson attacks on mosques, and another Palestinian child was shot in the face and killed by Israeli forces.

Here’s a brief look at another troubling week in Palestine:

Israeli settlers stole Palestinian water pipes and attacked several villages across the occupied West Bank, Palestinian media reported. The pipes that were stolen in the Khalayel Al-Loz area in the southern West Bank provided drinking water to Palestinian residents.

In Wadi Rahhal, south of Bethlehem, settlers reportedly attacked employees of the Jerusalem District Electricity Company while they were working in the area, causing minor injuries and extensive damage to their vehicle.

In the village of Jit, settlers reportedly torched Palestinian farmland and four vehicles, causing significant damage.

Israel killed 3-year-old Rayan Abu al-Ajeen, shooting him in the head, as his father carried him in central Gaza, Al Jazeera reported. His father was injured.

“My grandson, Rayan, was killed by a gunshot to the head; the bullet entered his head and exited through his eye,” Jaber Abu al-Ajeen, Rayan’s grandfather, told Al Jazeera.

The UK Court of Appeals ruled that the government’s decision to ban Palestine Action under the Terrorism Act was lawful, overturning a High Court ruling that found the proscription of the direct action group was unlawful.

Israel took over administrative control of Hebron’s holy sites, including the Ibrahimi Mosque, in violation of the 1997 Hebron Agreement, which gave the Palestinian-run Hebron Municipality planning and construction powers over the sites.

The Ibrahimi Mosque, seen as the resting place of the prophet Abraham, is significant to Muslims, Jews, and Christians alike.

The death toll from nearly-daily Israeli strikes in Gaza since the so-called “ceasefire” topped 1,000, the Palestinian Health Ministry said. That means, on average, four Palestinians have been killed each day since October.

According to Haaretz, the toll includes more than 150 children and over 120 women.

The leaders of eight Muslim-majority countries issued a statement, saying they hold Israel responsible for the rising settler violence in the occupied West Bank, including two recent arson attacks on mosques.

Palestinians say Israeli settlers set fire to mosques in the villages of Jiljiliya and Mazari a-Nubani early Wednesday, causing extensive damage. The settlers also defaced the buildings with graffiti reading “revenge” and “night of the mosques.”

Similar arson attacks took place earlier in the week.

Israel has killed one child, on average, every day during the ceasefire, UNICEF spokesperson James Elder said at a briefing.

“Let us be clear about what this means. These children were not killed in a warzone. They were killed in their homes. In their schools,” Elder said. Playing football. Fishing. They were shot, bombed, and struck by quadcopters.”

Miss last week’s edition? Catch up here. Plus, check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Trump Admin Tries to Jump on Team USA World Cup Bandwagon… But Makes Racist Fool of Itself
item.description: The 2026 World Cup in the United States is off to an unmistakably racist start . A Somali referee barred. An Iraqi player reportedly interrogated for seven hours. Uzbek players patted down and aggressively screened outside the stadium. And then there is Iran: exiled to Mexico, despite all three of…
item.author: Mehdi from Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The 2026 World Cup in the United States is off to an unmistakably racist start. A Somali referee barred. An Iraqi player reportedly interrogated for seven hours. Uzbek players patted down and aggressively screened outside the stadium. And then there is Iran: exiled to Mexico, despite all three of its group-stage matches being played in the United States. “Our team is the most oppressed one in the whole World Cup,” Iran head coach Amir Ghalenoei said earlier this week.

On Friday, the Trump administration took that racism and xenophobia to the next level, but, thankfully, made a fool of itself in the process.

The groypers apparently moonlighting as the Department of Homeland Security’s social media team tried to jump on the World Cup bandwagon with this far-right, anti-immigrant tweet, ahead of the USMNT’s clash with Australia:

Got that? “Our soil. Defend the homeland. One nation. One homeland. One team.”

First, what a bizarre slogan for a host nation of the World Cup to use while describing a home game: “Defend the Homeland”? As if other teams playing at the World Cup are… invaders? Second, notice how the DHS tagline bears an uncomfortable resemblance to the Nazi slogan “Ein Volk, ein Reich, ein Führer” (“one people, one realm, one leader”). I have written elsewhere about the way in which the Trump administration’s social media accounts “can’t stop posting Nazi imagery and memes… and leaning heavily into fascist aesthetics.”

But the funniest part is that the image itself completely undermines the accompanying far-right message. It is a picture of three non-white American players (Chris Richards, Sergiño Dest, Folarin Balogun), one of whom (Balogun) scored twice in the USMNT’s 4-1 demolition of Paraguay at the start of the tournament.

Balogun is an American by virtue of.. wait for it… birthright citizenship.

The Monaco striker was born in Brooklyn in 2001, but only by accident. His parents were visiting New York from the UK, where they lived. When Balogun’s mother, seven months pregnant with a self-described “massive” belly, tried to board the return flight, she was blocked by a “concerned airline staffer.” So Balogun was born in the U.S. before returning to the UK at 2 months old.

To be clear, if the Trump administration had its way, Balogun would not be able to play for the United States. He would either be playing for England, where he was raised, or Nigeria, where his parents are from. And, in the coming weeks, if the conservative-dominated Supreme Court accepts the Trump administration’s claims and guts the 14th Amendment’s long-standing guarantee of birthright citizenship to all children born in the U.S., there will be no more Baloguns in the future.

But it isn’t just Balogun. Consider the rest of the U.S. starting lineup for Friday’s game against Australia. Four other non-white USMNT players on that pitch in Seattle also made a mockery of the dumb DHS tweet.

  • Antonee Robinson–The Fulham full-back was born in England, the son of an English mother and anaturalized American citizenfather.
  • Sergiño Dest–The PSV full-back was born in the Netherlands to a Dutch mother and aSurinamese father, who was a naturalized U.S. citizen.
  • Malik Tillman–The Bayer Leverkusen midfielder was born in Germany to an American father and aGerman mother.
  • Ricardo Pepi–The PSV striker was born in the U.S., the son ofMexican immigrant parents.

And those are just players from Friday’s starting 11.

There were also five USMNT substitutes who took to the field against Australia, three of whom have immigrant connections: Sebastian Berhalter, born in the UK, and of Puerto Rican, Polish, and German descent;

Then there is the USMNT’s star player, Christian Pulisic, nicknamed “Captain America,” who missed Friday's game against Australia due to an injury. He is, as his last name suggests, of Croatian descent.

Oh, and let’s not forget the current manager of the USMNT, Mauricio Pochettino, who is an Argentine immigrant.

So Stephen Miller can rant and rave about the threat of migrants to our culture; Donald Trump can try to block birthright citizenship; and the far-right social media team at the DHS can keep posting their neo-Nazi memes, but when it comes to the USMNT at this World Cup, it simply cannot be denied that immigrants are a blessing and diversity is our strength.

item.title: FIFA Has a Dark History of Covering for Fascists – And Trump Is No Exception
item.description: FIFA and fascism go hand-in-hand. Yes, you read that right. The organizer of the World Cup has historically been complicit in legitimizing authoritarian regimes and helping distract from their crimes – a phenomenon commonly known as “sportswashing.” Just take a look at the 1934 World Cup. And the…
item.author: "Zeteo | Dark Side of the World Cup "
item.content:encoded:
FIFA and fascism go hand-in-hand.

Yes, you read that right. The organizer of the World Cup has historically been complicit in legitimizing authoritarian regimes and helping distract from their crimes – a phenomenon commonly known as “sportswashing.”

Just take a look at the 1934 World Cup. And the 1978 World Cup… Oh, and the 2026 World Cup.

In the second episode of Zeteo’s three-part video series, “The Dark Side of the World Cup,” Mehdi walks through these three World Cups – which respectively take place under Italy’s Benito Mussolini, Argentina’s junta leader Jorge Videla, and the U.S.’s Donald Trump – to connect the dots and highlight how FIFA helped these authoritarians cover up their crimes.

Plus, Mehdi breaks down the controversies around the Russia- and Qatar-hosted World Cups and digs into the double standards when it comes to the lack of media coverage on the U.S.’s own controversies.

Paid subscribers can watch the full video above, while free subscribers can access a two-minute preview. Consider upgrading your subscription to access this full series and all Zeteo has to offer.

And if you haven’t caught up already, check out Episode 1 here.

item.title: Trump Privately Rages That Israel Is Trying to Drag Him Back Into His Iran War
item.description: Donald Trump desperately wants out of the massively unpopular war he started, and appears willing to hand Iran a stunning victory in exchange. And the U.S. president is absolutely furious at the Israeli government and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu for trying to stop him from bailing on their…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Donald Trump desperately wants out of the massively unpopular war he started, and appears willing to hand Iran a stunning victory in exchange. And the U.S. president is absolutely furious at the Israeli government and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu for trying to stop him from bailing on their disastrous war of choice, by continuing to kill people in Lebanon despite the U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding establishing a ceasefire there.

During the past several days, Trump has aggressively complained to administration officials and other confidants that the Israelis are trying to trick him into restarting a full-blown war with Iran, three people with direct knowledge of the matter and another person briefed on it tell Zeteo.

“He’s swearing a lot about it,” according to a close Trump adviser. A Trump administration official adds bluntly: “At least right now, he’s definitely madder at the Israelis than the Iranians.”

The sources who’ve spoken to President Trump in the past several days say Israel’s continued attacks in Lebanon and Israeli leaders’ efforts to pressure the Americans into abandoning the (albeit incredibly shaky) memorandum of understanding with Iran have, in fact, further driven Trump in the opposite direction.

The president keeps venting to advisers how angry he is at Netanyahu and other political and media figures – in the U.S. and in Israel – for transparently trying to drag him back into war, or for suggesting that Trump is surrendering to Iran.

The president is, however, effectively surrendering to Iran. Practically every knowledgeable observer – those opposed to the war from the jump, those who’ve been trying to start this war for decades – views the terms of the MOU as extraordinarily favorable to Iran. The deal, which Trump keeps saying is a “great deal,” would put the Iranian regime in a substantively stronger position (with a lot more cash, massive sanctions relief, and continued control over the Strait of Hormuz, for starters) than it had been in before the war began – and also help solidify its international bragging rights for fighting off the mighty war machine of the world’s undisputed superpower.

It was always clear that this war was unwinnable from its inception, and that its negotiated conclusion would never be solely on the Trump administration’s terms – and yet, Trump went ahead anyway. Trump is taking fire now from Republicans, hawkish Democrats, and much of the media for giving Iran what it wants to end this conflict, when that is the unavoidable cost of launching this incredibly stupid and avoidable war.

The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment on this story.

Trump, of course, is not mad at Israeli leadership out of any moral urgency or compunction. Trump, the “lazy warmonger,” illegally started a regional bloodbath, and he and his administration own the carnage and the coming years of fallout forever. And just because the Israelis, who want to keep the war going, feel betrayed by Trump, and Trump is fuming about and cursing out Netanyahu, doesn’t mean this is a permanent break in the relationship.

After all, Netanyahu and Trump have been here before. The American president spent years absolutely loathing the Israeli PM because he congratulated Joe Biden after the Democrat won the 2020 presidential election – only for Trump and Netanyahu to make up and back each other’s increasingly brutal policies. Trump has also reportedly expressed anger against Netanyahu before, in the context of Israel’s genocide in Gaza. It hasn’t meant much. Since the supposed “ceasefire” in Gaza, negotiated by Trump, Israeli forces have killed more than 1,000 people.

And just because Trump is feeling one way this week doesn’t mean he won’t redirect his wrath (or start bombing again) the following week. Since February, the U.S. commander-in-chief has vacillated so many times between waging a savage war and begging for a lasting “peace” – swinging quickly from plotting invasions and publicly threatening apocalyptic war crimes to crying uncle and pulling the plug – that it’s been easy to lose count.

What we have today is a quarrel between two like-minded, allied warmongers – it’s just that the American warmonger now seems to instinctively realize how badly his warmongering is politically destroying him and his party at home.

As Zeteo reported at the dawn of Trump’s disastrous war, virtually nobody at the highest levels of the Trump-Vance administration earnestly wanted to wage it – except for President Trump, the only one who truly matters. Basically everybody else, despite their varying degrees of queasiness, went along to get along – letting Trump know that they were with him all the way.

Now, many of them are relieved that Trump really wants to ditch the war now, meaning they’ll have more time to focus on the midterm elections and on their domestic agenda of waging war on ordinary Americans. For one thing, the number of administration officials who use the R-word when venting to Zeteo about Trump’s war is… embarrassingly substantial.

Furthermore, private polling circulated among Republicans shows that signing the MOU and Trump’s decision to try to end the war is now polling upwards of 60 percent in the U.S., a source familiar with the matter tells Zeteo. This is not, per se, because Americans broadly approve of every line or disputed detail of the memorandum; a solid majority just want Trump’s war to end ASAP, and wish it was never started in the first place!

In fact, Trump publicly acknowledged this week that his war was driving the U.S. – and global – economy into the abyss, and that he didn’t want to be a “Herbert Hoover”-type president: “I didn’t want to see economic catastrophe. If you kept this going, that could have happened. But all I know is, every time we talked about the possibility of peace, the stock market shot up like a rocket ship.”

On Wednesday, Trump called out Israel, saying he thinks they could “do better” in their conduct in Lebanon. “I’m not saying they shouldn’t protect themselves. I’m saying, when two drones are shot into the desert and drop harmlessly, you don’t have to knock down buildings in Beirut. They could behave better, and frankly they could do a better job.”

That same day, the U.S. and Iran signed a memorandum of understanding for a peace deal – ahead of a planned Friday signing – marking the biggest development toward ending Trump’s “little excursion.”

The MOU explicitly included Lebanon, reading: “The United States of America and the Islamic Republic of Iran and their allies in the current war, by signing this MOU, declared the immediate and permanent termination of military operations on all fronts, including in Lebanon, and… ensuring the territorial integrity and sovereignty of Lebanon.”

Nevertheless, Israeli leaders continued their opposition to the terms, actively continuing attacks in Lebanon. Meanwhile, many Israeli and pro-Israel officials and commentators slammed the Trump administration, aruging that it was a boon for Iran and bad for Israel. Some went as far as to attack President Trump, and even call the U.S. “treacherous.”

In response, Trump and Vance have dug their heels in, at least publicly.

In an interview with the New York Times published Thursday, Vance responded to criticism from top Israeli officials like Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich and National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir.

“What is your exact proposal? You’re a country of nine million people,” Vance said. “You can’t just kill your way out of solving every single national security problem that you have.” He pushed Israel to allow the negotiations to play out and “give a little bit of credit to the United States of America, which I think has been an incredible partner for the Israeli government for a long time.”

At a press conference at the White House the same day, Vance continued to respond sharply to Israeli critics.

“Number one: Donald J. Trump is the only head of state in the entire world who is sympathetic to the nation of Israel at this moment in time, and he happens to be the head of state of the world’s superpower,” Vance said. “If I was in the cabinet of the Israeli government, I might not be attacking the only powerful ally that I have anywhere left in the entire world.”

The vice president wasn’t freelancing; he was doing what Trump sent him out there to do. “Trump LOVED it,” a source close to the president messaged Zeteo, describing Trump’s elation at watching Vance chastise the Israeli government on TV.

Vance added: “The other thing that I would say is that over the last three months, two-thirds of the defensive weapons that have protected your homeland have been built by American hands and paid for by American tax dollars. The problem for Israel is not Donald J. Trump. And anybody in Israel who thinks their biggest problem is the President of the United States needs to wake up and smell the reality of the situation that country is in.”

And still, Israeli officials outwardly flouted the warnings, continuing to bomb Lebanon. Early Friday, Israeli forces reportedly killed 47 people and wounded another 97, while Hezbollah reportedly killed four Israeli soldiers in a tank.

Early Friday morning, the Israeli national security minister, Ben-Gvir, issued among the most horrific and dangerous statements: “For every tear of an Israeli mother, a thousand Lebanese mothers must weep. All of Lebanon must burn!”

He added: “With all due respect to the Americans, Israel must make it clear to the entire world that the blood of our sons and the security of our citizens are not forfeit. All of Lebanon must burn.”

Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz expressed similar sentiments, saying, “The entire first line of Lebanese villages has been destroyed.”

“We are destroying all the houses,” he said. “The residents will never see them standing before their eyes again.”

Israel and Hezbollah then reportedly reached a tentative ceasefire.

Iran has reportedly told mediators it stands ready to suspend the agreement and launch strikes against Israel if it does not stop attacking Lebanon. “Should the United States intervene, conditions particularly those related to the Strait of Hormuz could rapidly revert to a wartime environment,” an Iranian official told Drop Site News.

And despite that, U.S. intelligence agencies warn the Trump administration, Netanyahu is likely to undermine the peace deal by continuing Israel’s violence in Lebanon.

Do you have any info for Swin and Prem? Text them on Signal at swin24.68 and premthakker.35. Your anonymity will be protected.

item.title: On Juneteenth, 7 Ways Trump Attacked Black History in the U.S.
item.description: Juneteenth is a significant day on the U.S. calendar that commemorates the end of slavery in 1865 . It’s long been a day celebrated by Black Americans, but it only became a federal holiday in 2021, under Joe Biden. During his first term, Donald Trump ridiculously claimed he made the holiday –…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Juneteenth is a significant day on the U.S. calendar that commemorates the end of slavery in 1865. It’s long been a day celebrated by Black Americans, but it only became a federal holiday in 2021, under Joe Biden.

During his first term, Donald Trump ridiculously claimed he made the holiday – which, according to him, “no one” had “ever heard of” – “very famous” after backlash over a rally he had planned on the day in Tulsa, Oklahoma. Last year, he didn’t recognize it at all. Instead, he complained that the country had “too many non-working holidays.”

Even if he wanted to, Trump cannot cancel Juneteenth without Congress. But that hasn’t stopped him and his administration from attempting to erase what the important day stands for and what it’s supposed to remember. Here are seven ways Trump and his administration have attacked Black history, Black Americans, and civil rights since returning to office:

Trump removed Juneteenth and Martin Luther King Jr. Day from the National Park Service’s annual free-entry days calendar, replacing them with Flag Day and, wait for it, his own birthday.

Just weeks into his second term as president, Trump’s war on diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) led to a pause on all federal activities and events related to “special observances,” including Juneteenth, Martin Luther King Jr. Day, and Black History Month, among many others.

Nearly 400 books were removed from the shelves of the U.S. Naval Academy as part of Trump’s attacks on DEI. Nearly half of the books centered around race, while many others included race as a secondary theme.

In August 2025, Trump ranted on his social media site about the country’s museums, calling them “the last remaining segment of ‘WOKE.’” The president claimed, “Everything discussed is how horrible our Country is, how bad Slavery was, and how unaccomplished the downtrodden have been — Nothing about Success.”

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has been accused of disproportionately targeting Black and female service members. Almost 60% of the senior officers Hegseth and Trump have fired are Black or women, according to Democratic Senator Jack Reed, the ranking member of the Armed Services Committee.

Those sacked included General Charles Q. Brown, who was only the second Black American to be named chair of the Joint Chiefs of Staff. Hegseth had previously said Brown should be fired for being “woke.”
Earlier this month, Hegseth blocked the promotion of nine Navy officers, including two Black officers. It followed a similar action in March.

The Trump administration removed a directive explicitly preventing federal contractors from having segregated facilities.

While businesses must still follow federal and state laws outlawing segregation and discrimination, experts and civil rights groups said the move “sends a clear message that the federal government does not care” if companies introduce segregation into their workplaces.

Federal employees last year dismantled an exhibit about nine people enslaved by President George Washington at the President’s House in Philadelphia. Some of the exhibits have since been restored. This month, a federal judge has ordered the reinstallation of signs and exhibits on topics like slavery and climate change nationwide.

***

It didn’t take long for Trump’s racism to influence government policy. His attacks on the country’s Black history serve as a reminder that racism in the U.S. is not a thing of the past.

Zeteo’s Mohammed Abdelbary contributed to this piece.
