item.title: Bari Weiss’s Newest Hire Has a Long History of Anti-Muslim Remarks
item.description: Hello, and welcome to this week’s early edition of ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In today’s edition, we dig into Bari Weiss’s latest big hire at CBS News – and the allegations of Islamophobia…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Hello, and welcome to this week’s early edition of ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In today’s edition, we dig into Bari Weiss’s latest big hire at CBS News – and the allegations of Islamophobia that have followed him throughout his UK broadcasting and political career. Also, is woke algae ruining Trump’s big, beautiful reflecting pool? And how are the MAGA hawks at Fox dealing with Trump’s supposed Iran peace deal?

“No, don’t try that one on me!” Sky News presenter Trevor Phillips dismissively sneered at UK Green Party leader Zack Polanski last month during an extremely contentious interview.

That contemptuous response came after Polanski, the only Jewish party leader in the UK, as well as the only leader who is outspoken against the genocide in Gaza, wondered why his “Jewish identity [was] being erased from this conversation” as Phillips continuously twisted the left-wing politician’s previous remarks about the threat of antisemitism in the UK.

Polanski noted that many Jews – such as himself – do not feel that pro-Palestinian rallies should be perceived as unsafe hate marches, while acknowledging that attacks on Jewish sites and people in Britain are on the rise. But Phillips chose to ignore and even belittle Polanski’s personal experiences and feelings on the matter. Even when the progressive leader brought up his own recent brushes with antisemitism.

Five weeks after Phillips “monstered” Polanski on air, as Zeteo’s own Owen Jones observed in a recent column in The Guardian, CBS News editor-in-chief Bari Weiss announced that the longtime British broadcaster and political figure had been hired as her network’s senior global affairs correspondent.

“Trevor Phillips cuts through the noise. His decades-long career is a masterclass in seeing beyond groupthink and pursuing the truth,” Weiss said in a statement about his hiring, noting that his reporting will appear on all of CBS News’s programming. “Trevor’s deep knowledge of geopolitics and history will be an incredible asset at CBS News, where he’ll quickly become an indispensable voice for audiences across all platforms.”

Following the five-alarm fire she set at the network in recent weeks with her “Black Thursday” firings at ‘60 Minutes,’ which has sparked growing murmurs that Weiss could be demoted ahead of the CNN-CBS News merger, one wonders why her next big move was to hire a 72-year-old opinion host unfamiliar to American viewers. (At the very least, it doesn’t seem like this will move the needle with younger audiences, which Weiss’s digital-first strategy was supposed to appeal to.)

“I guess I’d be looking at what correspondent work he had done before. He is a presenter. That’s different,” one former CBS foreign bureau staffer told me.

Of course, with Weiss – a former New York Times opinion columnist and founder of the anti-woke blog The Free Press – seeing herself as a heterodox thought leader, it doesn’t take much digging to see exactly why Phillips appeals to her.

First, Phillips will be reporting out of the network’s London bureau, which has already seen a huge shakeup after Weiss pushed out highly respected bureau chief Claire Day after a freelance cameraman accused Day of running the bureau like a “Hamas cell.” She replaced Day with Shayndi Raice, a former Wall Street Journal editor with no broadcast experience but similar views on Israel and Gaza as the “Zionist fanatic” Weiss.

And while Phillips comes to CBS News following a long and distinguished career in British politics, writing and broadcasting, it is not without a fair bit of controversy. He was a Labour Party politician who served as the chairman of the UK’s Equality and Human Rights Commission (and the Commission for Racial Equality, its predecessor) for nearly a decade, leaving in September 2012.

His management of the EHRC was wracked with turmoil, which saw six of the organization’s commissioners leave due to this “divisive leadership” that had led to “deep discontentment and dissatisfaction. It was also during his time in charge of the race commission that Phillips, in 2004, said that the UK should stop supporting multiculturalism and instead “assert a core of Britishness.”

His views on Islam, however, are what have drawn the most notice in recent years. Having initially helped usher in the term “Islamophobia” due to his work on a groundbreaking 1997 report by the Runnymede Trust about rampant anti-Muslim sentiment in the UK, by January 2016, Phillips was insisting that British Muslims “see the world differently from the rest of us.” In an April 2016 documentary, he argued that while anti-Muslim sentiment was well-documented, the 1997 commission “got almost everything else wrong” in its report.

Warning that Muslims were building “nations within nations” in the West, Phillips said that the UK was “heading in entirely the wrong direction” and there was” a life-and-death struggle for the soul of British Islam” in which the population needed to “take sides.” Right-wing author and notorious Islamophobe Douglas Murray, who recently joined Weiss’s The Free Press as a weekly columnist, heaped praise on Phillips at the time for breaking “taboos.”

A month later, Phillips published a 100-page paper in which he argued that Britain was “sleepwalking to catastrophe” over race, drawing a striking parallel to the late British Conservative politician Enoch Powell’s infamous ‘Rivers of Blood’ speech.

“Rome may not yet be in flames, but I think I can smell the smouldering whilst we hum to the music of liberal self-delusion,” Phillips wrote, adding: “Any attempt to ask whether aspects of minority disadvantage may be self-inflicted is denounced as ‘blaming the victim.’ Instead, we prefer to answer any difficult questions by focusing on the historic prejudices of the dominant majority. In short, it’s all about white racism.”

In 2017, Phillips said placing a Christian child with Muslim foster parents was “akin to child abuse.” He later claimed, according to an internal Labour Party investigation, that few British Muslims wore poppies to remember the UK’s war dead on Remembrance Sunday.

All of this eventually led the Labour Party to suspend Phillips over his allegedly Islamophobic views and comments in March 2020.

“They say I am accusing Muslims of being different. Well, actually, that’s true. The point is Muslims are different. And in many ways, I think that’s admirable,” Phillips declared following the suspension. Phillips, who had blamed the suspension on “political gangsterism” led by then-party leader Jeremy Corbyn, was then quietly reinstated just over a year later under the new centrist leadership of Keir Starmer.

Since then, Phillips has remained outspoken in his criticism of the Muslim community in Britain. After his tense back-and-forth with Polanski, he told Times Radio that there’s “an Islamist community that wants to kill Jews and they want to use the arguments that are being had about the Middle East as cover for their malign intent,” adding that the Green Party “draws its support from that community.”

The controversy doesn’t end with just Phillips’s anti-’woke’ political and cultural views, though. In recent months, his decades-long friendship with Peter Mandelson – the disgraced British diplomat and politician whose lengthy relationship with Jeffrey Epstein sparked a full-blown political scandal and his own arrest in February – has raised questions for Phillips. Questions that he’s essentially brushed aside.

Saying that he has not cut off Mandelson amid the revelations of his close ties to the deceased sex predator, Phillips merely said nobody is “perfect” while defending his longtime friend, who once served as best man at his wedding.

“What would that say about me as a person if I suddenly decided to unperson somebody because of what I discovered about them?” he said in February.


Meanwhile, as Mehdi observed on Twitter at the time, Phillips continued to cover his friend even as authorities began investigating Mandelson for misconduct. “Given Mandelson was best man at Trevor Phillips’s wedding, and they have been friends and political allies for 50 years, shouldn’t Phillips recuse himself from all Mandelson coverage at Sky News? What am I missing here?” Mehdi wondered.

Still, it is Phillips’s views on race and Muslims that have some CBS News staffers expressing concern and outrage over the latest new face that Weiss has welcomed into the Tiffany Network.

“What an utter joke. His anti-Muslim views are clearly a qualifier,” one senior CBS staffer told me, while a network insider stated that “most people I’ve talked to are just disgusted with the hire.”
According to one CBS News reporter, the poaching of Phillips from Sky News is a data point that tells a bigger story when it comes to Weiss’s editorial vision for the network.

“The hiring of Trevor Philips just further cements what’s really going on here. Bari Weiss has no interest in providing space for different viewpoints she doesn’t ideologically agree with. CBS News already has a history of a lack of inclusion. Right now, you can count on one hand, maybe half a hand, how many people with editorially prominent positions are of Arab or Muslim descent,” the reporter said.

“Trevor Philips has been accused on various occasions of Islamophobia and anti-Muslim statements and attitudes,” the reporter continued. “I think what we’re seeing here is Bari looking to utilize a Black man who is one of the very few minorities that have been hired since she took over – but he’s not just a minority, he’s a minority that doesn’t believe in multiculturalism and says ‘Muslims are different.’”

Representatives for CBS News, Weiss, and Phillips did not respond to requests for comment.

After spending over $14 million on no-bid contracts to coat the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool with “American flag blue” waterproofing paint, Donald Trump has relentlessly congratulated himself for making the pool “beautiful” after other presidents had left it “filthy” and “dirty” for years.

Unfortunately, as soon as the project was completed, algae returned in force and turned the water green – prompting MAGA to go into desperate spin mode. According to Real America’s Voice host Grant Stinchfield, the president could be the victim of foul play ahead of America’s 250th birthday bash.

“President Trump fixes the reflecting pool and a week later it’s green again, loaded with algae… Sabotage… Vandalism? I believe it is. The left can’t stand Trump, American greatness and his quest to make DC beautiful again,” he tweeted.

Other right-wing luminaries, meanwhile, attempted to claim that pictures and videos of the dirty pool were faked by “TDS sufferers genuinely hate America.” As for Fox, it was probably summed up best by Jesse Watters’s lackey Johnny Belisario, who merely waved off the algal invasion as anti-Trump noise.

“I’m here at the newly renovated reflecting pool. It’s painted American flag blue,” he said on Monday night. “The Democrats will tell you there’s green algae. There’s pool guys cleaning it up. No other president would do that.”

While “MAGA hacks” like Benny Johnson gush over the announced U.S.-Iran peace agreement as a “historic” American victory, the hawks that make up Trump’s ‘Fox News Cabinet’ aren’t nearly as jubilant – largely because the president is refusing to release any text of the agreement until after it’s signed on Friday.

“[W]hy can’t we, the people, see the damn MOU? Not through people briefed by an anonymous person. Honestly, I’ve never seen anything like this,” Fox host Mark Levin, who has advised Trump on the war effort, tweeted this week.

Fox anchor Dana Perino, a former George W. Bush administration official, grumbled about the secrecy over the agreement, adding that Trump boasts of having the “most transparent administration in history.”

“There is talk of a reconstruction fund of hundreds of billions of dollars,” Fox contributor Marc Thiessen, who has also had the president’s ear throughout the war, groaned. “That will be like doing the Marshall Plan for Germany while the Nazis were still in power. I’m anxious to see what the details of the deal are and what gets negotiated, but I’m concerned.”

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘ Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

item.title: Fascists and Football: How Italy's Mussolini and Argentina's Junta Turned the World Cup Authoritarian
item.description: “History repeats itself,” people often say in reference to politics and current affairs. But the saying also applies to sports. While Trump’s authoritarian agenda is certainly bringing chaos to the World Cup, he isn’t the first fascist to do this. In this second episode of Zeteo’s three-part video…
item.author: "Zeteo | Dark Side of the World Cup "
item.content:encoded:
“History repeats itself,” people often say in reference to politics and current affairs. But the saying also applies to sports. While Trump’s authoritarian agenda is certainly bringing chaos to the World Cup, he isn’t the first fascist to do this.

In this second episode of Zeteo’s three-part video series, Mehdi goes back in time to two famous World Cup competitions that were plagued by authoritarian regimes:

  • Italy’s

    1934 World Cup, which took place during the height of dictator Benito Mussolini’s fascist regime and his country’sgenocidein Libya

  • Argentina’s

    1978 World Cup, which was held during Argentina’s “Dirty War,” in which the military regime forciblydisappearedtens of thousands of Argentines and tortured their people in detention centers just kilometers away from the World Cup stadium

“So many Argentines had a sibling or a parent or a child who had just been picked up off the street and was gone,” Argentine-American NPR journalist Jasmine Garsd says in the episode. “Sportswashing works for a moment…and then everyone has to go back to a life of terror. And distress.”

Mehdi and the episode’s other guests – including Human Rights Watch’s Minky Worden and Propaganda Researcher Daniele Serapiglia – also discuss the controversies around Russia and Qatar’s recent World Cup competitions and the double standards when it comes to the coverage of the U.S.’s own scandals.

Paid subscribers can watch this full episode, as well as the third one that will be released soon. They can also access the first episode here.

Free subscribers can watch a 2-minute preview. Upgrade your subscription today to access this episode and the others, paywall-free!

item.title: Trump Can’t Buy Joy
item.description: On this day in 1858, at the Illinois Republican Convention in Springfield, U.S. Senate candidate Abraham Lincoln warned that amid national divides over slavery, “A house divided against itself cannot stand.” Within three years, Lincoln would be president, with civil war raging. Good morning! Prem…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1858, at the Illinois Republican Convention in Springfield, U.S. Senate candidate Abraham Lincoln warned that amid national divides over slavery, “A house divided against itself cannot stand.” Within three years, Lincoln would be president, with civil war raging.

Good morning! Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Tuesday wherever you are. I’m just returning from London, where I met Paddington, cursed the monarchy, and ate scones. One observation: It is crazy how captured US society is by crypto and AI. On the “tube,” I saw maybe one ad every now and again about our dear favorite industries. But in the New York subways, you simply cannot escape the next “.ai” or startup shoving itself in your face. I’m sure they’re on their way everywhere, but it was jarring to see the contrast even between two otherwise relatively similar cities…

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ the joy of sports in New York outshines Trump’s pathetic UFC 80th birthday extravaganza (which also served as one huge advertisement for trash he and his family are invested in). Plus: Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed is out with a new ad featuring one Bernie Sanders.

Could there have been a better illustration of the competing visions of society than Donald Trump’s pathetic, sad UFC extravaganza happening alongside the effervescence in New York, as thousands from around the world enjoy the World Cup, and rejoice in the Knicks’ victory?

Let me clear my throat first: There’s no need to strain ourselves to project political commentary onto sports, or to conflate only certain evils with certain sports. The NBA isn’t inherently a holier institution than the UFC, not when you recall that Knicks’ owner Jim Dolan warmly welcomed Trump to a game last week, or that Dolan has also allegedly used facial recognition technology to surveil a trans woman, lawyers, protesters, and more.

What’s of interest here to me is how the public engaged with sport, and what sport did or did not afford to society. So let’s take a look.

In one corner, we have Trump’s UFC event. A spectacle that took nearly a month, hundreds of workers, and $60 million to set the stage for. The affair was paid for, in part, by a glitzy array of sponsors that Trump and his family themselves are invested in: the Trump sons’ crypto exchange; Trump’s sad, failing social media platform; and, of course, his gaudy “Trump Coins.” (More on all this corruption later.)

In attendance were an array of top Trump officials like Pete Hegseth, Marco Rubio, Kash Patel, and JD Vance; billionaire CEOs like Meta’s Mark Zuckerberg and Paramount’s David Ellison; sports team owners including the New England Patriots’ Robert Kraft and the New York Knicks’ James Dolan; and hundreds of military service members who were “recruited” to help fill the stands.

The function was topped off by a military flyover (and what appeared to be at least one brawl between fans).

The entire thing, notably, did not seem to register beyond traditional UFC media, or obsequious right-wing media. It didn’t seem to resonate with the public beyond, which may well be a good thing for Republicans: The event was every bit as shameful and corrupt as it was an insult to American taxpayers and institutions. Perhaps the only moment that broke through from the whole affair was when fighter Josh Hokit exclaimed in the ring, to a microphone held by podcast host Joe Rogan: “Michelle Obama is a man! Am I right, America?!”

It was ugly enough that UFC honcho and Trump ally Dana White felt obligated to reject Hokit’s commentary.

Meanwhile, in another corner, we have the fervor coursing through New York City, amid the New York Knicks’ victorious run in the NBA playoffs – after more than 50 years – and the World Cup, which is being hosted in several cities across North America, including the New York and New Jersey area.

In contrast to Trump’s garish event, the revelry in New York had been open to the public: livestreams of the NBA playoffs were projected everywhere, including at city-run screens all over the city (a practice that will now continue for the World Cup).

People of every creed got in on the excitement. Basketball became the vessel for community, simply, for fun. The atmosphere wasn’t just reserved for billionaires and politicians and sponsors.

The phrase “My mayor’s Muslim, my bagel’s Jewish, my Christian Dior, Knicks in four!” (popularized first in a user’s comment on TikTok before being exploited, unfortunately, by prediction market Kalshi) went massively viral – emblematic of the confluence of optimism coursing through the city’s streets. History was being made, and everyone, everyone could be part of it – even in a city for which such a premise was hotly contested during the mayoral race just a year earlier.

And that spirit, naturally, ties beautifully into the World Cup. A global event that the United States is fortunate to host, alongside Canada and Mexico. While the Trump administration’s brutal immigration “enforcement” apparatus has put a damper on the World Cup, New York in particular serves as such a direct rebuke of Trump and his ilk’s aims. After all, whether Trump keeps thousands of fans out or not, New York itself teems with residents who hail from the nations descending upon the US.

And New York, under their Muslim Mayor Zohran Mamdani, has embraced it – low-cost tickets for the matches; soccer events across the city; extended soccer field lighting for residents; and encouraging millions of residents and visitors to travel across the city to explore the exceptionally wide range of immigrant communities and restaurants.

This week, sports in the U.S. presented two competing worlds. In one corner: We see sports as exclusive, gaudy, jingoistic thumping – not even for America, per se, but for one man who represents the worst of it. In the other, we see sports as a vehicle for a more international patriotism – for optimism, and interest in one another, and in fun being for all, and not for the few.

If nothing else, where would you rather be? The 80th birthday party for the guy who was best friends with the world’s most infamous pedophile? Or the streets of New York City, dancing and singing and chanting and having hope and meeting a new friend who’s at once from oceans away, but who, just like you, was in the city too?

  • Signs and wonders:Trump’s deal with Iran wassignedelectronically – on the U.S. side by an administration that hasclaimednon-personal signing is invalid – before a formal event in Switzerland on Friday.
  • Term limits:In France, for theG7, Trumptalked upthe deal. Terms were not disclosed, butReutersreporteda U.S. official saying they included immediate opening of the Strait of Hormuz, ending the U.S. blockade, and economic relief for Iran.Tehransaidit would charge “fees” in the Strait, not “tolls.”
  • Windfall waffle:On CBS, JD Vance wasasked: “The Iranians are saying they’re gonna have access to a $300 billion reconstruction fund. True or false?” The VP’s answer contained many words, all evasive and boilable down to… “True.” Trump on Tuesdaydeniedthe claim, calling it “ridiculous” and a “rumor.”
  • Secret cash:In Israel, it wasreportedthat before the deal was reached, the Trump administration covertly approved Iran receiving billions of dollars in return for Qatari vessels being allowed through the Strait. Trump has made payments to Iran central to his case against Barack Obama’s Iran nuclear deal from 2015.
  • Gas plunge:Oil prices fell, but after three-and-a-half months of war, the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve wasreportedto be at its lowest level since 1983.
  • Lebanon latest:Though the U.S.-Iran deal said conflict in Lebanon would cease, Israel wasn’t a party to it. One person waskilledin an Israeli strike in southern Lebanon, while an Israeli drone was reported over Beirut. Israel has killed nearly 3,800 people in Lebanon since the start of the war. Tehranwarnedthat any Israeli strike on Lebanon or its continued occupation, as Benjamin Netanyahu has promised, will constitute a violation of the agreement.
  • ‘Not happy’:Trumpsaidtoday that he’s “not happy with the way Israel has handled themselves with Lebanon and with Hezbollah.” He laterclaimedthat he suggested to Israel to let Syria “take care” of Hezbollah. “[T]o be honest with you, I think they’d do a better job of doing it.”
  • ‘Not all Hezbollah’:In an apparent moment of some form of clarity, Trumpsaidthat “too many people are being killed” in Lebanon and criticized Israel for striking entire residential buildings: “You don’t have to knock down an apartment house every time you’re looking for somebody because there’s a lot of people in those apartment houses – and they’re not all Hezbollah.”

Donald Trump’s long-promised White House UFC fight might’ve been staged in part as a presidential birthday party, in part as a patriotic pageant, but contrary to White House denials, it also amounted to one long ad for businesses from which the first family and friends derive great profit. Here’s a look at how:

TKO Holdings: Donald Trump purchased up to $50,000 of stock in TKO Holdings, the parent company of UFC, in advance of the White House event.

World Liberty Financial: The Trump family’s crypto exchange, run by Donald Trump Jr and Eric Trump, gave $125,000 worth of its USD1 stablecoin to two champions, Justin Gaethje and Ciryl Gane. Gaethje was the night’s biggest earner with $825,000 in total. One crypto expert told The Guardian the payout amounted to a giant ad for the Trump family token. At the event, an announcer

Truth Social: The deeply unprofitable Trump social media platform sponsored the heavyweight match between Josh Hokit and Derrick Lewis. “Truth Social… the real voice of President Trump,” the announcer declared. Trump is the company’s largest shareholder.

Trump Coins: Trump Coins – not to be confused with the president’s crypto “meme coin,” and not a new grift for the president – sponsored closed captioning on the UFC broadcast. A website for the coins, which bear Trump’s likeness, describes them as “the official gold and silver of UFC Freedom 250.” A special edition 1oz gold version made for the event sold for $11,999.99, a markup of more than $7,000 over the melt value of the gold it contained.

Polymarket: Donald Trump Jr. advises the prediction market, whose logo lined the cage. His father’s Commodity Futures Trading Commission chair, Mike Selig, the sole sitting member, is fighting to shield Polymarket from state regulators, having until last year worked at a law firm that represents the market.

Crypto.com: The night’s co-presenter (and funder of a $1 million crypto bonus pool) is a Trump business partner, building prediction markets into Truth Social and backing a planned Truth Social exchange-traded fund. Its parent has given $35 million to Trump’s super PAC this election cycle.

Abdul El-Sayed, the progressive candidate for the Democratic nomination for U.S. Senate in Michigan, is out with a new campaign ad.

Titled “Chorus” and made by Fight Agency – started by veterans of campaigns for Senators Bernie Sanders, Ruben Gallego, and John Fetterman – the ad is backed by an initial six-figure spend. Polling shows a tight race between El-Sayed and AIPAC-backed Rep. Haley Stevens, with state Senator Mallory McMorrow also in contention.

  • Political probe:Gavin Newsomsaidhe and his wife were being investigated by Donald Trump’s DOJ, because the California governor is “considering running for president.” Citing prominent figures targeted by the administration, including James Comey and Letitia James, Newsom said, “Today, I proudly join that list.”
  • By the book:New York Timesreporters Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swanrevealedmore behind-the-scenes White House conversations in a new excerpt of the forthcoming bookRegime Change, this time on how Trump aides debated suspending legal rights in response to anti-ICE protests in Minnesota this year.
  • Rally plan:Trumpsaidhe would turn a July 4 250th anniversary event on the National Mall into “the most spectacular Trump rally of them all.”
  • Senate news:Republicans havealreadybooked about $100 million in ads against Graham Platner, the Democratic U.S. candidate in Maine. In Alaska, in a win for incumbent Republican Dan Sullivan, a candidate with the same name wasruledineligible.In Montana, Democrat Alani Bankheaddeclinedcalls to drop out and back independent Seth Bodnar against Republican Steve Daines.
  • Media matters:TheWall Street Journalsaidthe Trump DOJ closed a probe of Paramount’s bid for Warner Bros. Discovery, to head off opposition to the deal. Foxagreedto buy streamer Roku in a $22 billion cash-and-stocks deal.
  • GOP disgrace:A Muslim Republican was told to “convert or leave” the Texas GOP convention in Houston, theTexas Tribunereported. Other Muslims also reported hostility.
  • Pet horror:An LA familymournedJameson, a 2-year-old, Knicks-jersey-wearing dog, shot dead by cops amid celebrations of New York’s NBA title win.

UFC fighter Josh Hokit’s transphobic comment about Michelle Obama on Sunday night was not his first such disgrace. Which sports star did he target after a fight in January?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israel killed four Palestinians on Monday, officialssaid. Two victims, a medic and his son, were killed in a rooftop strike. The death toll from Israeli strikes since a so-called “ceasefire” last October is now nearly 1,000.
  • 🇺🇦 🇷🇺 Russia strikes:Drones and missiles hit Kyiv and Kharkiv, killing 11 and setting a cathedral alight. With G7 leaders set to discuss the war, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskycalledfor a “decisive and substantive” response.

  • 🇨🇴

    Polls pause:ELN, the largest remaining rebel group in Colombia,announceda unilateral ceasefire ahead of next weekend’s presidential election.

  • 🇸🇩

    Drones toll:More than 1,000 have been killed by drones in Sudan this year, the UNsaid, adding that sexual violence was “rampant” in the war-torn country.

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:DR Congoreporteda jump in new cases, with 72 reported in 24 hours, 29 fatal, bringing the caseload to 782 and the death toll to 181. A Doctors Without Borders official said the outbreak was “outpacing the response effort.”


Florida GOP gubernatorial candidate Byron Donalds spoke about the importance of childhood literacy from a podium emblazoned with… a spelling error. In the words of one observer, that’s some “Veep level shit.”

Zeteo’s Andrew McDonald contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: Trump Can’t Buy Joy
item.description: On this day in 1858, at the Illinois Republican Convention in Springfield, U.S. Senate candidate Abraham Lincoln warned that amid national divides over slavery, “A house divided against itself cannot stand.” Within three years, Lincoln would be president, with civil war raging. Good morning! Prem…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1858, at the Illinois Republican Convention in Springfield, U.S. Senate candidate Abraham Lincoln warned that amid national divides over slavery, “A house divided against itself cannot stand.” Within three years, Lincoln would be president, with civil war raging.

Good morning! Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Tuesday wherever you are. I’m just returning from London, where I met Paddington, cursed the monarchy, and ate scones. One observation: It is crazy how captured US society is by crypto and AI. On the “tube,” I saw maybe one ad every now and again about our dear favorite industries. But in the New York subways, you simply cannot escape the next “.ai” or startup shoving itself in your face. I’m sure they’re on their way everywhere, but it was jarring to see the contrast even between two otherwise relatively similar cities…

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ the joy of sports in New York outshines Trump’s pathetic UFC 80th birthday extravaganza (which also served as one huge advertisement for trash he and his family are invested in). Plus: Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed is out with a new ad featuring one Bernie Sanders.

Could there have been a better illustration of the competing visions of society than Donald Trump’s pathetic, sad UFC extravaganza happening alongside the effervescence in New York, as thousands from around the world enjoy the World Cup, and rejoice in the Knicks’ victory?

Let me clear my throat first: There’s no need to strain ourselves to project political commentary onto sports, or to conflate only certain evils with certain sports. The NBA isn’t inherently a holier institution than the UFC, not when you recall that Knicks’ owner Jim Dolan warmly welcomed Trump to a game last week, or that Dolan has also allegedly used facial recognition technology to surveil a trans woman, lawyers, protesters, and more.

What’s of interest here to me is how the public engaged with sport, and what sport did or did not afford to society. So let’s take a look.

In one corner, we have Trump’s UFC event. A spectacle that took nearly a month, hundreds of workers, and $60 million to set the stage for. The affair was paid for, in part, by a glitzy array of sponsors that Trump and his family themselves are invested in: the Trump sons’ crypto exchange; Trump’s sad, failing social media platform; and, of course, his gaudy “Trump Coins.” (More on all this corruption later.)

In attendance were an array of top Trump officials like Pete Hegseth, Marco Rubio, Kash Patel, and JD Vance; billionaire CEOs like Meta’s Mark Zuckerberg and Paramount’s David Ellison; sports team owners including the New England Patriots’ Robert Kraft and the New York Knicks’ James Dolan; and hundreds of military service members who were “recruited” to help fill the stands.

The function was topped off by a military flyover (and what appeared to be at least one brawl between fans).

The entire thing, notably, did not seem to register beyond traditional UFC media, or obsequious right-wing media. It didn’t seem to resonate with the public beyond, which may well be a good thing for Republicans: The event was every bit as shameful and corrupt as it was an insult to American taxpayers and institutions. Perhaps the only moment that broke through from the whole affair was when fighter Josh Hokit exclaimed in the ring, to a microphone held by podcast host Joe Rogan: “Michelle Obama is a man! Am I right, America?!”

It was ugly enough that UFC honcho and Trump ally Dana White felt obligated to reject Hokit’s commentary.

Meanwhile, in another corner, we have the fervor coursing through New York City, amid the New York Knicks’ victorious run in the NBA playoffs – after more than 50 years – and the World Cup, which is being hosted in several cities across North America, including the New York and New Jersey area.

In contrast to Trump’s garish event, the revelry in New York had been open to the public: livestreams of the NBA playoffs were projected everywhere, including at city-run screens all over the city (a practice that will now continue for the World Cup).

People of every creed got in on the excitement. Basketball became the vessel for community, simply, for fun. The atmosphere wasn’t just reserved for billionaires and politicians and sponsors.

The phrase “My mayor’s Muslim, my bagel’s Jewish, my Christian Dior, Knicks in four!” (popularized first in a user’s comment on TikTok before being exploited, unfortunately, by prediction market Kalshi) went massively viral – emblematic of the confluence of optimism coursing through the city’s streets. History was being made, and everyone, everyone could be part of it – even in a city for which such a premise was hotly contested during the mayoral race just a year earlier.

And that spirit, naturally, ties beautifully into the World Cup. A global event that the United States is fortunate to host, alongside Canada and Mexico. While the Trump administration’s brutal immigration “enforcement” apparatus has put a damper on the World Cup, New York in particular serves as such a direct rebuke of Trump and his ilk’s aims. After all, whether Trump keeps thousands of fans out or not, New York itself teems with residents who hail from the nations descending upon the US.

And New York, under their Muslim Mayor Zohran Mamdani, has embraced it – low-cost tickets for the matches; soccer events across the city; extended soccer field lighting for residents; and encouraging millions of residents and visitors to travel across the city to explore the exceptionally wide range of immigrant communities and restaurants.

This week, sports in the U.S. presented two competing worlds. In one corner: We see sports as exclusive, gaudy, jingoistic thumping – not even for America, per se, but for one man who represents the worst of it. In the other, we see sports as a vehicle for a more international patriotism – for optimism, and interest in one another, and in fun being for all, and not for the few.

If nothing else, where would you rather be? The 80th birthday party for the guy who was best friends with the world’s most infamous pedophile? Or the streets of New York City, dancing and singing and chanting and having hope and meeting a new friend who’s at once from oceans away, but who, just like you, was in the city too?

  • Signs and wonders:Trump’s deal with Iran wassignedelectronically – on the U.S. side by an administration that hasclaimednon-personal signing is invalid – before a formal event in Switzerland on Friday.
  • Term limits:In France, for theG7, Trumptalked upthe deal. Terms were not disclosed, butReutersreporteda U.S. official saying they included immediate opening of the Strait of Hormuz, ending the U.S. blockade, and economic relief for Iran.Tehransaidit would charge “fees” in the Strait, not “tolls.”
  • Windfall waffle:On CBS, JD Vance wasasked: “The Iranians are saying they’re gonna have access to a $300 billion reconstruction fund. True or false?” The VP’s answer contained many words, all evasive and boilable down to… “True.” Trump on Tuesdaydeniedthe claim, calling it “ridiculous” and a “rumor.”
  • Secret cash:In Israel, it wasreportedthat before the deal was reached, the Trump administration covertly approved Iran receiving billions of dollars in return for Qatari vessels being allowed through the Strait. Trump has made payments to Iran central to his case against Barack Obama’s Iran nuclear deal from 2015.
  • Gas plunge:Oil prices fell, but after three-and-a-half months of war, the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve wasreportedto be at its lowest level since 1983.
  • Lebanon latest:Though the U.S.-Iran deal said conflict in Lebanon would cease, Israel wasn’t a party to it. item.title: 9 Meltdowns From Top Israeli Figures Over Trump’s Iran Deal
item.description: Top Israeli officials and media figures are going ballistic over the U.S. and Iran agreement to end Donald Trump and Benjamin Netanyahu’s disastrous war. Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif said the deal will cover Lebanon, while Israeli officials insist they will continue their violence there.…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Top Israeli officials and media figures are going ballistic over the U.S. and Iran agreement to end Donald Trump and Benjamin Netanyahu’s disastrous war.

Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif said the deal will cover Lebanon, while Israeli officials insist they will continue their violence there. A U.S. official reportedly said Israel’s withdrawal from Lebanon is not a condition of the agreement, but that the deal is a “ceasefire” that all sides, like Israel, should abide by.

While the deal – set to hopefully be signed on Friday – is technically another temporary ceasefire to allow for further negotiations, prominent Israeli figures, including those closest to Prime Minister Netanyahu, are incensed.

Here are nine fuming, red-in-the-face reactions from top Israeli figures, which have ranged from attacking the U.S.-Iran deal, to defending or castigating Netanyahu over it, to outright insulting Trump, to claiming they don’t have to abide by it:

Israel’s controversial (and yet immovable) National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir said clearly: “Trump’s agreement does not bind us. Israel is not subject to the United States, and we are an independent and sovereign nation!”

Recognizing how the U.S. subsidizes and provides cover for all of Israel’s illegality (including the settlement activity Ben-Gvir so zealously supports), the minister did also add “We emphasize: We love the USA and are grateful to President Trump.” And yet Ben-Gvir said Israel is not party to the agreement and would continue its attacks and violence in Lebanon, contrary to Iran’s insistence for a withdrawal from it.

Defense Minister Israel Katz chimed in as well, saying Trump was acting against U.S. interests, and pledging that Israel would not withdraw from Lebanon, Syria, or Gaza.

The Finance Minister and illegal settler Bezalel Smotrich joined the chorus, saying the deal “is bad for Israel and for the entire free world,” and suggesting Israel would find ways to continue its violent campaign despite any deal aimed at stopping it.

Yair Lapid, leader of the opposition against Netanyahu, blamed the deal on the Israeli prime minister. He said Netanyahu didn’t go far enough – failing to maintain Americans’ trust, and failing to either “rally global public opinion for a renewed war” or to bring the Gulf states into “joint combat.”

Golan, party leader of the The Democrats, slammed the deal for erasing Israel’s “immense military achievements” while “Netanyahu stood on the sidelines – weak, ill, isolated, and powerless.” The Israeli party leader said “Netanyahu is good for” Hamas, Iran, and Hezbollah, and not good for Israel, adding that replacing him “is not just a political necessity – it is an existential security imperative.”

Golan also accused Trump of signing a deal “that funnels billions to the Ayatollahs’ regime, leaves the nuclear infrastructure intact, preserves the ballistic threat as is, and throws a lifeline to the murderous regime in Tehran.”

Amit Segal, a prominent political commentator and the son of a convicted right-wing terrorist, kept it brief, quoting the suitable authority of Henry Kissinger:

“It may be dangerous to be America’s enemy, but to be America’s friend is fatal.”

More than $300 billion and an uncounted number of instances defending Israel from meaningful international confrontation. But apparently all that meant nothing, and is in fact “fatal,” given a single deal ostensibly aimed at ending a war!

Yinon Magal, eminent personality on the genocidal Israeli network Channel 14, blasted the entire Trump team. He called Trump a “loser”; described negotiators Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner (Trump’s son-in-law) as “two little Jews” who were “bought” by Qatar; and slammed Vice President JD Vance as a “lowlife.”

But, he ended with an optimistic tone.

“Bibi,

Go to sleep!

Tomorrow is a new day!

We’re behind you🙌”

Fellow Channel 14 personality Shimon Riklin held nothing back. He said Trump “is in total collapse,” called the U.S. “treacherous,” and added that “Trump is making America weaker than ever.”

Hanoch Milwidsky, chair of the Knesset finance committee, expressed firm support for Netanyahu, and said “Israel does not take instructions from external entities, no matter how important and friendly they may be, regarding the security of its citizens.”

“For some time now, we have not needed ‘recognition’ of our right to defend ourselves,” he said. “Thank you for your attention to this matter,” he added, invoking Trump’s own oft-used phrase.

Thank you for your continued support of Zeteo. If you’d like to help us further, please consider making a donation to Zeteo. Your help enables us to keep building the new media ecosystem the times demand.

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: A Dog Named Lucy Beaten. Sheep Burned Alive. Israeli Settlers Are Attacking Palestinians' Animals to Drive Them Of…
item.description: RAMALLAH, occupied West Bank – The morning Khalil Abu Ghanam ran toward his pen in al-Samu, south of Hebron, he already knew what had happened from the color of the smoke – Israeli settlers had set a fire to the enclosure. By the time he arrived, 75 goats and sheep were dead, charred inside the…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
RAMALLAH, occupied West Bank – The morning Khalil Abu Ghanam ran toward his pen in al-Samu, south of Hebron, he already knew what had happened from the color of the smoke – Israeli settlers had set a fire to the enclosure. By the time he arrived, 75 goats and sheep were dead, charred inside the structure he had built as he expanded his herd, animal by animal, over the years. Security camera footage captured the attack, residents say.

Abu Ghanam did not just lose livestock. He lost the income that kept his family on their land. Today, he lives a hollowed-out version of his previous life, with nothing left to do but to start over – and slowly build his herd again.

It’s a position more and more Palestinians are finding themselves in. Across the West Bank, attacks on Palestinians’ animals – sheep, goats, horses, guard dogs – have become a documented feature of Israeli settler violence. Two videos last month – one of a settler beating a dog named Lucy and another of a settler running over sheep with his ATV and beating a shepherd – went viral. But such cases often receive less international attention than attacks on people. Still, they serve the same purpose: making Palestinian life on Palestinian land unsustainable.

The timing of many attacks is not accidental.

In Masafer Yatta, south of Hebron, Mohammad Abu Ali walked out to his pen on the morning of Eid al-Adha to find the locks cut and between 45 and 50 sheep gone, along with the guard dogs that protected them. Eid al-Adha, the Festival of Sacrifice, is the single most important occasion in the year for families who raise livestock. The animals stolen were the ones his family had raised to sell for the holiday.

He sat outside the empty pen. The loss was not only financial, though years of work had disappeared in a single night. For families in these communities, the ability to perform the Eid sacrifice is bound up with dignity, continuity, and the ability to live as a family on ancestral land.

Al-Baydar, an organization that monitors settler attacks on Bedouin and rural Palestinian communities, documented the theft of more than 12,000 heads of livestock across the West Bank in 2025, and approximately 1,500 more in the first months of 2026.

At an estimated market value of $400-$850 per animal, the losses from 2025 alone amount to $4-10 million. For many of the affected families, the animals were their only source of income.

In mid-May, two incidents were filmed and shared online within 48 hours of each other. Together, they reached audiences far beyond the occupied West Bank.

On May 15, a masked settler entered the village of Atara, north of Ramallah, where an illegal outpost had been established on the village’s land the previous year. In the yard of a Palestinian resident, he found Lucy, a guard dog tied to a post, and beat her repeatedly with a club. The owner, who requested anonymity for fear of reprisal, told Haaretz: “She wasn’t loose, she didn’t attack him or bite him. He attacked a tied-up dog.” Lucy survived and is being treated by Palestinian veterinarians. Israeli police opened an investigation, and a settler was subsequently arrested, though no indictment has been confirmed. The arrest itself was unusual. Charges for settler violence against Palestinians are very rare.

Two days later, on May 17, a settler driving an ATV with a bull skull mounted on its front ran his vehicle into a flock of sheep in Khirbet al-Tawil, east of Aqraba near Nablus, trapping two animals beneath the wheels. He then stepped off the vehicle and beat the Palestinian shepherd who had been filming. The footage was published by Al Jazeera. Police say they are investigating. No arrests have been reported.

Residents of Khirbet al-Tawil said the incident was not a surprise. The area has long been under pressure from surrounding settler-pastoral outposts. Salah Bani Jaber, mayor of Aqraba, said livestock are being targeted directly to force residents to leave. “When you destroy a source of income,” he told me, “departure becomes a logical outcome, not a free choice.”

Human rights organizations, including Al-Haq, Yesh Din, B’Tselem, and the UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, have documented livestock theft and attacks on shepherds as part of a broader strategy to pressure Palestinian communities off their land.

Amir Daoud, documentation director at the Anti-Wall and Settlement Commission, told me that “pastoral Palestinian communities have become a primary target because they represent guardians of Palestinian land. Targeting their animals and their shepherds is part of an effort to erode the capacity to remain.”

In Sinjil, north of Ramallah, settlers attempted to drive off a flock at the edge of town. Residents mobilized immediately. Dozens of people moved toward the surrounding hills where settlers were trying to move the animals away. Mayor Moataz Tawafsheh said the community understood the theft was not only the owner’s problem. “The attackers are not stealing animals,” he told me. “They are targeting the sources of livelihood that people depend on to stay on their land.” Some of the sheep were recovered.

In Ein Yabrud, northeast of Ramallah, Mohammad Hajeer lost three horses to theft over several months. When he tried to recover one of them, approximately 14 settlers beat him while taking the animal. He could not get help; the village entrances were blocked. He said he has no intention of leaving. “Staying has become another form of resistance,” he said.

According to the UN humanitarian office, OCHA, more than 760 settler attacks were documented in the West Bank in 2026 as of early May, an average of six per day, killing at least 13 Palestinians and displacing nearly 2,000 people, including 900 children.

The videos of Lucy and the sheep in Khirbet al-Tawil are unusual not because the violence they show is new, but because they produced visible documentation that traveled internationally. Israeli police opened investigations into both. The gap between documentation and accountability is what human rights organizations say enables the pattern to continue.

Yesh Din’s data covering 2005 to 2025 shows that almost 94% of investigation files related to settler violence against Palestinians were closed without conviction or effective legal accountability. The rate of indictments remains negligible relative to the number of complaints filed.

Last month, Israeli Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich watched as 3,000 Palestinian-planted trees were uprooted to expand settlements, and said: “We are building the Land of Israel and destroying the idea of a Palestinian state.”

For the families in al-Samu, Masafer Yatta, Atara, Khirbet al-Tawil, Sinjil, and Ein Yabrud, that statement describes what they have been forced to witness and contend with every day.

Aseel Mafarjeh is a West Bank-focused journalist based in Ramallah, focusing on local stories that speak of the challenges and creativity of youth in Palestine.

This story was reported in collaboration with Egab.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Iran War Deal Declared – But Will It Last?
item.description: On this day in 1974, Washington Post reporters Bob Woodward and Carl Bernstein published ‘All the President’s Men,’ the definitive book on the Watergate scandal. Two months later, Richard Nixon became the first and so far only president to resign. Good morning! Martin here, writing from the fetid…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1974, Washington Post reporters Bob Woodward and Carl Bernstein published ‘All the President’s Men,’ the definitive book on the Watergate scandal. Two months later, Richard Nixon became the first and so far only president to resign.

Good morning! Martin here, writing from the fetid swamp that is Washington, DC, in mid-June, a few miles up the hill from the worst of it and safely out of earshot, if not alas out of livestream reach, of events to mark the president’s 80th birthday at the White House. I hope you all read this email in more pleasant, air-conditioned climes.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we digest the flurry of news and statements and news about statements (and, go on then, statements about news) that happened Sunday, when a deal to end Donald Trump’s illegal war on Iran was announced to the world. At Zeteo, we were among the more skeptical observers. We also take a look at what happened when the Make-a-Wish president got to stage his big boy’s birthday party on the White House lawn, and consider what fresh hell might be coming down the tracks for Trump, thanks to authors Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan.

Early last week, CNN published a piece about Donald Trump’s incessant promises that an end to his illegal war on Iran was just around the corner.

“Including the period before the ceasefire,” Aaron Blake wrote, “he’s done it at least 38 times. That’s the number of times he’s said directly – in social media posts, public appearances and phone calls with the media – that a deal was nigh or claimed Iran was desperate to cut one.”

No deal had emerged, of course, and the war had devolved back into strike and counter-strike – not including Israel’s ongoing campaign of slaughter in Lebanon, which never truly paused.

And so, not least because Iran said an end to Lebanon strikes was a prerequisite for peace, when Trump spent the next few days claiming a deal was close, skepticism was common in response. According to Trump, a deal was likely over the weekend, perhaps by Sunday, as chance would have it, his 80th birthday. But Sunday did not dawn well, with news of an Israeli strike on Beirut that killed three and injured 16. Here, it seemed, we were about to go again: Trump’s inability to control Benjamin Netanyahu’s rabidly genocidal regime leading the U.S. back to war, and the world to ever-worsening economic problems, the Strait of Hormuz shut tight.

And then, the announcement came.

Pakistan PM Shehbaz Sharif, a key player in negotiations along with Qatar, said the U.S. and Iran had reached a 60-day ceasefire deal, including “the immediate and permanent termination of military operations on all fronts, including in Lebanon.”

Sharif also said the deal would be signed in Switzerland on Friday, June 19, prior to further talks. That gave reason for pause. Five days is plenty of time for Netanyahu to make mischief, and to kill many more Lebanese citizens. But then came Trump, on Truth Social, claiming to be “fully authoriz[ing] the toll free opening of the Strait of Hormuz and… the immediate removal of the U.S. naval blockade” of Iran.

“Let the oil flow!” the president declared, absurdly.

Then came Iranian reactions:

  • “Deputy FM Gharibabaditells Iranian mediathat [Parliamentary Speaker Mohammed Bagher] Qalibaf instructed a cut to negotiations after [the] Israeli attack, and that ultimately the 30-day period for lifting of the blockade was expedited as a US concession.”
  • “Netanyahu’s stupiditybroke the deadlock at the negotiating table. Contrary to some speculation, there was no final text until his criminal attack forced Trump to accept Iran’s demands, especially on Lebanon.”

Iranian state TV put it baldly, broadcasting its message loud and clear: “The Islamic Republic of Iran has officially forced the American-Zionist enemy to end the war on all fronts.”

Has it? If the war has finished, it seems inarguable that Trump has lost it. There is no regime change in Iran. The terms of the deal, as briefed to reporters, are remarkably favorable to Tehran. Iran will commit not to pursue a nuclear weapon, but it is set to see assets unfrozen and reconstruction aided. What is there that Barack Obama did not get in 2015? Not much, while thanks to Trump, 13 Americans are dead, others wounded, bases damaged and aircraft lost, munitions stockpiles much depleted, the economy roundly battered. The Strait will reopen, but the knock-on shocks from its closure will affect gas prices for months to come. The midterms loom in November.

On Sunday, Trump repeated to the New York Times that the Strait would be

Trump also offered more words likely to offend Netanyahu: “He’s a very difficult guy, and to be honest with you, he should be very thankful to us for doing this. Because if Iran had a nuclear weapon, Israel wouldn’t be around for two hours.”

A reminder: Obama’s deal, the JCPOA from 2015, said, “Iran reaffirms that under no circumstances will Iran ever seek, develop or acquire any nuclear weapons.”

If Netanyahu thinks all Trump has achieved is a return to that pre-war status quo, with Hezbollah undefeated, why wouldn’t he keep on fighting? To repeat: Five days until a promised deal signing in Switzerland, after Trump has attended the G7 summit in France, is plenty of time for Israel to intervene. The Israelis were not directly party to the talks that produced the U.S-Iran deal. In Tel Aviv and Jerusalem, discontent is widely reported.

Today, Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz said the Israeli military will not withdraw from the parts of Lebanon, Syria, and Gaza that it continues to occupy.

Ultimately, as those who announced the deal said, it’s only a ceasefire, good for 60 days while yet more talks take place. It’s not a nuclear agreement or a peace treaty. And new hardliners have emerged in Tehran, determined to make the U.S. suffer in response to this war of aggression.

In other words, Trump made this mess; it’s still all over the floor, and he’s by no means free of it yet.

  • Trump blast:The U.S. presidentsaidthe Beirut attack on Sunday “should not have happened.” He also said Israel should not strike anywhere else in Lebanon and attacked Benjamin Netanyahu,tellingvariousoutletsthe Israeli PM has “no fucking judgment” and saying he had told him so himself.
  • MOU praised:Countries across the Middle East and worldwidepraisedthe memorandum of understanding between the U.S. and Iran.
  • …but not in Israel:As mentioned above, news of the agreement did not receive a warm reception, with Israel’s ultranationalist Ministers Bezalel Smotrich and Ben-Gvir rejecting it. Smotrichcalledthe MOU “bad for Israel” and insisted Israel would continue to fight Iran in “creative ways,” while Ben-Gvirclaimedit did not “bind” the country.
  • Lasting effects:Oil pricesfellon news of the deal, but energy expertstold the Associated Pressit could take months for oil and gas supplies to return to normal around the world – if the U.S.-Iran agreement holds.

For weeks, elite Republicans talked about the UFC at the White House as “the hottest ticket in town.” But by Sunday, it seemed clear that even in such rabid circles, heat, humidity, stormy forecasts, and expected mosquito swarms had dampened some such enthusiasm... and on Trump’s 80th birthday, too!

Three sources invited to the White House event who had planned to attend told Zeteo they would not do so. “I don’t want to be eaten alive by mosquitoes,” one Republican told Swin. “I can watch it on TV.” Another said they planned to attend a watch party at Ned’s Club instead.

The White House was getting on with the important stuff, namely attacking The Weather Channel for reporting the weather, in this case, that it was threatening storms: “This event is about celebrating America’s unmatched greatness after 250 years – which apparently doesn’t sit well with the friendless loser who wrote this bullshit clickbait headline.”

Unfortunately, it’s just a fact that watching sports in the DC summer can range from distinctly unpleasant to outright dangerous. One lightning strike and it’s indoors for everyone until the threat has passed. Forecasted storms meant the first fight was delayed to 9pm. Thunder was first heard shortly before that and repeated for a while, like Trump after a McDonald’s dinner. When the action started, some covered the fights as sports. To others, it was just tawdry.

Before the event, UFC announced some fighters would receive bonuses in the form of crypto “stablecoins” offered by the Trump family crypto business, World Liberty Financial. The Paramount+ broadcast saw an announcer shouting out the firm: “Tonight’s $250,000 performance bonus in USD1 is presented by World Liberty Financial. USD1 is a digital dollar – send money faster and cheaper, available on leading exchanges.” Reuters showed what happens to the suckers, sorry, customers, when the Trumps offer crypto.

Fighters walked a tunnel of saluting service members, to enter a ring advertising Crypto.com. Really. Disgustingly, one fighter used a post-fight interview to shout, to Joe Rogan, “Michelle Obama is a man.” Really. It dragged on into the small hours, Trump, a president who sleeps in the daytime, watching it all with delight.

It pays to remember that Trump’s 79th birthday wasn’t great either, also involving the cheapening abuse of the armed forces he commands. An anemic military parade limped down Constitution Avenue, underwhelming all. A year on, there were other embarrassments. Down the Mall from the White House, Trump’s expensively refurbished reflecting pool was full of algae. At the Kennedy Center, Trump’s name had come down.

Nonetheless, the indefatigable in pursuit of the awful, Trump staged his White House fights. One consolation: the UFC card polled even worse than he does. And, at length, it was over. On to the next national humiliation we trudge.

According to news site-slash-Trump-mouthpiece Axios, “top White House officials” are scared of Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan.

The officials, Axios said, believe the New York Times reporters “obtained audio recordings of Situation Room meetings for their forthcoming book, Regime Change,” which if true “would be a shocking breach of one of the most secure settings on earth” – although, it might’ve added, not that shocking, given reality TV villain turned Trump aide Omarosa Manigault made such recordings for her own tell-all in 2018.

One source told Axios: “We’re afraid some of our most sensitive conversations were being recorded. And we have no idea which ones.”

The Times has published two Regime Change excerpts, including an account of discussions about Jeffrey Epstein and the failed attempt to cover up files about the late financier’s sexual crimes and links to powerful figures, Trump included. Axios said it was “telling” that Trumpers have not contested either account, and reported Trump being “furious.”

As mentioned atop this newsletter, today is the 52nd anniversary of All the President’s Men, Woodward and Carl Bernstein’s Watergate book that preceded Richard Nixon’s downfall. Regime Change is out next Tuesday, June 23. As politics hacks await embargoed copies (and others scramble to obtain one naughtily), expect more drama to come.

  • Old Times:On Trump’s 80th birthday, theTimestook alookat his struggles with age. Nameless officials insisted Trump was fine and not sleeping in public, really, though he did recently forget someone’s name. The Pentagon Papers, it was not.
  • Don the con:The Trump family has generated at least $2.3 billion in profit from crypto ventures while investors have lost the same amount, Reuterssaid.
  • McConnell hospitalized:Former Republican Senate leader Mitch McConnell, 84, wasadmittedto the hospital. His office did not say why.
  • Georgia on his mind:Trumpendorsedfar-right U.S. Rep. Mike Collins over Derek Dooley, the mainstream pick in the Senate primary to face Democrat Jon Ossoff.
  • Sweet home:The Alabama GOPsaidTrump-adoring Senator Tommy Tuberville could stay on the ballot for governor, amid doubts that he lives in the state.
  • Florida fury:Extremist GOP gubernatorial candidate James FishbackcalledTrump ally Byron Donalds a “slave.” Fishback is white, Donalds is Black.
  • PAC mentality:A record number of super PACs are exploiting a gap in campaign finance law to spend big on primaries without revealing their donors before the elections take place,Politicoreported.

In May 2025, what Washington development did Republican Senate hopeful Mike Collins compare to “the Muslim conquest of Jerusalem in the 7th century”?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:The death toll in Gaza since the genocide began passed 73,000, the Palestinian Health Ministrysaid, as Israeli strikes continued in disregard of the supposed ceasefire. A 13-year-old boy was among at least eight people killed over the weekend.
  • 🇺🇦 🇷🇺 Trump talks:Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine and Vladimir Putin of Russiabothspoke to the U.S. president. In the English Channel, British forces detained a tanker the UK said belonged to a Russian “shadow fleet” used to beat sanctions.

  • 🇻🇪

    Gangster diplomacy:The leader of the Tren de Aragua gang waskilledin a U.S. airstrike, with the co-operation of the Venezuelan government.

  • 🇬🇧

    Unlawful lawful:The UK Court of Appealsruledthat the proscription of the Palestinian Action group was lawful, after the government appealed a High Court ruling that had found the designation unlawful.

  • 🇬🇧

    Social media ban:British Prime Minister Keir Starmerannounceda ban on social media sites for children under the age of 16.

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:The number ofreportedcases of the deadly disease in DR Congo passed 700, including 149 deaths, government data showed.


Texas Republicans decided to bring a live elephant into their convention in Houston. Lovers of the classic British children’s TV show ‘Blue Peter’ could have warned them what might happen. And did.

  • Speaking to CBS’s ‘Face the Nation,’

    Secretary of Defense Pete Hegsethrejectedthe idea that the U.S. faces a crisis over munitions supplies depleted by war with Iran, despite having testified it could take “months and years” to replenish. “That is a manufactured story the media wants to peddle,” Hegseth said. “Our stockpiles are great, and they’re only getting stronger.”Democratic Senator Mark Kellysaid: “We’re in a posture where we’ve got to be incredibly careful.”
  • Senator Mark Warner, the top Senate intelligence Democrat, was alsoonCBS. Indicating backing forManhattan U.S. Attorney Jay Claytonas director of national intelligence, Warner asked why that pick wasn’t made beforeTrumpnamed MAGA warriorBill Pultein an acting capacity, stoking congressional rebellion and the lapse of a key surveillance power. “If we can get unanimous consent, we could even get [Clayton] confirmed this week,” Warner said.

  • On NBC’s ‘Meet the Press,’

    House Democratic leader Hakeem Jeffrieswas asked if he would impeachPresidentDonald Trumpif he controlled the chamber next year. Jeffriessaid: “We haven’t ruled anything in or ruled anything out.” Nor was the New Yorker about to stir trouble overGraham Platner, the Democratic Senate candidate in Maine. Jeffries insisted the big news of the week “was the Knicks winning the NBA championship for the first time in 53 years.”
  • Monday, June 15:Trump will arrive in Geneva, Switzerland, before traveling to Evian-les-Bains, France, for the G7 Summit.
  • Tuesday, June 16:Alabama, DC, Georgia, and Oklahoma hold primary elections, with Louisiana following suit on Wednesday.
  • Wednesday, June 17:The Senate is set to hold a confirmation hearing for Jay Clayton, the US attorney for Manhattan, nominated to be director of national intelligence. A quick confirmation will provide for the extension of lapsed foreign surveillance powers, though the House is out.
  • Friday, June 19:The U.S. and Iran are due to sign an agreement ending their three-and-a-half-month war in Geneva, Switzerland.

Zeteo’s Asawin Suebsaeng contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

    Secretary of Defense Pete Hegsethrejectedthe idea that the U.S. faces a crisis over munitions supplies depleted by war with Iran, despite having testified it could take “months and years” to replenish. “That is a manufactured story the media wants to peddle,” Hegseth said. “Our stockpiles are great, and they’re only getting stronger.”Democratic Senator Mark Kellysaid: “We’re in a posture where we’ve got to be incredibly careful.”
  • Senator Mark Warner, the top Senate intelligence Democrat, was alsoonCBS. Indicating backing forManhattan U.S. Attorney Jay Claytonas director of national intelligence, Warner asked why that pick wasn’t made beforeTrumpnamed MAGA warriorBill Pultein an acting capacity, stoking congressional rebellion and the lapse of a key surveillance power. “If we can get unanimous consent, we could even get [Clayton] confirmed this week,” Warner said.

  • On NBC’s ‘Meet the Press,’

    House Democratic leader Hakeem Jeffrieswas asked if he would impeachPresidentDonald Trumpif he controlled the chamber next year. Jeffriessaid: “We haven’t ruled anything in or ruled anything out.” Nor was the New Yorker about to stir trouble overGraham Platner, the Democratic Senate candidate in Maine. Jeffries insisted the big news of the week “was the Knicks winning the NBA championship for the first time in 53 years.”
  • Monday, June 15:Trump will arrive in Geneva, Switzerland, before traveling to Evian-les-Bains, France, for the G7 Summit.
  • Tuesday, June 16:Alabama, DC, Georgia, and Oklahoma hold primary elections, with Louisiana following suit on Wednesday.
  • Wednesday, June 17:The Senate is set to hold a confirmation hearing for Jay Clayton, the US attorney for Manhattan, nominated to be director of national intelligence. A quick confirmation will provide for the extension of lapsed foreign surveillance powers, though the House is out.
  • Friday, June 19:The U.S. and Iran are due to sign an agreement ending their three-and-a-half-month war in Geneva, Switzerland.

Zeteo’s Asawin Suebsaeng contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Trump Implemented Far-Right Republican Policies – And They’re Failing
item.description: As it wreaks enormous damage on America and the world, Donald Trump’s second presidency has in one way performed a service. Again and again, Trump has acted without restraint to indulge his longstanding obsessions and those of the Republican Party that empowers him. His abandon has produced…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
As it wreaks enormous damage on America and the world, Donald Trump’s second presidency has in one way performed a service.

Again and again, Trump has acted without restraint to indulge his longstanding obsessions and those of the Republican Party that empowers him. His abandon has produced real-time tests of familiar arguments they’ve offered for years.

And in one realm after another, they’ve flunked. Let’s call the roll.

For decades, GOP hard-liners have yearned to use American might against Iran and its Islamist theocracy. To their chagrin, presidents of both parties shied away from the risk. When Barack Obama rallied U.S. allies behind a negotiated agreement to limit Iran’s nuclear threat, Republican hawks denounced the deal as a giveaway.

In his first term, Trump scuttled it. In his second, he has gone to war.

Instead of the quick-strike triumph he envisioned, the president has met strategic disaster.

Despite the on-the-ground destruction from a massive U.S. bombing campaign, Iran’s resilient response has strengthened its extremist regime, weakened the global economy, and raised prices for American consumers. Now Trump desperately wants out.

His Iran blunder has simultaneously exposed the fecklessness of the administration’s approach to military affairs more broadly. Fox anchor-turned-Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, like Trump preoccupied with appearances and bygone social hierarchies, has culled Black and women commanders, demanded “warfighters” with better-sculpted bodies, and made “lethality” his pre-eminent value.

Yet Hegseth’s “warrior ethos” has not achieved U.S. objectives in an era that favors technological resourcefulness over brawn. While the Pentagon burns through its arsenal of heavy weaponry, Iran has thwarted Trump with inexpensive drones – one of which may have just downed an American helicopter near the Strait of Hormuz.

Nor is brute force winning Trump’s drug war. Courting working-class communities anguished by toll of narcotics, Trump in 2024 suggested the military could smash international cartels. Back in office, he has authorized deadly strikes against small boats in the Caribbean allegedly carrying cocaine and fentanyl.

The strikes, gleefully promoted by the administration in social media videos, have summarily executed nearly 200 people without trial. But drug war experts recently told the New York Times they haven’t diminished the availability or even raised the price of cocaine on American streets. A Johns Hopkins professor likened the campaign to bombing a few McDonald’s restaurants and claiming to have curbed consumption of junk food.

The GOP’s swelling white nationalist constituency claims that removing dark-skinned immigrants will benefit native-born U.S. citizens. Yet Trump and his ICE shock troops have demonstrated in Minnesota and elsewhere that “mass deportation” requires more government violence and disruption than the American people will tolerate.

So with midterm elections approaching, the administration has somewhat backed off.

For many years, Trump has claimed that tariffs on imports from abroad would revive American industry. As they imposed large tariffs on “Liberation Day” last year, the president and administration lackeys insisted that foreign countries would foot the bill.

But have they? The Yale Budget Lab has calculated that the price of imports rose by 1.5% during 2025 – boosting the inflation that Trump had promised to eliminate. And if you think the administration doesn’t know that they’ve been taxing American consumers and business, consider: The White House recently announced tariff cuts on agricultural equipment to reduce costs for struggling farmers and manufacturers.

And then there’s all that federal spending fraud that right-wingers have insisted could be excised if only budget officials had the guts. Well, Trump handed Elon Musk and his tech-bro guerrillas a DOGE chainsaw and they couldn’t find it. (DOGE succeeded, however, in cutting a program to monitor the cross-border scourge of New World screwworm, which has now been found in animals in Texas and New Mexico.)

Since Ronald Reagan’s era, Republican supply-siders have predicted that tax cuts would pay for themselves. By making America’s economy take off “like a rocket ship,” as Trump likes to say, tax cuts generate new government revenues from increased growth.

It hasn’t happened. After Trump’s first big tax cut, the federal budget deficit grew as a share of the economy from 3.4% in 2017 to 4.6% in 2019, the White House budget office has reported.

His second, in 2025, will expand the red ink by $4.7 trillion through 2035, the Congressional Budget Office forecasts; the budget deficit will expand to 6.7% of the economy.

One more thing. Anyone who remembers the self-styled Moral Majority, and The Book of Virtues by ex-Reagan Cabinet member William Bennett, knows how Republicans have repeatedly claimed the high ground on values. They’ve cast Democrats as the libertine party of “moral relativism” and disdain for America.

Grading such claims has been subjective and difficult – until now. Considering Trump’s undisguised corruption, dishonesty, contempt for the rule of law, and preference for autocracies over our democratic allies, what do you think?

John Harwood is the former chief Washington correspondent for CNBC and White House correspondent for CNN. He has interviewed every president from George H.W. Bush to Joe Biden. Sign up for ‘The Stakes with John Harwood’ to get all of his columns in your inbox.
