item.title: 80 Things to Know About Donald Trump on His 80th Birthday
item.description: To mark Donald Trump’s 80th birthday, here are 80 things you should know about the sitting president of the United States (who will leave office in January 2029 as the oldest president in the history of the country). His father was a real estate mogul Donald Trump’s father, Fred Trump, was a…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
To mark Donald Trump’s 80th birthday, here are 80 things you should know about the sitting president of the United States (who will leave office in January 2029 as the oldest president in the history of the country).

  • His father was a real estate mogul

    • Donald Trump’s father, Fred Trump, was a prominent real estate developer who

      builtmore than 27,000 apartments and row houses across New York City.
  • His mother was an immigrant

    • Mary Anne Trump

      immigratedto the U.S. from Scotland in 1930, having learned English as asecond language.
  • He grew up in a mansion

    • Donald Trump grew up in a 23-room brick

      mansionin the affluent neighborhood of Jamaica Estates, Queens.
  • He became a millionaire at just eight years old

    • According to a

      New York Timesinvestigation, by the age of three, Donald Trump earned today’s equivalent of more than $200,000 a year, making him a millionaire by the age of eight.
  • He was seen as the favorite son

    • Donald Trump was seen as the

      favoriteof the three Trump boys, taking over his eldest brother’s position as the apparent heir to the Trump real estate empire.
  • He does not drink alcohol

    • Speaking of Trump’s eldest brother, Freddy

      developed alcoholismin the 1960s and died of a heart attack at the age of 42. Donald Trumpcites thisas the reason he doesn’t drink.
  • His late sister was a Clinton-appointed judge

    • Maryanne Trump Barry was

      appointedby President Bill Clinton to the U.S. Court of Appeals in 1999, serving in the role for 16 years before retiring amid aninvestigationinto whether she had committed tax fraud. Guess it runs in the family.
  • He was sent to military school

    • Donald Trump was sent to

      the New York Military Academyat the age of 13, where he wasdescribedby many of his classmates as a “bully.” Are we surprised?
  • Yet… he dodged the Vietnam War draft

    • Despite attending military school, Trump did not, in fact, go on to serve in the military during the Vietnam War. He avoided the draft

      five times: the first four were to complete his college degree, and the fifth time, he was diagnosed withbone spursthat would disqualify him from service.
  • He graduated from the University of Pennsylvania’s Wharton School of Business

    • Trump has repeatedly claimed he graduated at the top of his class in 1968, yet, Wharton’s published academic honors and deans lists for that year

      do not mention his name. Awkward.
  • He’s a nepo baby

    • After graduating from the University of Pennsylvania, Trump immediately began working for his father. He became

      presidentof the Trump Organization a whopping three years later.
    Donald Trump and Fred Trump at the Trump Tower Atrium in New York City on Dec. 12, 1987. Photo by Sonia Moskowitz/Getty Images
  • He and his father were sued for discrimination

    • The Justice Department

      suedthe Trumps in 1973 for refusing to rent apartments in predominantly white buildings to Black tenants.
  • But he did not have to admit to using discriminatory practices

    • The case ended in a

      settlementthat did not require the Trumps to explicitly acknowledge that they used discriminatory practices. However, the government’s description of the settlement says they “failed and neglected” to comply with the Fair Housing Act.
  • Roy Cohn was one of his closest friends and mentors

    • Roy Cohn, who once worked for Sen. Joseph McCarthy as a

      “Red Scare” prosecutor, became one of Trump’s closest allies, and the tworeportedlyspoke on the phone up to five times per day.
  • He used Cohn’s mob ties to help build his real estate projects

    • Roy Cohn also just so happened to have

      connections with the mobwhich controlled a large part of New York City’s construction unions. Trump used these mob connections to helpbuildTrump Tower and Trump Plaza.
  • He also relied on undocumented workers

    • To make way for Trump Tower, Trump

      employed200 undocumented Polish workers to demolish the Bonwit Teller building. In true capitalist fashion, he subjected them to 12-hour shifts where they were paid as little as $4 an hour – less than half the union wage.
  • He failed to build a casino empire

    • Trump

      openedthree casinos in Atlantic City, New Jersey – all of which later filed for bankruptcy. You can’t win ‘em all.
  • He bought an airline

    • In 1988, Trump

      launched‘Trump Shuttle,’ a fleet of planes and their accompanying routes and terminalspurchasedfrom the failing Eastern Airlines. It was never profitable, and was ultimately sold to U.S. Airways.
    Donald Trump during Launch of Trump Steaks in New York City. Photo by Stephen Lovekin/WireImage for Hill & Knowlton
  • He started a steak brand
  • And a water brand

    • Dubbed ‘Trump Ice,’ Donald Trump’s

      bottled water brandlaunched in 2004 and was also a failure. The good news? If you have an unopened bottle lying around, you can make apretty penny.
  • … And a Vodka brand
  • Oh, and don’t forget, more recently, the Bibles!
  • He’s filed for Chapter 11 Bankruptcy six times

    • After the three Atlantic City casinos declared bankruptcy, the Plaza Hotel, Trump Hotels and Casinos Resorts, and Trump Entertainment Resorts

      alsoall filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy.
  • While Trump’s Atlantic City stint was short-lived, it landed him in the WWE universe

    • The Trump Plaza in Atlantic City NJ

      hostedWrestleMania two years in a row.
  • He even appeared in WWE storylines

    • In

      2007, WWE Chairman Vince McMahonchallengedTrump to a “Battle of the Billionaires” match at Wrestlemania 23. Trump won, and as his prize, heshavedMcMahon’s head in the ring.
    Vince McMahon has his head shaved by Donald Trump and Bobby Lashley at the 2007 Wrestlemania on April 1, 2007 in Detroit, Michigan. Photo by Bill Pugliano/Getty Images
  • He made a cameo in Home Alone 2

    • The Christmas classic was

      filmedin the Plaza Hotel, which only happened underone condition: that its owner, Donald Trump, would be featured in the movie.
  • He had a brief stint as a Broadway producer
  • He became a reality television star

    • Trump hosted ‘The Apprentice’ in 2004, where he would become famous for telling contestants “You’re Fired!”

  • He also became involved in pageants

    • In 1996, Trump became a

      co-ownerof Miss Universe, which also includes Miss USA and Miss Teen USA pageants. We can all see where this is going…
  • He was a known creep among pageant contestants

    • Surprise, surprise! Donald Trump was often found

      lurkingin the dressing rooms, includingMiss Teen USApageants where some of the girls were completely naked.
  • He’s joked that he’d date his own daughter

    • In 2006, Trump

      saidon ABC’s ‘The View,’ that his then 24-year-old daughter Ivanka “does have a very nice figure.” He continued, “I’ve said if Ivanka weren’t my daughter, perhaps I’d be dating her.”
  • He’s been accused of sexual misconduct at least 28 times

    • Yes,

      28 accusations… that we know of. The list includes allegations of forcible kissing, touching, groping, rape, and more. He’s denied all accusations.
  • He’s even bragged about it!

    • In a resurfaced ‘Access Hollywood’ tape from 2005, Trump was caught

      saying, “I’m automatically attracted to beautiful women. I just start kissing them, it’s like a magnet. Just kiss. I don’t even wait. And when you’re a star, they let you do it. You can do anything – Grab ‘em by the pussy.”
  • He was found liable for sexual abuse in a civil trial

    • A jury found Trump

      liablefor sexually abusing writer E. Jean Carroll in 1996, after she alleged that he raped her in the dressing room of a department store.
  • He is the first U.S. president to stand a criminal trial

    • Donald Trump became the first U.S. president to stand

      criminal trialwhen he was charged with 34 counts of falsifying business records in New York.
  • He was found guilty of paying ‘hush money’ to a sex worker

    • In that very same criminal trial, Donald Trump was

      found guiltyof paying adult film star Stormy Daniels $130,000 tokeep quietabout their affair during the 2016 presidential election.
  • He was friends with Jeffrey Epstein

    • Trump and Epstein’s friendship dates back to the late 1980s, where the two would often

      mingleat Trump’s Mar-a-Lago Estate. And according to a birthday card obtained by theHouse Oversight Committee, Trump alluded to a “wonderful secret” they both shared…Photo released by the House Oversight Committee.
  • They bonded over creeping on women

    • In an interview with

      New York Magazine, Trumpsaidthat Epstein, “likes beautiful women as much as I do, and many of them are on the younger side.” Gross.
  • And Trump allegedly groped Epstein’s girlfriend while he watched

    • Former model Stacey Williams, Epstein’s former girlfriend,

      allegedthat Trump groped her while Epstein looked on. Trump denies doing so.
  • He’s even been accused of raping one of his wives

    • Trump’s first wife, Ivana Trump,

      accusedhim of raping her. She later walked it back, saying her words shouldn’t be interpreted in a “literal or criminal sense.”
  • He’s been married three times

    • Trump married

      threedifferent women: model Ivana Trump, actress and singer Marla Maples, and model and First Lady Melania Trump.
  • He has five children from three wives

    • Donald Jr., Ivanka, and Eric with his first wife Ivana; Tiffany with his second wife Marla; and Barron with Melania.

  • And they, too, are nepo babies

    • Ivanka and her husband Jared Kushner were

      senior advisersto Trump during his first term as president. Meanwhile, Eric and Don Jr. stepped in to run the Trump Organization.
  • He wasn’t always a Republican

    • Trump first

      registeredas a Republican in 1969, but later became a member of the Independence Party – an offshoot of the centrist Reform Party – in 1999. Three years later, he would register as a Democrat, before reverting back to Republican in 2009.
  • He asked to be George H.W. Bush’s vice president

    • In 1988, Trump allegedly

      approachedthen RNC chair Lee Atwater to be Bush’s running mate – a request Bush described as “strange and unbelievable.” Trump laterallegedthat Atwater was the one to approachhim.Sure.
  • He ran for president once before the 2016 election

    • In 2000, Trump

      ran for presidentunder the Reform Party but withdrew after failing to secure the nomination.
  • He amplified the Obama ‘birther’ conspiracy

    • As Barack Obama sought reelection in 2011, Donald Trump

      cast doubtonto his citizenship status,allegingthat he was actually born in Kenya, not Hawaii.
    Donald Trump rides an escalator to a press event to announce his candidacy on June 16, 2015 in New York City. Photo by Christopher Gregory/Getty Images
  • He launched his 2016 campaign in the most Trumpian way

    • In June 2015, Donald Trump

      descendeda golden escalator in Trump Tower to officially launch his presidential campaign.
  • He hired paid actors to attend his campaign launch

    • If the golden escalator wasn’t out of touch enough, his campaign literally

      paidactors $50 to “wear t-shirts and carry signs and help cheer him in support of his announcement.”
  • His 2016 run was shadowed by his history of racism

    • While campaigning, Trump faced backlash for his aggressive lobbying for the

      death penaltyagainst the ‘Central Park Five,’ a group of Black and Latino boys who were wrongfully convicted of rape in 1989.
  • His history of fraud also came to light

    • Trump’s opponents, including

      Marco Rubio, slammed him for his role indefrauding studentsat his short-lived real estate school, Trump University. Hesettledthe case for $25 million in 2025.
  • He’s aware of the cult following he’s created

    • During the 2016 campaign trail, Trump

      proclaimed, “I could stand in the middle of Fifth Avenue and shoot somebody, and I wouldn’t lose any voters.”
  • He was the most inexperienced president in U.S. history

    • Trump heavily relied on his cult following to land himself in the White House, becoming the first U.S. president to have

      no priormilitary or political experience.
  • Russia wanted him to win

    • An intelligence community

      investigationrevealed that Russia meddled in the 2016 election and “demonstrated a preference for candidate Trump.”
  • He was the first modern president to not release his tax returns

    • Trump became the first presidential candidate in more than 40 years to not release his

      tax returnsprior to taking office, likely to avoid the fact that he paidvery little.
  • He enforced an insanely cruel ‘child separation’ policy

    • More than 5,000 children were forcibly

      separated(kidnapped?) from their parents at the border during Trump’s first term, many of whom wereheld in cages.
  • He hates the media
  • Except for Fox…

    • Yes, Trump is seemingly

      addictedto watching Fox, a staple channel in his daily dose ofreportedlyat least 4 hours worth of cable news programming.
  • He wrote Kim Jong Un ‘love letters’

    • Trump and the North Korean dictator

      exchangedletters during his first presidency, which were filled with flowery language. Even Trumpdescribedthem as “love letters.”
    Donald Trump removes his mask upon return to the White House from Walter Reed National Military Medical Center on Oct. 5, 2020 in Washington, DC. Photo by Win McNamee/Getty Images
  • He suggested dangerous (non-) cures for Covid infections

    • At the height of the Covid pandemic, Trump

      suggestedinjecting disinfectants like bleach into the body and even exposing infected individuals to UV light.
  • He grossly mishandled the pandemic

    • Trump could have avoided

      hundreds of thousandsof Covid-related deaths in the US if he had implemented earlier mask mandates, social distancing rules, and robust testing guidelines, according tomultiplestudies.
  • He described some white supremacists as “very fine people”

    • After the 2017 neo-Nazi “Unite the Right” rally in Charlottesville, Trump

      describedthe attendees as “very fine people on both sides.” He then clarified that the neo-Nazis and white nationalists should be “condemned totally.”
  • He wanted to ‘shoot’ Black Lives Matter protesters

    • As Black Lives Matter protests broke out in response to George Floyd’s murder, Trump

      reportedlysuggested shooting protesters in the legs to quell unrest.
  • He pressured Ukraine to investigate the Bidens

    • In the lead-up to the 2020 election, Trump reportedly

      urgedUkrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky to launch an investigation into Joe Biden and his son, Hunter. This push came after Trump’s decision to freeze military aid to Ukraine, and was widely regarded as aquid pro quodeal.
  • He tried to overturn the 2020 election

    • As the votes were being counted, Trump falsely claimed the results were rigged against him and,

      among other things,pressuredGeorgia’s secretary of state to reverse the results, citing conspiracies about widespread voter fraud, andaskedhim to “find 11,780 votes” – one more vote than what Biden received.
    Donald Trump poses for his booking photo at the Fulton County Jail on Aug. 24, 2023 in Atlanta, Georgia. Photo by Fulton County Sheriff's Office via Getty Images
  • He became the first president to have to pose for a mug shot

    • Following his attempt to overturn Georgia’s election results, Trump, facing 13 criminal charges,

      posedfor his infamous mugshot.
  • He ‘lit the fire’ of the Jan. 6 insurrection

    • When the election didn’t fall in his favor, a House investigation

      revealedthat Trump encouraged his supporters to take to the Capitol on Jan. 6, telling them to “fight like hell.”
  • And then pardoned the insurrectionists

    • Upon his return to office, Trump

      pardonedthe violent insurrectionists who were indicted or sentenced after the Capitol attack.
  • He was impeached twice

    • Trump is the only U.S. president to be

      impeachedtwice: once for the Ukraine controversy and once for inciting the Jan. 6 insurrection. He wasacquittedby the Senate in both proceedings.
    Stacks of boxes in a bathroom and shower in Donald Trump’s Mar-a-Lago estate in Palm Beach, Florida. Photo by U.S. Department of Justice via Getty Images
  • He stored classified government documents at his home
  • He launched a meme coin days before he took office for the second time

    • The Trump family has

      raked inat least $2.3 billion in profit from their crypto ventures since the beginning of the second administration, according to a Reuters analysis.
  • He’s issued a record number of executive orders

    • In the first 100 days of his presidency, Trump issued

      more executive ordersthan any other U.S. president, covering everything from attacks on DEI programs to establishing DOGE to gut key parts of the government.
  • He’s obsessed with tariffs

    • In the first few months of his second term, Trump unleashed a

      waveof tariffs on nearly every single country – a move that the Supreme Court laterknocked down. But his obsession with tariffs dates back to the 80s, when he called for a 15-20% tax on Japanese imports afterlosing an auctionto a Japanese trading company.
    Donald Trump holds up a chart while speaking during his “Liberation Day” event on April 2, 2025. Photo by Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images
  • He’s pushing a dangerous imperialist agenda

    • Since taking office, Trump has repeatedly threatened to

      annexGreenland and Canada in a bid to revive the era of Americanexpansionism.
  • And he’s already had his hand in regime change

    • Trump

      kidnappedVenezuelan President Nicolás Maduro, took credit forassassinatingIranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, and iseyeinga regime change in Cuba.
  • He started an illegal war with Iran

    • Despite

      vowingto not start any new wars, Trump launched an illegal war in Iran with Israel and hascontinued to lieto the American public about it ending.
  • He’s trying to get rid of birthright citizenship

    • In January 2025, Trump signed an

      executive orderto strip birthright citizenship from babies born in the U.S. to non-citizen parents. If upheld by the Supreme Court, roughly 2.7 million people areexpectedto lose their status over the next 20 years.
  • He’s weaponized ICE as his personal militia

    • Trump has cracked down hard on immigration, deploying masked,

      militarizedICE agents to blue cities like Los Angeles and Minneapolis. These ICE agents have evenkilledU.S. citizens in broad daylight.
  • He’s spent more time golfing than any other president

    • When he’s not starting new wars or terrorizing everyday Americans, Trump is often golfing. In fact, he’s spent over 20% of his second term playing golf,

      costingtaxpayers an estimated $155 million.
  • He’s not ruling out a third term

    • Yes, you read that right. Trump has repeatedly

      suggestedthat he’s weighing an unconstitutional third term, which he would begin at the ripe age of 82.

item.title: This Week in Palestine: Israel Kills 8-Year-Old Schoolboy and 15-Year-Old Fisherman Just Days After Murdering 7-Mo…
item.description: Not long after we wrapped up last week’s edition of ‘This Week in Palestine,’ we started to hear reports that Israeli forces had killed a 7-month-old boy the previous Friday in the occupied West Bank. His name was Sam Fahd Abu Haikal , a baby, shot in the face. Sam’s killing was barely a blimp in…
item.author: This Week in Palestine | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Not long after we wrapped up last week’s edition of ‘This Week in Palestine,’ we started to hear reports that Israeli forces had killed a 7-month-old boy the previous Friday in the occupied West Bank. His name was Sam Fahd Abu Haikal, a baby, shot in the face.

Sam’s killing was barely a blimp in the Western media news cycle when it happened. There was no worldwide outrage. Perhaps that’s because Western media, governments, and politicians like to take Israel at its word: the Israeli military, after all, claimed its soldiers “perceived” the vehicle Sam and his family were traveling in “accelerating toward them.”

What then, when video was later released showing the exact opposite? Shouldn’t there have been worldwide outrage then? As we know all too well, Israel kills children with impunity. Even when there are witnesses, and video, and photos, even when Israel is caught in yet another lie, even when those killed are babies, the world barely bats an eye.

Sam wasn’t the only child killed by Israel over the last week or so. At least three others were killed in Gaza. At the same time, settler violence in the occupied West Bank continued to escalate, and a well-known Gaza doctor held by Israeli without charge for nearly two years appeared via videolink for the first time in over a year.

Needless to say, it was another troubling week in Palestine:

Israeli settlers and at least one soldier rampaged through the occupied West Bank town of Huwara, beating Palestinian residents, smashing car windows, and stealing livestock, according to Palestinian media. At least nine Palestinians were injured in just the latest settler attack in the West Bank.

Israeli forces killed 15-year-old Mohammed Musa Abu Giab after the Israeli Navy opened fire on his and other fishing boats on the coast of Deir al-Balah in central Gaza. At least four other fishermen were reportedly detained.

Al Jazeera, citing Gaza’s Fishermen’s Syndicate, reported that Israel has killed more than 235 Palestinian fishermen in Gaza since the genocide began.

Abu Giab was one of 13 people killed on Sunday, according to news reports. At least one other child was killed in those strikes.

Israel killed at least eight people in Gaza, including 8-year-old Jad Suleiman as he returned home from school in Jabaliya refugee camp in northern Gaza. He was one of seven Palestinians reportedly killed in Israeli strikes on Monday.

The strikes on Sunday and Monday brought the death toll from Israeli attacks since the so-called “ceasefire” took effect to more than 970.

A UN inquiry found what we’ve long known: Israeli authorities have been directly involved in settler attacks that have harmed and displaced Palestinians in the occupied West Bank, and through financial and military support, the state has enabled such violence to rise.

“Violence by settlers is the direct outcome of Israeli policies that support, enable and protect their actions, whereas Hamas-affiliated forces have exploited the vacuum created by relentless Israeli attacks and widespread destruction of Gaza,” said Srinivasan Muralidhar, Chair of the Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory.

Dr. Hussam Abu Safiya, who was abducted from Gaza in December 2024, appeared briefly by video in the Israeli Supreme Court. It was the first time he had been seen publicly in over a year.

He was visibly thinner. His family, lawyers, and former detainees had previously said he had lost more than 88 lbs, been tortured, and suffered health conditions, including scabies.

Haaretz

The new outpost is in the occupied West Bank city of Jenin, where the Israeli military displaced thousands when it launched a military operation there and in Tulkarm and Nur al-Shams in January 2025. Many homes were occupied by Israeli forces and some were later demolished.

The Israeli military said the outpost was supposed to help create conditions that would lead to the eventual withdrawal from the camp, but Haaretz wrote, “sources familiar with developments on the ground told Haaretz that the army outpost is also likely intended to help protect Israeli settlers who are returning to live in the Jenin area.”

A UK judge sentenced four Palestine Action activists to jail terms of roughly five years to eight years, saying their criminal damage conviction over a raid on the Bristol site of the Israeli arms company Elbit Systems in August 2024 had a “terrorist connection.”

Middle East Eye wrote, “This marks the first time in British legal history that the [terrorist] designation has been applied to direct action protesters who were not convicted of terrorist offences or of causing intentional violence.”

Amnesty International warned the decision “risks marking a new low in the ongoing crackdown against protest across the UK.”

Miss last week’s edition? Catch up here. Plus, check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.
